revision.comment: [[WP:AES|←]]Changed redirect target from [[Grand Tutor]] to [[Three Ducal Ministers]]
revision.text: #REDIRECT [[Three Ducal Ministers]]

'''''Taifu''''' (大夫) was a [[Nobility|noble]] title in Japan, denoting a [[List of Japanese court ranks, positions and hereditary titles|court rank]] between [[Senior First Rank|First Rank]] and [[Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade|Fifth Rank]] under the ''[[Ritsuryō]]'' system. It was also commonly used to refer to a holder of Fifth Rank, but also for holders of Fourth and Fifth Rank, to differentiate from holders of First, Second and Third Rank, collectively known as [[Kugyō|''kugyō'']].
== History == In the ancient [[Yamato period]] Japan, the title was used to refer to a close attendant of the [[Emperor of Japan|Emperor]] or [[Okimi]]. Prior to the [[Taika Reform]] in 645, a government official below [[Omi|''Ōomi'']] and [[Muraji|''Ōmuraji'']] of the same name was called ''Maetsugimi'',<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|title=Nihon kokugo daijiten|publisher=Shōgakkan|others=Shōgakkan. Kokugo Jiten Henshūbu, 小学館. 国語辞典編集部.|year=2006|isbn=4-09-521021-4|at=大夫|oclc=70216445}}</ref><ref name=":3">{{Cite book|title=Nihon dai hyakka zensho|publisher=Shōgakkan|year=1989|isbn=4-09-526001-7|at=大夫|oclc=14970117}}</ref> whose duty was to submit matters to the Emperor.<ref name=":3" />
According to the ''kushiki-ryō'' of the ''[[ritsuryō]]'' law, a ''Taifu'' holds a court rank of Third Rank or higher in the [[Daijō-kan|Great Council of State]], a court rank of Fourth Rank in a government institution above a bureau, and a court rank of Fifth Rank or higher as a ''shi'' (official in a government department beneath a bureau) or in a ''chūgoku'' (a province of the second highest rank) or lower.<ref name=":3" />
Although ''Taifu'' is the collective honorary title denoting a court rank between First Rank and Fifth Rank, holders of high-ranking court ranks above Third Rank were referred to as [[Kugyō|''kugyō'']], and thus ''Taifu'' became commonly used for holders of Fourth and Fifth Rank. As ''Taifu'' is an honorary title, the title was in heavy use for the holders of Fifth Rank, which ultimately led to ''Taifu'' colloquially becoming synonymous with Fifth Rank.<ref name=":0" />
Despite Fifth Rank being the lowest rank for [[peerage]], for regional [[samurai]] with no illustrious lineage, conferring a peerage and being awarded the title of ''Taifu'' was glorious. For a similar reason, leading actors, entertainers, priests and prostitutes were dubbed with this title.<ref name=":0" /> For example, in [[kabuki]] theater, the leading female role actor (''tateoyama'') is referred to as ''taifu'', and in ''[[sarugaku]]'' theater, the leader of a troupe is called ''taifu''.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Matsumura|first=Akira|title=Daijisen|publisher=Shōgakkan|others="Daijisen" Henshūbu|year=1995|isbn=4-09-501211-0|at=大夫|language=Japanese|oclc=34170147}}</ref>
In the [[Edo period]], a similar title seen in ancient [[Zhou dynasty|Zhou China]] as an official rank below ''kei'' (卿) and above ''shi'' (士), ''Taifu'', was taken from the Zhou Chinese system, and was used for the [[Hatamoto|''hatamoto'']].<ref name=":0" />
== References ==

'''''Taifu''''' (大夫) was a [[Nobility|noble]] title in Japan, denoting a [[List of Japanese court ranks, positions and hereditary titles|court rank]] between [[Senior First Rank|First Rank]] and [[Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade|Fifth Rank]] under the ''[[Ritsuryō]]'' system. It was also commonly used to refer to a holder of Fifth Rank, but also for holders of Fourth and Fifth Rank, to differentiate from holders of First, Second and Third Rank, collectively known as [[Kugyō|''kugyō'']].
== History == In the ancient [[Yamato period]] Japan, the title was used to refer to a close attendant of the [[Emperor of Japan|Emperor]] or [[Okimi]]. Prior to the [[Taika Reform]] in 645, a government official below [[Omi|''Ōomi'']] and [[Muraji|''Ōmuraji'']] of the same name was called ''Maetsugimi'',<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|title=Nihon kokugo daijiten|publisher=Shōgakkan|others=Shōgakkan. Kokugo Jiten Henshūbu, 小学館. 国語辞典編集部.|year=2006|isbn=4-09-521021-4|at=大夫|oclc=70216445}}</ref><ref name=":3">{{Cite book|title=Nihon dai hyakka zensho|publisher=Shōgakkan|year=1989|isbn=4-09-526001-7|at=大夫|oclc=14970117}}</ref> whose duty was to submit matters to the Emperor.<ref name=":3" />
According to the ''kushiki-ryō'' of the ''[[ritsuryō]]'' law, a ''Taifu'' holds a court rank of Third Rank or higher in the [[Daijō-kan|Great Council of State]], a court rank of Fourth Rank in a government institution above a bureau, and a court rank of Fifth Rank or higher as a ''shi'' (official in a government department beneath a bureau) or in a ''chūgoku'' (a province of the second highest rank) or lower.<ref name=":3" />
Although ''Taifu'' is the collective honorary title denoting a court rank between First Rank and Fifth Rank, holders of high-ranking court ranks above Third Rank were referred to as [[Kugyō|''kugyō'']], and thus ''Taifu'' became commonly used for holders of Fourth and Fifth Rank. As ''Taifu'' is an honorary title, the title was in heavy use for the holders of Fifth Rank, which ultimately led to ''Taifu'' colloquially becoming synonymous with Fifth Rank.<ref name=":0" />
Despite Fifth Rank being the lowest rank for [[peerage]], for regional [[samurai]] with no illustrious lineage, conferring a peerage and being awarded the title of ''Taifu'' was glorious. For a similar reason, leading actors, entertainers, priests and prostitutes were dubbed with this title.<ref name=":0" /> For example, in [[kabuki]] theater, the leading female role actor (''tateoyama'') is referred to as ''taifu'', and in ''[[sarugaku]]'' theater, the leader of a troupe is called ''taifu''.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Matsumura|first=Akira|title=Daijisen|publisher=Shōgakkan|others="Daijisen" Henshūbu|year=1995|isbn=4-09-501211-0|at=大夫|language=Japanese|oclc=34170147}}</ref>

== Pronunciation == Although the kana form of the title is "taifu", in modern Japanese the pronunciation would be "tayū".<ref name=":0" />
The government office of the same name (but pronounced daibu) referred to the Director of the Household Agency of Imperial Wives (chūgūshiki), the Crown Prince's Quarters (tōgūbō), or the Household Agency of Queen-consort (kōgōgūshiki).<ref name=":3" /> This pronunciation was to differentiate from the Vice-Minister of the eight ministries with the same pronunciation but different kanji writing form (大輔).<ref name=":0" />
== References ==

In [[Shinto]], a priest who practices rituals through [[performing arts]] is called ''tayū''. In [[Jōruri (music)|''jōruri'']] narrative music, the singer is called ''tayū''. In the modern times, similar usage of ''tayū'' exists in Japanese [[street performance]], ''kadozuke'' (performance at the gate of a house), and [[Manzai|''manzai'']].<ref>{{Cite book|last=Matsumura|first=Akira|title=Daijisen|publisher=Shōgakkan|others="Daijisen" Henshūbu|year=1995|isbn=4-09-501211-0|at=大夫|language=Japanese|oclc=34170147}}</ref>

[[Category:Japanese historical terms]] [[Category:Titles]] [[Category:Japanese nobility]]

'''''Taifu''''' (大夫) was a [[Nobility|noble]] title in Japan, denoting a [[List of Japanese court ranks, positions and hereditary titles|court rank]] between [[Senior First Rank|First Rank]] and [[Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade|Fifth Rank]] under the ''[[Ritsuryō]]'' system. == History == In the ancient [[Yamato period]] Japan, the title was used to refer to a close attendant of the [[Emperor of Japan|Emperor]] or [[Okimi]]. Prior to the [[Taika Reform]] in 645, a government official below [[Omi|''Ōomi'']] and [[Muraji|''Ōmuraji'']] of the same name was called ''Maetsugimi'',<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|title=Nihon kokugo daijiten|publisher=Shōgakkan|others=Shōgakkan. Kokugo Jiten Henshūbu, 小学館. 国語辞典編集部.|year=2006|isbn=4-09-521021-4|at=大夫|oclc=70216445}}</ref><ref name=":3">{{Cite book|title=Nihon dai hyakka zensho|publisher=Shōgakkan|year=1989|isbn=4-09-526001-7|at=大夫|oclc=14970117}}</ref> whose duty was to submit matters to the Emperor.<ref name=":3" />
According to the ''kushiki-ryō'' of the ''[[ritsuryō]]'' law, a ''Taifu'' holds a court rank of Third Rank or higher in the [[Daijō-kan|Great Council of State]], a court rank of Fourth Rank in a government institution above a bureau, and a court rank of Fifth Rank or higher as a ''shi'' (official in a government department beneath a bureau) or in a ''chūgoku'' (a [[Provinces of Japan|province]] of the second highest rank) or lower.<ref name=":3" />
Although ''Taifu'' is the collective honorary title denoting a court rank between First Rank and Fifth Rank, holders of high-ranking court ranks above Third Rank were referred to as [[Kugyō|''kugyō'']], and thus ''Taifu'' became commonly used for holders of Fourth and Fifth Rank. As ''Taifu'' is an honorary title, the title was in heavy use for the holders of Fifth Rank, which ultimately led to ''Taifu'' colloquially becoming synonymous with Fifth Rank.<ref name=":0" />
Despite Fifth Rank being the lowest rank for [[peerage]], for regional [[samurai]] with no illustrious lineage, conferring a peerage and being awarded the title of ''Taifu'' was glorious.<ref name=":0" />
In the [[Edo period]], a similar title seen in ancient [[Zhou dynasty|Zhou China]] as an official rank below ''kei'' (卿) and above ''shi'' (士), ''Taifu'', was taken from the Zhou Chinese system, and was used for the [[Hatamoto|''hatamoto'']].<ref name=":0" />
== Pronunciation == Although the [[kana]] form of the title is "''taifu''", in modern Japanese the pronunciation would be "''tayū''".<ref name=":0" />
The government office of the same name (but pronounced ''daibu'') referred to the Director of the Household Agency of Imperial Wives (''chūgūshiki''), the Crown Prince's Quarters (''tōgūbō''), or the Household Agency of Queen-consort (''kōgōgūshiki'').<ref name=":3" /> This pronunciation was to differentiate from the Vice-Minister of the eight ministries with the same pronunciation but different ''[[kanji]]'' writing form (大輔).<ref name=":0" />
== Other usages == As Fifth Rank was seen as the entrance to peerage for the [[Commoner|commoners]] and low-ranking samurai alike, it became a popular word for a distinguished person. As a result, many respectable figures in entertainment such as leading actors, entertainers, priests and prostitutes began to be dubbed with this title.<ref name=":0" />
For example, in [[kabuki]] theater, the leading female role actor (''tateoyama'') is referred to as ''tayū'', and in ''[[sarugaku]]'' theater, the leader of a troupe is called ''tayū''. In [[Shinto]], a priest who practices rituals through [[performing arts]] is called ''tayū''. In [[Jōruri (music)|''jōruri'']] narrative music, the singer is called ''tayū''. Among government permitted prostitutes, the highest rank, ''matsu'', was also called ''tayū''.<ref name=":1">{{Cite book|last=Matsumura|first=Akira|title=Daijisen|publisher=Shōgakkan|others="Daijisen" Henshūbu|year=1995|isbn=4-09-501211-0|at=大夫|language=Japanese|oclc=34170147}}</ref>
In the modern times, similar usage of ''tayū'' exists in Japanese [[street performance]], ''kadozuke'' (performance at the gate of a house), and [[Manzai|''manzai'']].<ref name=":1" />
== References ==
[[Category:Japanese historical terms]] [[Category:Titles]] [[Category:Japanese nobility]]

'''''Taifu''''' (大夫) was a [[Nobility|noble]] title in Japan, denoting a [[List of Japanese court ranks, positions and hereditary titles|court rank]] between [[Senior First Rank|First Rank]] and [[Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade|Fifth Rank]] under the ''[[Ritsuryō]]'' system. It was also commonly used to refer to a holder of Fifth Rank, but also for holders of Fourth and Fifth Rank, to differentiate from holders of First, Second and Third Rank, collectively known as [[Kugyō|''kugyō'']].
== History == In the ancient [[Yamato period]] Japan, the title was used to refer to a close attendant of the [[Emperor of Japan|Emperor]] or [[Okimi]]. Prior to the [[Taika Reform]] in 645, a government official below [[Omi|''Ōomi'']] and [[Muraji|''Ōmuraji'']] of the same name was called ''Maetsugimi'',<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|title=Nihon kokugo daijiten|publisher=Shōgakkan|others=Shōgakkan. Kokugo Jiten Henshūbu, 小学館. 国語辞典編集部.|year=2006|isbn=4-09-521021-4|at=大夫|oclc=70216445}}</ref><ref name=":3">{{Cite book|title=Nihon dai hyakka zensho|publisher=Shōgakkan|year=1989|isbn=4-09-526001-7|at=大夫|oclc=14970117}}</ref> whose duty was to submit matters to the Emperor.<ref name=":3" />
According to the ''kushiki-ryō'' of the ''[[ritsuryō]]'' law, a ''Taifu'' holds a court rank of Third Rank or higher in the [[Daijō-kan|Great Council of State]], a court rank of Fourth Rank in a government institution above a bureau, and a court rank of Fifth Rank or higher as a ''shi'' (official in a government department beneath a bureau) or in a ''chūgoku'' (a [[Provinces of Japan|province]] of the second highest rank) or lower.<ref name=":3" />
Although ''Taifu'' is the collective honorary title denoting a court rank between First Rank and Fifth Rank, holders of high-ranking court ranks above Third Rank were referred to as [[Kugyō|''kugyō'']], and thus ''Taifu'' became commonly used for holders of Fourth and Fifth Rank. As ''Taifu'' is an honorary title, the title was in heavy use for the holders of Fifth Rank, which ultimately led to ''Taifu'' colloquially becoming synonymous with Fifth Rank.<ref name=":0" />
Despite Fifth Rank being the lowest rank for [[peerage]], for regional [[samurai]] with no illustrious lineage, conferring a peerage and being awarded the title of ''Taifu'' was glorious.<ref name=":0" />
In the [[Edo period]], the top-ranking samurai officials and advisors, ''[[karō]]'', were called ''taifu''.<ref name=":1" /> A similar title seen in ancient [[Zhou dynasty|Zhou China]] as an official rank below ''kei'' (卿) and above ''shi'' (士), ''Taifu'', was taken from the Zhou Chinese system, and was used for the [[Hatamoto|''hatamoto'']].<ref name=":0" />
At the [[Ise Grand Shrine]], the deputy senior priest, ''gonnegi'', holding the court rank of Fifth Rank is called ''Taifu''.<ref name=":1" />
== Pronunciation == Although the [[kana]] form of the title is "''taifu''", in modern Japanese the pronunciation would be "''tayū''".<ref name=":0" />
The government office of the same name (but pronounced ''daibu'') referred to the Director of the Household Agency of Imperial Wives (''chūgūshiki''), the Crown Prince's Quarters (''tōgūbō''), or the Household Agency of Queen-consort (''kōgōgūshiki'').<ref name=":3" /> This pronunciation was to differentiate from the Vice-Minister of the eight ministries with the same pronunciation but different ''[[kanji]]'' writing form (大輔).<ref name=":0" />
== Other usages == As Fifth Rank was seen as the entrance to peerage for the [[Commoner|commoners]] and low-ranking samurai alike, it became a popular word for a distinguished person. As a result, many respectable figures in entertainment such as leading actors, entertainers, priests and prostitutes began to be dubbed with this title.<ref name=":0" />
For example, in [[kabuki]] theater, the leading female role actor (''tateoyama'') is referred to as ''tayū'', and in ''[[sarugaku]]'' theater, the leader of a troupe is called ''tayū''. In [[Shinto]], a priest who practices rituals through [[performing arts]] is called ''tayū''. In [[Jōruri (music)|''jōruri'']] narrative music, the singer is called ''tayū''. Among government permitted prostitutes, the highest rank, ''matsu'', was also called ''tayū''.<ref name=":1">{{Cite book|last=Matsumura|first=Akira|title=Daijisen|publisher=Shōgakkan|others="Daijisen" Henshūbu|year=1995|isbn=4-09-501211-0|at=大夫|language=Japanese|oclc=34170147}}</ref>
In the modern times, similar usage of ''tayū'' exists in Japanese [[street performance]], ''kadozuke'' (performance at the gate of a house), and [[Manzai|''manzai'']].<ref name=":1" />

It was also commonly used to refer to a holder of Fifth Rank, but also for holders of Fourth and Fifth Rank, to differentiate from holders of First, Second and Third Rank, collectively known as [[Kugyō|''kugyō'']].
== History == In the ancient [[Yamato period]] Japan, the title was used to refer to a close attendant of the [[Emperor of Japan|Emperor]] or [[Okimi]]. Prior to the [[Taika Reform]] in 645, a government official below [[Omi|''Ōomi'']] and [[Muraji|''Ōmuraji'']] of the same name was called ''Maetsugimi'',<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|title=Nihon kokugo daijiten|publisher=Shōgakkan|others=Shōgakkan. Kokugo Jiten Henshūbu, 小学館. 国語辞典編集部.|year=2006|isbn=4-09-521021-4|at=大夫|oclc=70216445}}</ref><ref name=":3">{{Cite book|title=Nihon dai hyakka zensho|publisher=Shōgakkan|year=1989|isbn=4-09-526001-7|at=大夫|oclc=14970117}}</ref> whose duty was to submit matters to the Emperor.<ref name=":3" />
According to the ''kushiki-ryō'' of the ''[[ritsuryō]]'' law, a ''Taifu'' holds a court rank of Third Rank or higher in the [[Daijō-kan|Great Council of State]], a court rank of Fourth Rank in a government institution above a bureau, and a court rank of Fifth Rank or higher as a ''shi'' (official in a government department beneath a bureau) or in a ''chūgoku'' (a [[Provinces of Japan|province]] of the second highest rank) or lower.<ref name=":3" /><ref>{{Cite book|last=Kamochi|first=Masazumi|title=萬葉集古義 第 1 巻|publisher=Seibunkan (精文館)|year=1932|pages=61}}</ref>
Although ''Taifu'' is the collective honorary title denoting a court rank between First Rank and Fifth Rank, holders of high-ranking court ranks above Third Rank were referred to as [[Kugyō|''kugyō'']], and thus ''Taifu'' became commonly used for holders of Fourth and Fifth Rank.<ref name=":0" /><ref>{{Cite book|last=Kishimoto|first=Yuzuru|title=万葉集攷証 第3巻(上)|publisher=Kokonshoin (古今書院)|year=1924|at=大夫}}</ref> As ''Taifu'' is an honorary title, the title was in heavy use for the holders of Fifth Rank, which ultimately led to ''Taifu'' colloquially becoming synonymous with Fifth Rank.<ref name=":0" />
Despite Fifth Rank being the lowest rank for [[peerage]], for regional [[samurai]] with no illustrious lineage, conferring a peerage and being awarded the title of ''Taifu'' was glorious.<ref name=":0" />
In the [[Edo period]], the top-ranking samurai officials and advisors, ''[[karō]]'', were called ''taifu''.<ref name=":1" /> A similar title seen in ancient [[Zhou dynasty|Zhou China]] as an official rank called 大夫 (''dàfū''), which ranked below 卿 (''qīng'') and above 士 (''shì''), was taken from the Zhou Chinese system, and of which the Japanized form ''taifu'' was used for the [[Hatamoto|''hatamoto'']].<ref name=":0" />
At the [[Ise Grand Shrine]], the deputy senior priest, ''gonnegi'', holding the court rank of Fifth Rank is called ''Taifu''.<ref name=":1" />
== Pronunciation == Although the [[kana]] form of the title is "''taifu''", in modern Japanese the pronunciation would be "''tayū''".<ref name=":0" />
The government office of the same name (but pronounced ''daibu'') referred to the Director of the Household Agency of Imperial Wives (''chūgūshiki''), the Crown Prince's Quarters (''tōgūbō''), or the Household Agency of Empress-consort (''kōgōgūshiki'').<ref name=":3" /> This pronunciation was to differentiate from the Vice-Minister of the eight ministries with the same pronunciation but different ''[[kanji]]'' writing form (大輔).<ref name=":0" />
== Other usages == As Fifth Rank was seen as the entrance to peerage for the [[Commoner|commoners]] and low-ranking samurai alike, it became a popular word for a distinguished person. As a result, many respectable figures in entertainment such as leading actors, entertainers, priests and prostitutes began to be dubbed with this title.<ref name=":0" />
For example, in [[kabuki]] theater, the leading female role actor (''tateoyama'') is referred to as ''tayū'', and in ''[[sarugaku]]'' theater, the leader of a troupe is called ''tayū''. In [[Shinto]], a priest who practices rituals through [[performing arts]] is called ''tayū''. In [[Jōruri (music)|''jōruri'']] narrative music, the singer is called ''tayū''. Among government permitted prostitutes, the highest rank, ''matsu'', was also called ''tayū''.<ref name=":1">{{Cite book|last=Matsumura|first=Akira|title=Daijisen|publisher=Shōgakkan|others="Daijisen" Henshūbu|year=1995|isbn=4-09-501211-0|at=大夫|language=Japanese|oclc=34170147}}</ref>
In the modern times, similar usage of ''tayū'' exists in Japanese [[street performance]], ''kadozuke'' (performance at the gate of a house), and [[Manzai|''manzai'']].<ref name=":1" />
== See also ==
* ''[[Kugyō]]'' * ''[[Omi]]'' * [[Scholar-official]] (China)

In the modern times, similar usage of ''tayū'' exists in Japanese [[street performance]], ''kadozuke'' (performance at the gate of a house), and ''[[manzai]]''.<ref name=":1" />
== See also ==
* ''[[Kugyō]]'' * ''[[Omi]]'' * [[Scholar-official]] (China)
== References == {{Reflist}}
[[Category:Japanese historical terms]] [[Category:Titles]] [[Category:Japanese nobility]]

{{Short description|Japanese noble title}} '''''Taifu''''' (大夫) was a [[Nobility|noble]] title in Japan, denoting a [[List of Japanese court ranks, positions and hereditary titles|court rank]] between [[Senior First Rank|First Rank]] and [[Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade|Fifth Rank]] under the ''[[Ritsuryō]]'' system. It was also commonly used to refer to a holder of Fifth Rank, but also for holders of Fourth and Fifth Rank, to differentiate from holders of First, Second and Third Rank, collectively known as ''[[kugyō]]''.
== History == In the ancient [[Yamato period]] Japan, the title was used to refer to a close attendant of the [[Emperor of Japan|Emperor]] or [[Okimi]]. Prior to the [[Taika Reform]] in 645, a government official below [[Omi|''Ōomi'']] and [[Muraji|''Ōmuraji'']] of the same name was called ''Maetsugimi'',<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|title=Nihon kokugo daijiten|publisher=Shōgakkan|others=Shōgakkan. Kokugo Jiten Henshūbu, 小学館. 国語辞典編集部.|year=2006|isbn=4-09-521021-4|at=大夫|oclc=70216445}}</ref><ref name=":3">{{Cite book|title=Nihon dai hyakka zensho|publisher=Shōgakkan|year=1989|isbn=4-09-526001-7|at=大夫|oclc=14970117}}</ref> whose duty was to submit matters to the Emperor.<ref name=":3" />
According to the ''kushiki-ryō'' of the ''[[ritsuryō]]'' law, a ''Taifu'' holds a court rank of Third Rank or higher in the [[Daijō-kan|Great Council of State]], a court rank of Fourth Rank in a government institution above a bureau, and a court rank of Fifth Rank or higher as a ''shi'' (official in a government department beneath a bureau) or in a ''chūgoku'' (a [[Provinces of Japan|province]] of the second highest rank) or lower.<ref name=":3" /><ref>{{Cite book|last=Kamochi|first=Masazumi|title=萬葉集古義 第 1 巻|publisher=Seibunkan (精文館)|year=1932|pages=61}}</ref>
Although ''Taifu'' is the collective honorary title denoting a court rank between First Rank and Fifth Rank, holders of high-ranking court ranks above Third Rank were referred to as ''[[kugyō]]'', and thus ''Taifu'' became commonly used for holders of Fourth and Fifth Rank.<ref name=":0" /><ref>{{Cite book|last=Kishimoto|first=Yuzuru|title=万葉集攷証 第3巻(上)|publisher=Kokonshoin (古今書院)|year=1924|at=大夫}}</ref> As ''Taifu'' is an honorary title, the title was in heavy use for the holders of Fifth Rank, which ultimately led to ''Taifu'' colloquially becoming synonymous with Fifth Rank.<ref name=":0" />
Despite Fifth Rank being the lowest rank for [[peerage]], for regional [[samurai]] with no illustrious lineage, conferring a peerage and being awarded the title of ''Taifu'' was glorious.<ref name=":0" />
In the [[Edo period]], the top-ranking samurai officials and advisors, ''[[karō]]'', were called ''taifu''.<ref name=":1" /> A similar title seen in ancient [[Zhou dynasty|Zhou China]] as an official rank called 大夫 (''dàfū''), which ranked below 卿 (''qīng'') and above 士 (''shì''), was taken from the Zhou Chinese system, and of which the Japanized form ''taifu'' was used for the ''[[hatamoto]]''.<ref name=":0" />
At the [[Ise Grand Shrine]], the deputy senior priest, ''gonnegi'', holding the court rank of Fifth Rank is called ''Taifu''.<ref name=":1" />
== Pronunciation == Although the [[kana]] form of the title is "''taifu''", in modern Japanese the pronunciation would be "''tayū''".<ref name=":0" />
The government office of the same name (but pronounced ''daibu'') referred to the director of a government agency (''shiki'') or a government office (''bō''), such as High Steward of the [[Ukyō-ku, Kyoto|Right Capital District]] (''Ukyō no Daibu'') and Director of the Crown Prince's Quarters (''Tōgū no Daibu'').<ref name=":3" /><ref name=":1" /> This pronunciation was to differentiate from the Vice-Minister of the eight ministries with the same pronunciation but different ''[[kanji]]'' writing form (大輔).<ref name=":0" />
== Other usages == As Fifth Rank was seen as the entrance to peerage for the [[commoner]]s and low-ranking samurai alike, it became a popular word for a distinguished person. As a result, many respectable figures in entertainment such as leading actors, entertainers, priests and prostitutes began to be dubbed with this title.<ref name=":0" />
For example, in [[kabuki]] theater, the leading female role actor (''tateoyama'') is referred to as ''tayū'', and in ''[[sarugaku]]'' theater, the leader of a troupe is called ''tayū''. In [[Shinto]], a priest who practices rituals through [[performing arts]] is called ''tayū''. In [[Jōruri (music)|''jōruri'']] narrative music, the singer is called ''tayū''. Among [[Yūkaku|government permitted prostitutes]], the highest rank, ''matsu'', was also called ''tayū''.<ref name=":1">{{Cite book|last=Matsumura|first=Akira|title=Daijisen|publisher=Shōgakkan|others="Daijisen" Henshūbu|year=1995|isbn=4-09-501211-0|at=大夫|language=Japanese|oclc=34170147}}</ref>
In the modern times, similar usage of ''tayū'' exists in Japanese [[street performance]], ''kadozuke'' (performance at the gate of a house), and ''[[manzai]]''.<ref name=":1" />
== See also ==
* ''[[Kugyō]]'' * ''[[Omi]]'' * [[Scholar-official]] (China)
== References == {{Reflist}}
[[Category:Japanese historical terms]] [[Category:Titles]] [[Category:Japanese nobility]]

revision.comment: Added {{[[Template:Disputed|Disputed]]}} tag: The name 'Taifu' is originated and learnt from the ancient Chinese 'Dafu'. The description here is not accurate enough causing misleading.

In the [[Edo period]], the top-ranking samurai officials and advisors, ''[[karō]]'', were called ''taifu''.<ref name=":1" /> A similar title seen in ancient [[Zhou dynasty|Zhou China]] as an official rank called 大夫 (''dàfū''), which ranked below 卿 (''qīng'') and above 士 (''shì''), was taken from the Zhou Chinese system, and of which the Japanized form ''taifu'' was used for the ''[[hatamoto]]''.<ref name=":0" />
At the [[Ise Grand Shrine]], the deputy senior priest, ''gonnegi'', holding the court rank of Fifth Rank is called ''Taifu''.<ref name=":1" />
== Pronunciation == Although the [[kana]] form of the title is "''taifu''", in modern Japanese the pronunciation would be "''tayū''".<ref name=":0" />
The government office of the same name (but pronounced ''daibu'') referred to the director of a government agency (''shiki'') or a government office (''bō''), such as High Steward of the [[Ukyō-ku, Kyoto|Right Capital District]] (''Ukyō no Daibu'') and Director of the Crown Prince's Quarters (''Tōgū no Daibu'').<ref name=":3" /><ref name=":1" /> This pronunciation was to differentiate from the Vice-Minister of the eight ministries with the same pronunciation but different ''[[kanji]]'' writing form (大輔).<ref name=":0" />
== Other usages == As Fifth Rank was seen as the entrance to peerage for the [[commoner]]s and low-ranking samurai alike, it became a popular word for a distinguished person. As a result, many respectable figures in entertainment such as leading actors, entertainers, priests and prostitutes began to be dubbed with this title.<ref name=":0" />
For example, in [[kabuki]] theater, the leading female role actor (''tateoyama'') is referred to as ''tayū'', and in ''[[sarugaku]]'' theater, the leader of a troupe is called ''tayū''. In [[Shinto]], a priest who practices rituals through [[performing arts]] is called ''tayū''. In [[Jōruri (music)|''jōruri'']] narrative music, the singer is called ''tayū''. Among [[Yūkaku|government permitted prostitutes]], the highest rank, ''matsu'', was also called ''tayū''.<ref name=":1">{{Cite book|last=Matsumura|first=Akira|title=Daijisen|publisher=Shōgakkan|others="Daijisen" Henshūbu|year=1995|isbn=4-09-501211-0|at=大夫|language=Japanese|oclc=34170147}}</ref>
In the modern times, similar usage of ''tayū'' exists in Japanese [[street performance]], ''kadozuke'' (performance at the gate of a house), and ''[[manzai]]''.<ref name=":1" />

{{Short description|Japanese noble title}} {{Disputed|date=December 2023}} '''''Taifu''''' (大夫) was a [[Nobility|noble]] title in Japan, denoting a [[List of Japanese court ranks, positions and hereditary titles|court rank]] between [[Senior First Rank|First Rank]] and [[Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade|Fifth Rank]] under the ''[[Ritsuryō]]'' system. It was also commonly used to refer to a holder of Fifth Rank, but also for holders of Fourth and Fifth Rank, to differentiate from holders of First, Second and Third Rank, collectively known as ''[[kugyō]]''.
== History == In the ancient [[Yamato period]] Japan, the title was used to refer to a close attendant of the [[Emperor of Japan|Emperor]] or [[Okimi]]. Prior to the [[Taika Reform]] in 645, a government official below [[Omi (title)|''Ōomi'']] and [[Muraji|''Ōmuraji'']] of the same name was called ''Maetsugimi'',<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|title=Nihon kokugo daijiten|publisher=Shōgakkan|others=Shōgakkan. Kokugo Jiten Henshūbu, 小学館. 国語辞典編集部.|year=2006|isbn=4-09-521021-4|at=大夫|oclc=70216445}}</ref><ref name=":3">{{Cite book|title=Nihon dai hyakka zensho|publisher=Shōgakkan|year=1989|isbn=4-09-526001-7|at=大夫|oclc=14970117}}</ref> whose duty was to submit matters to the Emperor.<ref name=":3" />
According to the ''kushiki-ryō'' of the ''[[ritsuryō]]'' law, a ''Taifu'' holds a court rank of Third Rank or higher in the [[Daijō-kan|Great Council of State]], a court rank of Fourth Rank in a government institution above a bureau, and a court rank of Fifth Rank or higher as a ''shi'' (official in a government department beneath a bureau) or in a ''chūgoku'' (a [[Provinces of Japan|province]] of the second highest rank) or lower.<ref name=":3" /><ref>{{Cite book|last=Kamochi|first=Masazumi|title=萬葉集古義 第 1 巻|publisher=Seibunkan (精文館)|year=1932|pages=61}}</ref>

At the [[Ise Grand Shrine]], the deputy senior priest, ''gonnegi'', holding the court rank of Fifth Rank is called ''Taifu''.<ref name=":1" />

revision.text:
{{Short description|Japanese noble title}} {{Disputed|date=December 2023}} '''''Taifu''''' (大夫) was a [[Nobility|noble]] title in Japan, denoting a [[List of Japanese court ranks, positions and hereditary titles|court rank]] between [[Senior First Rank|First Rank]] and [[Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade|Fifth Rank]] under the ''[[Ritsuryō]]'' system. It was also commonly used to refer to a holder of Fifth Rank, but also for holders of Fourth and Fifth Rank, to differentiate from holders of First, Second and Third Rank, collectively known as ''[[kugyō]]''.
== History == In the ancient [[Yamato period]] Japan, the title was used to refer to a close attendant of the [[Emperor of Japan|Emperor]] or [[Okimi]]. Prior to the [[Taika Reform]] in 645, a government official below [[Omi (title)|''Ōomi'']] and [[Muraji|''Ōmuraji'']] of the same name was called ''Maetsugimi'',<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|title=Nihon kokugo daijiten|publisher=Shōgakkan|others=Shōgakkan. Kokugo Jiten Henshūbu, 小学館. 国語辞典編集部.|year=2006|isbn=4-09-521021-4|at=大夫|oclc=70216445}}</ref><ref name=":3">{{Cite book|title=Nihon dai hyakka zensho|publisher=Shōgakkan|year=1989|isbn=4-09-526001-7|at=大夫|oclc=14970117}}</ref> whose duty was to submit matters to the Emperor.<ref name=":3" />
According to the ''kushiki-ryō'' of the ''[[ritsuryō]]'' law, a ''Taifu'' holds a court rank of Third Rank or higher in the [[Daijō-kan|Great Council of State]], a court rank of Fourth Rank in a government institution above a bureau, and a court rank of Fifth Rank or higher as a ''shi'' (official in a government department beneath a bureau) or in a ''chūgoku'' (a [[Provinces of Japan|province]] of the second highest rank) or lower.<ref name=":3" /><ref>{{Cite book|last=Kamochi|first=Masazumi|title=萬葉集古義 第 1 巻|publisher=Seibunkan (精文館)|year=1932|pages=61}}</ref>
Although ''Taifu'' is the collective honorary title denoting a court rank between First Rank and Fifth Rank, holders of high-ranking court ranks above Third Rank were referred to as ''[[kugyō]]'', and thus ''Taifu'' became commonly used for holders of Fourth and Fifth Rank.<ref name=":0" /><ref>{{Cite book|last=Kishimoto|first=Yuzuru|title=万葉集攷証 第3巻(上)|publisher=Kokonshoin (古今書院)|year=1924|at=大夫}}</ref> As ''Taifu'' is an honorary title, the title was in heavy use for the holders of Fifth Rank, which ultimately led to ''Taifu'' colloquially becoming synonymous with Fifth Rank.<ref name=":0" />
Despite Fifth Rank being the lowest rank for [[peerage]], for regional [[samurai]] with no illustrious lineage, conferring a peerage and being awarded the title of ''Taifu'' was glorious.<ref name=":0" />
In the [[Edo period]], the top-ranking samurai officials and advisors, ''[[karō]]'', were called ''taifu''.<ref name=":1" /> A similar title seen in ancient [[Zhou dynasty|Zhou China]] as an official rank called 大夫 (''dàfū''), which ranked below 卿 (''qīng'') and above 士 (''shì''), was taken from the Zhou Chinese system, and of which the Japanized form ''taifu'' was used for the ''[[hatamoto]]''.<ref name=":0" />
At the [[Ise Grand Shrine]], the deputy senior priest, ''gonnegi'', holding the court rank of Fifth Rank is called ''Taifu''.<ref name=":1" />
== Pronunciation == Although the [[kana]] form of the title is "''taifu''", in modern Japanese the pronunciation would be "''tayū''".<ref name=":0" />
The government office of the same name (but pronounced ''daibu'') referred to the director of a government agency (''shiki'') or a government office (''bō''), such as High Steward of the [[Ukyō-ku, Kyoto|Right Capital District]] (''Ukyō no Daibu'') and Director of the Crown Prince's Quarters (''Tōgū no Daibu'').<ref name=":3" /><ref name=":1" /> This pronunciation was to differentiate from the Vice-Minister of the eight ministries with the same pronunciation but different ''[[kanji]]'' writing form (大輔).<ref name=":0" />
== Other usages == As Fifth Rank was seen as the entrance to peerage for the [[commoner]]s and low-ranking samurai alike, it became a popular word for a distinguished person. As a result, many respectable figures in entertainment such as leading actors, entertainers, priests and prostitutes began to be dubbed with this title.<ref name=":0" />
For example, in [[kabuki]] theater, the leading female role actor (''tateoyama'') is referred to as ''tayū'', and in ''[[sarugaku]]'' theater, the leader of a troupe is called ''tayū''. In [[Shinto]], a priest who practices rituals through [[performing arts]] is called ''tayū''. In [[Jōruri (music)|''jōruri'']] narrative music, the singer is called ''tayū''. Among [[Yūkaku|government permitted prostitutes]], the highest rank, ''matsu'', was also called ''tayū''.<ref name=":1">{{Cite book|last=Matsumura|first=Akira|title=Daijisen|publisher=Shōgakkan|others="Daijisen" Henshūbu|year=1995|isbn=4-09-501211-0|at=大夫|language=Japanese|oclc=34170147}}</ref>
In the modern times, similar usage of ''tayū'' exists in Japanese [[street performance]], ''kadozuke'' (performance at the gate of a house), and ''[[manzai]]''.<ref name=":1" />
== See also ==
* ''[[Kugyō]]'' * ''[[Omi (title)|Omi]]'' * [[Scholar-official]] (China)
== References == {{Reflist}}
[[Category:Uncategorized pages]][[Category:uncategorized pages]][[Category:Uncategorized pages]][[Category:uncategorized pages]][[Category:Uncategorized pages]][[Category:uncategorized pages]][[Category:Uncategorized pages]][[Category:Japanese historical terms]] [[Category:Uncategorized pages]][[Category:uncategorized pages]][[Category:Uncategorized pages]][[Category:uncategorized pages]][[Category:Uncategorized pages]][[Category:uncategorized pages]][[Category:Uncategorized pages]][[Category:Titles]] [[Category:Uncategorized pages]][[Category:uncategorized pages]][[Category:Uncategorized pages]][[Category:uncategorized pages]][[Category:Uncategorized pages]][[Category:uncategorized pages]][[Category:Uncategorized pages]][[Category:Japanese nobility]] [[Category:Uncategorized pages]][[Category:uncategorized pages]][[Category:Uncategorized pages]][[Category:dd]] [[Category:Uncategorized pages]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Uncategorized pages]][[Category:Taifu]][[Category:Uncategorized pages]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:Uncategorized pages]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Uncategorized pages]][[Category:Taifu]][[Category:Uncategorized pages]][[Category:kk]]

Among [[Yūkaku|government permitted prostitutes]], the highest rank, ''matsu'', was also called ''tayū''.<ref name=":1">{{Cite book|last=Matsumura|first=Akira|title=Daijisen|publisher=Shōgakkan|others="Daijisen" Henshūbu|year=1995|isbn=4-09-501211-0|at=大夫|language=Japanese|oclc=34170147}}</ref>
In the modern times, similar usage of ''tayū'' exists in Japanese [[street performance]], ''kadozuke'' (performance at the gate of a house), and ''[[manzai]]''.<ref name=":1" />
== See also ==
* ''[[Kugyō]]'' * ''[[Omi (title)|Omi]]'' * [[Scholar-official]] (China)

{{Short description|Japanese noble title}} {{Disputed|date=December 2023}} '''''Taifu''''' (大夫) was a [[Nobility|noble]] title in Japan, denoting a [[List of Japanese court ranks, positions and hereditary titles|court rank]] between [[Senior First Rank|First Rank]] and [[Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade|Fifth Rank]] under the ''[[Ritsuryō]]'' system. It was also commonly used to refer to a holder of Fifth Rank, but also for holders of Fourth and Fifth Rank, to differentiate from holders of First, Second and Third Rank, collectively known as ''[[kugyō]]''.
== History == In the ancient [[Yamato period]] Japan, the title was used to refer to a close attendant of the [[Emperor of Japan|Emperor]] or [[Okimi]]. Prior to the [[Taika Reform]] in 645, a government official below [[Omi (title)|''Ōomi'']] and [[Muraji|''Ōmuraji'']] of the same name was called ''Maetsugimi'',<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|title=Nihon kokugo daijiten|publisher=Shōgakkan|others=Shōgakkan. Kokugo Jiten Henshūbu, 小学館. 国語辞典編集部.|year=2006|isbn=4-09-521021-4|at=大夫|oclc=70216445}}</ref><ref name=":3">{{Cite book|title=Nihon dai hyakka zensho|publisher=Shōgakkan|year=1989|isbn=4-09-526001-7|at=大夫|oclc=14970117}}</ref> whose duty was to submit matters to the Emperor.<ref name=":3" />
According to the ''kushiki-ryō'' of the ''[[ritsuryō]]'' law, a ''Taifu'' holds a court rank of Third Rank or higher in the [[Daijō-kan|Great Council of State]], a court rank of Fourth Rank in a government institution above a bureau, and a court rank of Fifth Rank or higher as a ''shi'' (official in a government department beneath a bureau) or in a ''chūgoku'' (a [[Provinces of Japan|province]] of the second highest rank) or lower.<ref name=":3" /><ref>{{Cite book|last=Kamochi|first=Masazumi|title=萬葉集古義 第 1 巻|publisher=Seibunkan (精文館)|year=1932|pages=61}}</ref>
Although ''Taifu'' is the collective honorary title denoting a court rank between First Rank and Fifth Rank, holders of high-ranking court ranks above Third Rank were referred to as ''[[kugyō]]'', and thus ''Taifu'' became commonly used for holders of Fourth and Fifth Rank.<ref name=":0" /><ref>{{Cite book|last=Kishimoto|first=Yuzuru|title=万葉集攷証 第3巻(上)|publisher=Kokonshoin (古今書院)|year=1924|at=大夫}}</ref> As ''Taifu'' is an honorary title, the title was in heavy use for the holders of Fifth Rank, which ultimately led to ''Taifu'' colloquially becoming synonymous with Fifth Rank.<ref name=":0" />
Despite Fifth Rank being the lowest rank for [[peerage]], for regional [[samurai]] with no illustrious lineage, conferring a peerage and being awarded the title of ''Taifu'' was glorious.<ref name=":0" />
In the [[Edo period]], the top-ranking samurai officials and advisors, ''[[karō]]'', were called ''taifu''.<ref name=":1" /> A similar title seen in ancient [[Zhou dynasty|Zhou China]] as an official rank called 大夫 (''dàfū''), which ranked below 卿 (''qīng'') and above 士 (''shì''), was taken from the Zhou Chinese system, and of which the Japanized form ''taifu'' was used for the ''[[hatamoto]]''.<ref name=":0" />
At the [[Ise Grand Shrine]], the deputy senior priest, ''gonnegi'', holding the court rank of Fifth Rank is called ''Taifu''.<ref name=":1" />
== Pronunciation == Although the [[kana]] form of the title is "''taifu''", in modern Japanese the pronunciation would be "''tayū''".<ref name=":0" />
The government office of the same name (but pronounced ''daibu'') referred to the director of a government agency (''shiki'') or a government office (''bō''), such as High Steward of the [[Ukyō-ku, Kyoto|Right Capital District]] (''Ukyō no Daibu'') and Director of the Crown Prince's Quarters (''Tōgū no Daibu'').<ref name=":3" /><ref name=":1" /> This pronunciation was to differentiate from the Vice-Minister of the eight ministries with the same pronunciation but different ''[[kanji]]'' writing form (大輔).<ref name=":0" />
== Other usages == As Fifth Rank was seen as the entrance to peerage for the [[commoner]]s and low-ranking samurai alike, it became a popular word for a distinguished person. As a result, many respectable figures in entertainment such as leading actors, entertainers, priests and prostitutes began to be dubbed with this title.<ref name=":0" />
For example, in [[kabuki]] theater, the leading female role actor (''tateoyama'') is referred to as ''tayū'', and in ''[[sarugaku]]'' theater, the leader of a troupe is called ''tayū''. In [[Shinto]], a priest who practices rituals through [[performing arts]] is called ''tayū''. In [[Jōruri (music)|''jōruri'']] narrative music, the singer is called ''tayū''. Among [[Yūkaku|government permitted prostitutes]], the highest rank, ''matsu'', was also called ''tayū''.<ref name=":1">{{Cite book|last=Matsumura|first=Akira|title=Daijisen|publisher=Shōgakkan|others="Daijisen" Henshūbu|year=1995|isbn=4-09-501211-0|at=大夫|language=Japanese|oclc=34170147}}</ref>
In the modern times, similar usage of ''tayū'' exists in Japanese [[street performance]], ''kadozuke'' (performance at the gate of a house), and ''[[manzai]]''.<ref name=":1" />
== See also ==
* ''[[Kugyō]]'' * ''[[Omi (title)|Omi]]'' * [[Scholar-official]] (China)
== References == {{Reflist}}

At the [[Ise Grand Shrine]], the deputy senior priest, ''gonnegi'', holding the court rank of Fifth Rank is called ''Taifu''.<ref name=":1" />

{{Short description|Japanese noble title}} {{Disputed|date=December 2023}} '''''Taifu''''' (大夫) was a [[Nobility|noble]] title in Japan, denoting a [[List of Japanese court ranks, positions and hereditary titles|court rank]] between [[Senior First Rank|First Rank]] and [[Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade|Fifth Rank]] under the ''[[Ritsuryō]]'' system. It was also commonly used to refer to a holder of Fifth Rank, but also for holders of Fourth and Fifth Rank, to differentiate from holders of First, Second and Third Rank, collectively known as ''[[kugyō]]''.
== History == In the ancient [[Yamato period]] Japan, the title was used to refer to a close attendant of the [[Emperor of Japan|Emperor]] or [[Okimi]]. Prior to the [[Taika Reform]] in 645, a government official below [[Omi (title)|''Ōomi'']] and [[Muraji|''Ōmuraji'']] of the same name was called ''Maetsugimi'',<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|title=Nihon kokugo daijiten|publisher=Shōgakkan|others=Shōgakkan. Kokugo Jiten Henshūbu, 小学館. 国語辞典編集部.|year=2006|isbn=4-09-521021-4|at=大夫|oclc=70216445}}</ref><ref name=":3">{{Cite book|title=Nihon dai hyakka zensho|publisher=Shōgakkan|year=1989|isbn=4-09-526001-7|at=大夫|oclc=14970117}}</ref> whose duty was to submit matters to the Emperor.<ref name=":3" />
According to the ''kushiki-ryō'' of the ''[[ritsuryō]]'' law, a ''Taifu'' holds a court rank of Third Rank or higher in the [[Daijō-kan|Great Council of State]], a court rank of Fourth Rank in a government institution above a bureau, and a court rank of Fifth Rank or higher as a ''shi'' (official in a government department beneath a bureau) or in a ''chūgoku'' (a [[Provinces of Japan|province]] of the second highest rank) or lower.<ref name=":3" /><ref>{{Cite book|last=Kamochi|first=Masazumi|title=萬葉集古義 第 1 巻|publisher=Seibunkan (精文館)|year=1932|pages=61}}</ref>
Although ''Taifu'' is the collective honorary title denoting a court rank between First Rank and Fifth Rank, holders of high-ranking court ranks above Third Rank were referred to as ''[[kugyō]]'', and thus ''Taifu'' became commonly used for holders of Fourth and Fifth Rank.<ref name=":0" /><ref>{{Cite book|last=Kishimoto|first=Yuzuru|title=万葉集攷証 第3巻(上)|publisher=Kokonshoin (古今書院)|year=1924|at=大夫}}</ref> As ''Taifu'' is an honorary title, the title was in heavy use for the holders of Fifth Rank, which ultimately led to ''Taifu'' colloquially becoming synonymous with Fifth Rank.<ref name=":0" />
Despite Fifth Rank being the lowest rank for [[peerage]], for regional [[samurai]] with no illustrious lineage, conferring a peerage and being awarded the title of ''Taifu'' was glorious.<ref name=":0" />
In the [[Edo period]], the top-ranking samurai officials and advisors, ''[[karō]]'', were called ''taifu''.<ref name=":1" /> A similar title seen in ancient [[Zhou dynasty|Zhou China]] as an official rank called 大夫 (''dàfū''), which ranked below 卿 (''qīng'') and above 士 (''shì''), was taken from the Zhou Chinese system, and of which the Japanized form ''taifu'' was used for the ''[[hatamoto]]''.<ref name=":0" />
At the [[Ise Grand Shrine]], the deputy senior priest, ''gonnegi'', holding the court rank of Fifth Rank is called ''Taifu''.<ref name=":1" />
== Pronunciation == Although the [[kana]] form of the title is "''taifu''", in modern Japanese the pronunciation would be "''tayū''".<ref name=":0" />
The government office of the same name (but pronounced ''daibu'') referred to the director of a government agency (''shiki'') or a government office (''bō''), such as High Steward of the [[Ukyō-ku, Kyoto|Right Capital District]] (''Ukyō no Daibu'') and Director of the Crown Prince's Quarters (''Tōgū no Daibu'').<ref name=":3" /><ref name=":1" /> This pronunciation was to differentiate from the Vice-Minister of the eight ministries with the same pronunciation but different ''[[kanji]]'' writing form (大輔).<ref name=":0" />
== Other usages == As Fifth Rank was seen as the entrance to peerage for the [[commoner]]s and low-ranking samurai alike, it became a popular word for a distinguished person. As a result, many respectable figures in entertainment such as leading actors, entertainers, priests and prostitutes began to be dubbed with this title.<ref name=":0" />
For example, in [[kabuki]] theater, the leading female role actor (''tateoyama'') is referred to as ''tayū'', and in ''[[sarugaku]]'' theater, the leader of a troupe is called ''tayū''. In [[Shinto]], a priest who practices rituals through [[performing arts]] is called ''tayū''. In [[Jōruri (music)|''jōruri'']] narrative music, the singer is called ''tayū''. Among [[Yūkaku|government permitted prostitutes]], the highest rank, ''matsu'', was also called ''tayū''.<ref name=":1">{{Cite book|last=Matsumura|first=Akira|title=Daijisen|publisher=Shōgakkan|others="Daijisen" Henshūbu|year=1995|isbn=4-09-501211-0|at=大夫|language=Japanese|oclc=34170147}}</ref>
In the modern times, similar usage of ''tayū'' exists in Japanese [[street performance]], ''kadozuke'' (performance at the gate of a house), and ''[[manzai]]''.<ref name=":1" />
== See also ==
* ''[[Kugyō]]'' * ''[[Omi (title)|Omi]]'' * [[Scholar-official]] (China)
== References == {{Reflist}}

Kokugo Jiten Henshūbu, 小学館. 国語辞典編集部.|year=2006|isbn=4-09-521021-4|at=大夫|oclc=70216445}}</ref><ref name=":3">{{Cite book|title=Nihon dai hyakka zensho|publisher=Shōgakkan|year=1989|isbn=4-09-526001-7|at=大夫|oclc=14970117}}</ref> whose duty was to submit matters to the Emperor.<ref name=":3" />
According to the ''kushiki-ryō'' of the ''[[ritsuryō]]'' law, a ''Taifu'' holds a court rank of Third Rank or higher in the [[Daijō-kan|Great Council of State]], a court rank of Fourth Rank in a government institution above a bureau, and a court rank of Fifth Rank or higher as a ''shi'' (official in a government department beneath a bureau) or in a ''chūgoku'' (a [[Provinces of Japan|province]] of the second highest rank) or lower.<ref name=":3" /><ref>{{Cite book|last=Kamochi|first=Masazumi|title=萬葉集古義 第 1 巻|publisher=Seibunkan (精文館)|year=1932|pages=61}}</ref>
Although ''Taifu'' is the collective honorary title denoting a court rank between First Rank and Fifth Rank, holders of high-ranking court ranks above Third Rank were referred to as ''[[kugyō]]'', and thus ''Taifu'' became commonly used for holders of Fourth and Fifth Rank.<ref name=":0" /><ref>{{Cite book|last=Kishimoto|first=Yuzuru|title=万葉集攷証 第3巻(上)|publisher=Kokonshoin (古今書院)|year=1924|at=大夫}}</ref> As ''Taifu'' is an honorary title, the title was in heavy use for the holders of Fifth Rank, which ultimately led to ''Taifu'' colloquially becoming synonymous with Fifth Rank.<ref name=":0" />
Despite Fifth Rank being the lowest rank for [[peerage]], for regional [[samurai]] with no illustrious lineage, conferring a peerage and being awarded the title of ''Taifu'' was glorious.<ref name=":0" />
In the [[Edo period]], the top-ranking samurai officials and advisors, ''[[karō]]'', were called ''taifu''.<ref name=":1" /> A similar title seen in ancient [[Zhou dynasty|Zhou China]] as an official rank called 大夫 (''dàfū''), which ranked below 卿 (''qīng'') and above 士 (''shì''), was taken from the Zhou Chinese system, and of which the Japanized form ''taifu'' was used for the ''[[hatamoto]]''.<ref name=":0" />
At the [[Ise Grand Shrine]], the deputy senior priest, ''gonnegi'', holding the court rank of Fifth Rank is called ''Taifu''.<ref name=":1" />
== Pronunciation == Although the [[kana]] form of the title is "''taifu''", in modern Japanese the pronunciation would be "''tayū''".<ref name=":0" />
The government office of the same name (but pronounced ''daibu'') referred to the director of a government agency (''shiki'') or a government office (''bō''), such as High Steward of the [[Ukyō-ku, Kyoto|Right Capital District]] (''Ukyō no Daibu'') and Director of the Crown Prince's Quarters (''Tōgū no Daibu'').<ref name=":3" /><ref name=":1" /> This pronunciation was to differentiate from the Vice-Minister of the eight ministries with the same pronunciation but different ''[[kanji]]'' writing form (大輔).<ref name=":0" />
== Other usages == As Fifth Rank was seen as the entrance to peerage for the [[commoner]]s and low-ranking samurai alike, it became a popular word for a distinguished person. As a result, many respectable figures in entertainment such as leading actors, entertainers, priests and prostitutes began to be dubbed with this title.<ref name=":0" />
For example, in [[kabuki]] theater, the leading female role actor (''tateoyama'') is referred to as ''tayū'', and in ''[[sarugaku]]'' theater, the leader of a troupe is called ''tayū''. In [[Shinto]], a priest who practices rituals through [[performing arts]] is called ''tayū''. In [[Jōruri (music)|''jōruri'']] narrative music, the singer is called ''tayū''. Among [[Yūkaku|government permitted prostitutes]], the highest rank, ''matsu'', was also called ''tayū''.<ref name=":1">{{Cite book|last=Matsumura|first=Akira|title=Daijisen|publisher=Shōgakkan|others="Daijisen" Henshūbu|year=1995|isbn=4-09-501211-0|at=大夫|language=Japanese|oclc=34170147}}</ref>
In the modern times, similar usage of ''tayū'' exists in Japanese [[street performance]], ''kadozuke'' (performance at the gate of a house), and ''[[manzai]]''.<ref name=":1" />
== See also ==
* ''[[Kugyō]]'' * ''[[Omi (title)|Omi]]'' * [[Scholar-official]] (China)
== References == {{Reflist}}

{{Short description|Japanese noble title}} {{Disputed|date=December 2023}} '''''Taifu''''' (大夫) was a [[Nobility|noble]] title in Japan, denoting a [[List of Japanese court ranks, positions and hereditary titles|court rank]] between [[Senior First Rank|First Rank]] and [[Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade|Fifth Rank]] under the ''[[Ritsuryō]]'' system. It was also commonly used to refer to a holder of Fifth Rank, but also for holders of Fourth and Fifth Rank, to differentiate from holders of First, Second and Third Rank, collectively known as ''[[kugyō]]''.
== History == In the ancient [[Yamato period]] Japan, the title was used to refer to a close attendant of the [[Emperor of Japan|Emperor]] or [[Okimi]]. Prior to the [[Taika Reform]] in 645, a government official below [[Omi (title)|''Ōomi'']] and [[Muraji|''Ōmuraji'']] of the same name was called ''Maetsugimi'',<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|title=Nihon kokugo daijiten|publisher=Shōgakkan|others=Shōgakkan. Kokugo Jiten Henshūbu, 小学館. 国語辞典編集部.|year=2006|isbn=4-09-521021-4|at=大夫|oclc=70216445}}</ref><ref name=":3">{{Cite book|title=Nihon dai hyakka zensho|publisher=Shōgakkan|year=1989|isbn=4-09-526001-7|at=大夫|oclc=14970117}}</ref> whose duty was to submit matters to the Emperor.<ref name=":3" />
According to the ''kushiki-ryō'' of the ''[[ritsuryō]]'' law, a ''Taifu'' holds a court rank of Third Rank or higher in the [[Daijō-kan|Great Council of State]], a court rank of Fourth Rank in a government institution above a bureau, and a court rank of Fifth Rank or higher as a ''shi'' (official in a government department beneath a bureau) or in a ''chūgoku'' (a [[Provinces of Japan|province]] of the second highest rank) or lower.<ref name=":3" /><ref>{{Cite book|last=Kamochi|first=Masazumi|title=萬葉集古義 第 1 巻|publisher=Seibunkan (精文館)|year=1932|pages=61}}</ref>
Although ''Taifu'' is the collective honorary title denoting a court rank between First Rank and Fifth Rank, holders of high-ranking court ranks above Third Rank were referred to as ''[[kugyō]]'', and thus ''Taifu'' became commonly used for holders of Fourth and Fifth Rank.<ref name=":0" /><ref>{{Cite book|last=Kishimoto|first=Yuzuru|title=万葉集攷証 第3巻(上)|publisher=Kokonshoin (古今書院)|year=1924|at=大夫}}</ref> As ''Taifu'' is an honorary title, the title was in heavy use for the holders of Fifth Rank, which ultimately led to ''Taifu'' colloquially becoming synonymous with Fifth Rank.<ref name=":0" />
Despite Fifth Rank being the lowest rank for [[peerage]], for regional [[samurai]] with no illustrious lineage, conferring a peerage and being awarded the title of ''Taifu'' was glorious.<ref name=":0" />
In the [[Edo period]], the top-ranking samurai officials and advisors, ''[[karō]]'', were called ''taifu''.<ref name=":1" /> A similar title seen in ancient [[Zhou dynasty|Zhou China]] as an official rank called 大夫 (''dàfū''), which ranked below 卿 (''qīng'') and above 士 (''shì''), was taken from the Zhou Chinese system, and of which the Japanized form ''taifu'' was used for the ''[[hatamoto]]''.<ref name=":0" />
At the [[Ise Grand Shrine]], the deputy senior priest, ''gonnegi'', holding the court rank of Fifth Rank is called ''Taifu''.<ref name=":1" />
== Pronunciation == Although the [[kana]] form of the title is "''taifu''", in modern Japanese the pronunciation would be "''tayū''".<ref name=":0" />
The government office of the same name (but pronounced ''daibu'') referred to the director of a government agency (''shiki'') or a government office (''bō''), such as High Steward of the [[Ukyō-ku, Kyoto|Right Capital District]] (''Ukyō no Daibu'') and Director of the Crown Prince's Quarters (''Tōgū no Daibu'').<ref name=":3" /><ref name=":1" /> This pronunciation was to differentiate from the Vice-Minister of the eight ministries with the same pronunciation but different ''[[kanji]]'' writing form (大輔).<ref name=":0" />
== Other usages == As Fifth Rank was seen as the entrance to peerage for the [[commoner]]s and low-ranking samurai alike, it became a popular word for a distinguished person. As a result, many respectable figures in entertainment such as leading actors, entertainers, priests and prostitutes began to be dubbed with this title.<ref name=":0" />
For example, in [[kabuki]] theater, the leading female role actor (''tateoyama'') is referred to as ''tayū'', and in ''[[sarugaku]]'' theater, the leader of a troupe is called ''tayū''. In [[Shinto]], a priest who practices rituals through [[performing arts]] is called ''tayū''. In [[Jōruri (music)|''jōruri'']] narrative music, the singer is called ''tayū''. Among [[Yūkaku|government permitted prostitutes]], the highest rank, ''matsu'', was also called ''tayū''.<ref name=":1">{{Cite book|last=Matsumura|first=Akira|title=Daijisen|publisher=Shōgakkan|others="Daijisen" Henshūbu|year=1995|isbn=4-09-501211-0|at=大夫|language=Japanese|oclc=34170147}}</ref>
In the modern times, similar usage of ''tayū'' exists in Japanese [[street performance]], ''kadozuke'' (performance at the gate of a house), and ''[[manzai]]''.<ref name=":1" />
== See also ==
* ''[[Kugyō]]'' * ''[[Omi (title)|Omi]]'' * [[Scholar-official]] (China)

{{Short description|Japanese noble title}} {{Disputed|date=December 2023}} '''''Taifu''''' (大夫) was a [[Nobility|noble]] title in Japan, denoting a [[List of Japanese court ranks, positions and hereditary titles|court rank]] between [[Senior First Rank|First Rank]] and [[Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade|Fifth Rank]] under the ''[[Ritsuryō]]'' system. It was also commonly used to refer to a holder of Fifth Rank, but also for holders of Fourth and Fifth Rank, to differentiate from holders of First, Second and Third Rank, collectively known as ''[[kugyō]]''.
== History == In the ancient [[Yamato period]] Japan, the title was used to refer to a close attendant of the [[Emperor of Japan|Emperor]] or [[Okimi]]. Prior to the [[Taika Reform]] in 645, a government official below [[Omi (title)|''Ōomi'']] and [[Muraji|''Ōmuraji'']] of the same name was called ''Maetsugimi'',<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|title=Nihon kokugo daijiten|publisher=Shōgakkan|others=Shōgakkan. Kokugo Jiten Henshūbu, 小学館. 国語辞典編集部.|year=2006|isbn=4-09-521021-4|at=大夫|oclc=70216445}}</ref><ref name=":3">{{Cite book|title=Nihon dai hyakka zensho|publisher=Shōgakkan|year=1989|isbn=4-09-526001-7|at=大夫|oclc=14970117}}</ref> whose duty was to submit matters to the Emperor.<ref name=":3" />
According to the ''kushiki-ryō'' of the ''[[ritsuryō]]'' law, a ''Taifu'' holds a court rank of Third Rank or higher in the [[Daijō-kan|Great Council of State]], a court rank of Fourth Rank in a government institution above a bureau, and a court rank of Fifth Rank or higher as a ''shi'' (official in a government department beneath a bureau) or in a ''chūgoku'' (a [[Provinces of Japan|province]] of the second highest rank) or lower.<ref name=":3" /><ref>{{Cite book|last=Kamochi|first=Masazumi|title=萬葉集古義 第 1 巻|publisher=Seibunkan (精文館)|year=1932|pages=61}}</ref>
Although ''Taifu'' is the collective honorary title denoting a court rank between First Rank and Fifth Rank, holders of high-ranking court ranks above Third Rank were referred to as ''[[kugyō]]'', and thus ''Taifu'' became commonly used for holders of Fourth and Fifth Rank.<ref name=":0" /><ref>{{Cite book|last=Kishimoto|first=Yuzuru|title=万葉集攷証 第3巻(上)|publisher=Kokonshoin (古今書院)|year=1924|at=大夫}}</ref> As ''Taifu'' is an honorary title, the title was in heavy use for the holders of Fifth Rank, which ultimately led to ''Taifu'' colloquially becoming synonymous with Fifth Rank.<ref name=":0" />
Despite Fifth Rank being the lowest rank for [[peerage]], for regional [[samurai]] with no illustrious lineage, conferring a peerage and being awarded the title of ''Taifu'' was glorious.<ref name=":0" />
In the [[Edo period]], the top-ranking samurai officials and advisors, ''[[karō]]'', were called ''taifu''.<ref name=":1" /> A similar title seen in ancient [[Zhou dynasty|Zhou China]] as an official rank called 大夫 (''dàfū''), which ranked below 卿 (''qīng'') and above 士 (''shì''), was taken from the Zhou Chinese system, and of which the Japanized form ''taifu'' was used for the ''[[hatamoto]]''.<ref name=":0" />
At the [[Ise Grand Shrine]], the deputy senior priest, ''gonnegi'', holding the court rank of Fifth Rank is called ''Taifu''.<ref name=":1" />
== Pronunciation == Although the [[kana]] form of the title is "''taifu''", in modern Japanese the pronunciation would be "''tayū''".<ref name=":0" />
The government office of the same name (but pronounced ''daibu'') referred to the director of a government agency (''shiki'') or a government office (''bō''), such as High Steward of the [[Ukyō-ku, Kyoto|Right Capital District]] (''Ukyō no Daibu'') and Director of the Crown Prince's Quarters (''Tōgū no Daibu'').<ref name=":3" /><ref name=":1" /> This pronunciation was to differentiate from the Vice-Minister of the eight ministries with the same pronunciation but different ''[[kanji]]'' writing form (大輔).<ref name=":0" />
== Other usages == As Fifth Rank was seen as the entrance to peerage for the [[commoner]]s and low-ranking samurai alike, it became a popular word for a distinguished person. As a result, many respectable figures in entertainment such as leading actors, entertainers, priests and prostitutes began to be dubbed with this title.<ref name=":0" />
For example, in [[kabuki]] theater, the leading female role actor (''tateoyama'') is referred to as ''tayū'', and in ''[[sarugaku]]'' theater, the leader of a troupe is called ''tayū''. In [[Shinto]], a priest who practices rituals through [[performing arts]] is called ''tayū''. In [[Jōruri (music)|''jōruri'']] narrative music, the singer is called ''tayū''. Among [[Yūkaku|government permitted prostitutes]], the highest rank, ''matsu'', was also called ''tayū''.<ref name=":1">{{Cite book|last=Matsumura|first=Akira|title=Daijisen|publisher=Shōgakkan|others="Daijisen" Henshūbu|year=1995|isbn=4-09-501211-0|at=大夫|language=Japanese|oclc=34170147}}</ref>
In the modern times, similar usage of ''tayū'' exists in Japanese [[street performance]], ''kadozuke'' (performance at the gate of a house), and ''[[manzai]]''.<ref name=":1" />
== See also ==
* ''[[Kugyō]]'' * ''[[Omi (title)|Omi]]'' * [[Scholar-official]] (China)

According to the ''kushiki-ryō'' of the ''[[ritsuryō]]'' law, a ''Taifu'' holds a court rank of Third Rank or higher in the [[Daijō-kan|Great Council of State]], a court rank of Fourth Rank in a government institution above a bureau, and a court rank of Fifth Rank or higher as a ''shi'' (official in a government department beneath a bureau) or in a ''chūgoku'' (a [[Provinces of Japan|province]] of the second highest rank) or lower.<ref name=":3" /><ref>{{Cite book|last=Kamochi|first=Masazumi|title=萬葉集古義 第 1 巻|publisher=Seibunkan (精文館)|year=1932|pages=61}}</ref>
Although ''Taifu'' is the collective honorary title denoting a court rank between First Rank and Fifth Rank, holders of high-ranking court ranks above Third Rank were referred to as ''[[kugyō]]'', and thus ''Taifu'' became commonly used for holders of Fourth and Fifth Rank.<ref name=":0" /><ref>{{Cite book|last=Kishimoto|first=Yuzuru|title=万葉集攷証 第3巻(上)|publisher=Kokonshoin (古今書院)|year=1924|at=大夫}}</ref> As ''Taifu'' is an honorary title, the title was in heavy use for the holders of Fifth Rank, which ultimately led to ''Taifu'' colloquially becoming synonymous with Fifth Rank.<ref name=":0" />
Despite Fifth Rank being the lowest rank for [[peerage]], for regional [[samurai]] with no illustrious lineage, conferring a peerage and being awarded the title of ''Taifu'' was glorious.<ref name=":0" />
In the [[Edo period]], the top-ranking samurai officials and advisors, ''[[karō]]'', were called ''taifu''.<ref name=":1" /> A similar title seen in ancient [[Zhou dynasty|Zhou China]] as an official rank called 大夫 (''dàfū''), which ranked below 卿 (''qīng'') and above 士 (''shì''), was taken from the Zhou Chinese system, and of which the Japanized form ''taifu'' was used for the ''[[hatamoto]]''.<ref name=":0" />
At the [[Ise Grand Shrine]], the deputy senior priest, ''gonnegi'', holding the court rank of Fifth Rank is called ''Taifu''.<ref name=":1" />
== Pronunciation == Although the [[kana]] form of the title is "''taifu''", in modern Japanese the pronunciation would be "''tayū''".<ref name=":0" />
The government office of the same name (but pronounced ''daibu'') referred to the director of a government agency (''shiki'') or a government office (''bō''), such as High Steward of the [[Ukyō-ku, Kyoto|Right Capital District]] (''Ukyō no Daibu'') and Director of the Crown Prince's Quarters (''Tōgū no Daibu'').<ref name=":3" /><ref name=":1" /> This pronunciation was to differentiate from the Vice-Minister of the eight ministries with the same pronunciation but different ''[[kanji]]'' writing form (大輔).<ref name=":0" />
== Other usages == As Fifth Rank was seen as the entrance to peerage for the [[commoner]]s and low-ranking samurai alike, it became a popular word for a distinguished person. As a result, many respectable figures in entertainment such as leading actors, entertainers, priests and prostitutes began to be dubbed with this title.<ref name=":0" />
For example, in [[kabuki]] theater, the leading female role actor (''tateoyama'') is referred to as ''tayū'', and in ''[[sarugaku]]'' theater, the leader of a troupe is called ''tayū''. In [[Shinto]], a priest who practices rituals through [[performing arts]] is called ''tayū''. In [[Jōruri (music)|''jōruri'']] narrative music, the singer is called ''tayū''. Among [[Yūkaku|government permitted prostitutes]], the highest rank, ''matsu'', was also called ''tayū''.<ref name=":1">{{Cite book|last=Matsumura|first=Akira|title=Daijisen|publisher=Shōgakkan|others="Daijisen" Henshūbu|year=1995|isbn=4-09-501211-0|at=大夫|language=Japanese|oclc=34170147}}</ref>
In the modern times, similar usage of ''tayū'' exists in Japanese [[street performance]], ''kadozuke'' (performance at the gate of a house), and ''[[manzai]]''.<ref name=":1" />

It was also commonly used to refer to a holder of Fifth Rank, but also for holders of Fourth and Fifth Rank, to differentiate from holders of First, Second and Third Rank, collectively known as ''[[kugyō]]''.

Prior to the [[Taika Reform]] in 645, a government official below [[Omi (title)|''Ōomi'']] and [[Muraji|''Ōmuraji'']] of the same name was called ''Maetsugimi'',<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|title=Nihon kokugo daijiten|publisher=Shōgakkan|others=Shōgakkan. Despite Fifth Rank being the lowest rank for [[peerage]], for regional [[samurai]] with no illustrious lineage, conferring a peerage and being awarded the title of ''Taifu'' was glorious.<ref name=":0" />
In the [[Edo period]], the top-ranking samurai officials and advisors, ''[[karō]]'', were called ''taifu''.<ref name=":1" /> A similar title seen in ancient [[Zhou dynasty|Zhou China]] as an official rank called 大夫 (''dàfū''), which ranked below 卿 (''qīng'') and above 士 (''shì''), was taken from the Zhou Chinese system, and of which the Japanized form ''taifu'' was used for the ''[[hatamoto]]''.<ref name=":0" />
At the [[Ise Grand Shrine]], the deputy senior priest, ''gonnegi'', holding the court rank of Fifth Rank is called ''Taifu''.<ref name=":1" />
== Pronunciation == Although the [[kana]] form of the title is "''taifu''", in modern Japanese the pronunciation would be "''tayū''".<ref name=":0" />
The government office of the same name (but pronounced ''daibu'') referred to the director of a government agency (''shiki'') or a government office (''bō''), such as High Steward of the [[Ukyō-ku, Kyoto|Right Capital District]] (''Ukyō no Daibu'') and Director of the Crown Prince's Quarters (''Tōgū no Daibu'').<ref name=":3" /><ref name=":1" /> This pronunciation was to differentiate from the Vice-Minister of the eight ministries with the same pronunciation but different ''[[kanji]]'' writing form (大輔).<ref name=":0" />

In [[Shinto]], a priest who practices rituals through [[performing arts]] is called ''tayū''. In [[Jōruri (music)|''jōruri'']] narrative music, the singer is called ''tayū''. Among [[Yūkaku|government permitted prostitutes]], the highest rank, ''matsu'', was also called ''tayū''.<ref name=":1">{{Cite book|last=Matsumura|first=Akira|title=Daijisen|publisher=Shōgakkan|others="Daijisen" Henshūbu|year=1995|isbn=4-09-501211-0|at=大夫|language=Japanese|oclc=34170147}}</ref>

Among [[Yūkaku|government permitted prostitutes]], the highest rank, ''matsu'', was also called ''tayū''.<ref name=":1">{{Cite book|last=Matsumura|first=Akira|title=Daijisen|publisher=Shōgakkan|others="Daijisen" Henshūbu|year=1995|isbn=4-09-501211-0|at=大夫|language=Japanese|oclc=34170147}}</ref>
In the modern times, similar usage of ''tayū'' exists in Japanese [[street performance]], ''kadozuke'' (performance at the gate of a house), and ''[[manzai]]''.<ref name=":1" />
== See also ==

{{Short description|Japanese noble title}} {{Disputed|date=December 2023}} '''''Taifu''''' (大夫) was a [[Nobility|noble]] title in Japan, denoting a [[List of Japanese court ranks, positions and hereditary titles|court rank]] between [[Senior First Rank|First Rank]] and [[Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade|Fifth Rank]] under the ''[[Ritsuryō]]'' system. It was also commonly used to refer to a holder of Fifth Rank, but also for holders of Fourth and Fifth Rank, to differentiate from holders of First, Second and Third Rank, collectively known as ''[[kugyō]]''.
== History == In the ancient [[Yamato period]] Japan, the title was used to refer to a close attendant of the [[Emperor of Japan|Emperor]] or [[Okimi]]. Prior to the [[Taika Reform]] in 645, a government official below [[Omi (title)|''Ōomi'']] and [[Muraji|''Ōmuraji'']] of the same name was called ''Maetsugimi'',<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|title=Nihon kokugo daijiten|publisher=Shōgakkan|others=Shōgakkan. Kokugo Jiten Henshūbu, 小学館. 国語辞典編集部.|year=2006|isbn=4-09-521021-4|at=大夫|oclc=70216445}}</ref><ref name=":3">{{Cite book|title=Nihon dai hyakka zensho|publisher=Shōgakkan|year=1989|isbn=4-09-526001-7|at=大夫|oclc=14970117}}</ref> whose duty was to submit matters to the Emperor.<ref name=":3" />
According to the ''kushiki-ryō'' of the ''[[ritsuryō]]'' law, a ''Taifu'' holds a court rank of Third Rank or higher in the [[Daijō-kan|Great Council of State]], a court rank of Fourth Rank in a government institution above a bureau, and a court rank of Fifth Rank or higher as a ''shi'' (official in a government department beneath a bureau) or in a ''chūgoku'' (a [[Provinces of Japan|province]] of the second highest rank) or lower.<ref name=":3" /><ref>{{Cite book|last=Kamochi|first=Masazumi|title=萬葉集古義 第 1 巻|publisher=Seibunkan (精文館)|year=1932|pages=61}}</ref>
Although ''Taifu'' is the collective honorary title denoting a court rank between First Rank and Fifth Rank, holders of high-ranking court ranks above Third Rank were referred to as ''[[kugyō]]'', and thus ''Taifu'' became commonly used for holders of Fourth and Fifth Rank.<ref name=":0" /><ref>{{Cite book|last=Kishimoto|first=Yuzuru|title=万葉集攷証 第3巻(上)|publisher=Kokonshoin (古今書院)|year=1924|at=大夫}}</ref> As ''Taifu'' is an honorary title, the title was in heavy use for the holders of Fifth Rank, which ultimately led to ''Taifu'' colloquially becoming synonymous with Fifth Rank.<ref name=":0" />
Despite Fifth Rank being the lowest rank for [[peerage]], for regional [[samurai]] with no illustrious lineage, conferring a peerage and being awarded the title of ''Taifu'' was glorious.<ref name=":0" />
In the [[Edo period]], the top-ranking samurai officials and advisors, ''[[karō]]'', were called ''taifu''.<ref name=":1" /> A similar title seen in ancient [[Zhou dynasty|Zhou China]] as an official rank called 大夫 (''dàfū''), which ranked below 卿 (''qīng'') and above 士 (''shì''), was taken from the Zhou Chinese system, and of which the Japanized form ''taifu'' was used for the ''[[hatamoto]]''.<ref name=":0" />
At the [[Ise Grand Shrine]], the deputy senior priest, ''gonnegi'', holding the court rank of Fifth Rank is called ''Taifu''.<ref name=":1" />
== Pronunciation == Although the [[kana]] form of the title is "''taifu''", in modern Japanese the pronunciation would be "''tayū''".<ref name=":0" />
The government office of the same name (but pronounced ''daibu'') referred to the director of a government agency (''shiki'') or a government office (''bō''), such as High Steward of the [[Ukyō-ku, Kyoto|Right Capital District]] (''Ukyō no Daibu'') and Director of the Crown Prince's Quarters (''Tōgū no Daibu'').<ref name=":3" /><ref name=":1" /> This pronunciation was to differentiate from the Vice-Minister of the eight ministries with the same pronunciation but different ''[[kanji]]'' writing form (大輔).<ref name=":0" />
== Other usages == As Fifth Rank was seen as the entrance to peerage for the [[commoner]]s and low-ranking samurai alike, it became a popular word for a distinguished person. As a result, many respectable figures in entertainment such as leading actors, entertainers, priests and prostitutes began to be dubbed with this title.<ref name=":0" />
For example, in [[kabuki]] theater, the leading female role actor (''tateoyama'') is referred to as ''tayū'', and in ''[[sarugaku]]'' theater, the leader of a troupe is called ''tayū''. In [[Shinto]], a priest who practices rituals through [[performing arts]] is called ''tayū''. In [[Jōruri (music)|''jōruri'']] narrative music, the singer is called ''tayū''. Among [[Yūkaku|government permitted prostitutes]], the highest rank, ''matsu'', was also called ''tayū''.<ref name=":1">{{Cite book|last=Matsumura|first=Akira|title=Daijisen|publisher=Shōgakkan|others="Daijisen" Henshūbu|year=1995|isbn=4-09-501211-0|at=大夫|language=Japanese|oclc=34170147}}</ref>
In the modern times, similar usage of ''tayū'' exists in Japanese [[street performance]], ''kadozuke'' (performance at the gate of a house), and ''[[manzai]]''.<ref name=":1" />
== See also ==
* ''[[Kugyō]]'' * ''[[Omi (title)|Omi]]'' * [[Scholar-official]] (China)

It was also commonly used to refer to a holder of Fifth Rank, but also for holders of Fourth and Fifth Rank, to differentiate from holders of First, Second and Third Rank, collectively known as ''[[kugyō]]''.
== History == In the ancient [[Yamato period]] Japan, the title was used to refer to a close attendant of the [[Emperor of Japan|Emperor]] or [[Okimi]]. Prior to the [[Taika Reform]] in 645, a government official below [[Omi (title)|''Ōomi'']] and [[Muraji|''Ōmuraji'']] of the same name was called ''Maetsugimi'',<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|title=Nihon kokugo daijiten|publisher=Shōgakkan|others=Shōgakkan. Kokugo Jiten Henshūbu, 小学館. 国語辞典編集部.|year=2006|isbn=4-09-521021-4|at=大夫|oclc=70216445}}</ref><ref name=":3">{{Cite book|title=Nihon dai hyakka zensho|publisher=Shōgakkan|year=1989|isbn=4-09-526001-7|at=大夫|oclc=14970117}}</ref> whose duty was to submit matters to the Emperor.<ref name=":3" />
According to the ''kushiki-ryō'' of the ''[[ritsuryō]]'' law, a ''Taifu'' holds a court rank of Third Rank or higher in the [[Daijō-kan|Great Council of State]], a court rank of Fourth Rank in a government institution above a bureau, and a court rank of Fifth Rank or higher as a ''shi'' (official in a government department beneath a bureau) or in a ''chūgoku'' (a [[Provinces of Japan|province]] of the second highest rank) or lower.<ref name=":3" /><ref>{{Cite book|last=Kamochi|first=Masazumi|title=萬葉集古義 第 1 巻|publisher=Seibunkan (精文館)|year=1932|pages=61}}</ref>
Although ''Taifu'' is the collective honorary title denoting a court rank between First Rank and Fifth Rank, holders of high-ranking court ranks above Third Rank were referred to as ''[[kugyō]]'', and thus ''Taifu'' became commonly used for holders of Fourth and Fifth Rank.<ref name=":0" /><ref>{{Cite book|last=Kishimoto|first=Yuzuru|title=万葉集攷証 第3巻(上)|publisher=Kokonshoin (古今書院)|year=1924|at=大夫}}</ref> As ''Taifu'' is an honorary title, the title was in heavy use for the holders of Fifth Rank, which ultimately led to ''Taifu'' colloquially becoming synonymous with Fifth Rank.<ref name=":0" />
Despite Fifth Rank being the lowest rank for [[peerage]], for regional [[samurai]] with no illustrious lineage, conferring a peerage and being awarded the title of ''Taifu'' was glorious.<ref name=":0" />
In the [[Edo period]], the top-ranking samurai officials and advisors, ''[[karō]]'', were called ''taifu''.<ref name=":1" /> A similar title seen in ancient [[Zhou dynasty|Zhou China]] as an official rank called 大夫 (''dàfū''), which ranked below 卿 (''qīng'') and above 士 (''shì''), was taken from the Zhou Chinese system, and of which the Japanized form ''taifu'' was used for the ''[[hatamoto]]''.<ref name=":0" />
At the [[Ise Grand Shrine]], the deputy senior priest, ''gonnegi'', holding the court rank of Fifth Rank is called ''Taifu''.<ref name=":1" />
== Pronunciation == Although the [[kana]] form of the title is "''taifu''", in modern Japanese the pronunciation would be "''tayū''".<ref name=":0" />
The government office of the same name (but pronounced ''daibu'') referred to the director of a government agency (''shiki'') or a government office (''bō''), such as High Steward of the [[Ukyō-ku, Kyoto|Right Capital District]] (''Ukyō no Daibu'') and Director of the Crown Prince's Quarters (''Tōgū no Daibu'').<ref name=":3" /><ref name=":1" /> This pronunciation was to differentiate from the Vice-Minister of the eight ministries with the same pronunciation but different ''[[kanji]]'' writing form (大輔).<ref name=":0" />
== Other usages == As Fifth Rank was seen as the entrance to peerage for the [[commoner]]s and low-ranking samurai alike, it became a popular word for a distinguished person. As a result, many respectable figures in entertainment such as leading actors, entertainers, priests and prostitutes began to be dubbed with this title.<ref name=":0" />
For example, in [[kabuki]] theater, the leading female role actor (''tateoyama'') is referred to as ''tayū'', and in ''[[sarugaku]]'' theater, the leader of a troupe is called ''tayū''. In [[Shinto]], a priest who practices rituals through [[performing arts]] is called ''tayū''. In [[Jōruri (music)|''jōruri'']] narrative music, the singer is called ''tayū''. Among [[Yūkaku|government permitted prostitutes]], the highest rank, ''matsu'', was also called ''tayū''.<ref name=":1">{{Cite book|last=Matsumura|first=Akira|title=Daijisen|publisher=Shōgakkan|others="Daijisen" Henshūbu|year=1995|isbn=4-09-501211-0|at=大夫|language=Japanese|oclc=34170147}}</ref>
In the modern times, similar usage of ''tayū'' exists in Japanese [[street performance]], ''kadozuke'' (performance at the gate of a house), and ''[[manzai]]''.<ref name=":1" />
== See also ==
* ''[[Kugyō]]'' * ''[[Omi (title)|Omi]]'' * [[Scholar-official]] (China)

* ''[[Kugyō]]'' * ''[[Omi (title)|Omi]]'' * [[Scholar-official]] (China)
== References == {{Reflist}}
