{{Short description|Japanese noble title}} {{Disputed|date=December 2023}} '''''Taifu''''' (大夫) was a [[Nobility|noble]] title in Japan, denoting a [[List of Japanese court ranks, positions and hereditary titles|court rank]] between [[Senior First Rank|First Rank]] and [[Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade|Fifth Rank]] under the ''[[Ritsuryō]]'' system. It was also commonly used to refer to a holder of Fifth Rank, but also for holders of Fourth and Fifth Rank, to differentiate from holders of First, Second and Third Rank, collectively known as ''[[kugyō]]''.
== History == In the ancient [[Yamato period]] Japan, the title was used to refer to a close attendant of the [[Emperor of Japan|Emperor]] or [[Okimi]]. Prior to the [[Taika Reform]] in 645, a government official below [[Omi (title)|''Ōomi'']] and [[Muraji|''Ōmuraji'']] of the same name was called ''Maetsugimi'',<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|title=Nihon kokugo daijiten|publisher=Shōgakkan|others=Shōgakkan. Kokugo Jiten Henshūbu, 小学館. 国語辞典編集部.|year=2006|isbn=4-09-521021-4|at=大夫|oclc=70216445}}</ref><ref name=":3">{{Cite book|title=Nihon dai hyakka zensho|publisher=Shōgakkan|year=1989|isbn=4-09-526001-7|at=大夫|oclc=14970117}}</ref> whose duty was to submit matters to the Emperor.<ref name=":3" />
According to the ''kushiki-ryō'' of the ''[[ritsuryō]]'' law, a ''Taifu'' holds a court rank of Third Rank or higher in the [[Daijō-kan|Great Council of State]], a court rank of Fourth Rank in a government institution above a bureau, and a court rank of Fifth Rank or higher as a ''shi'' (official in a government department beneath a bureau) or in a ''chūgoku'' (a [[Provinces of Japan|province]] of the second highest rank) or lower.<ref name=":3" /><ref>{{Cite book|last=Kamochi|first=Masazumi|title=萬葉集古義 第 1 巻|publisher=Seibunkan (精文館)|year=1932|pages=61}}</ref>
Although ''Taifu'' is the collective honorary title denoting a court rank between First Rank and Fifth Rank, holders of high-ranking court ranks above Third Rank were referred to as ''[[kugyō]]'', and thus ''Taifu'' became commonly used for holders of Fourth and Fifth Rank.<ref name=":0" /><ref>{{Cite book|last=Kishimoto|first=Yuzuru|title=万葉集攷証 第3巻(上)|publisher=Kokonshoin (古今書院)|year=1924|at=大夫}}</ref> As ''Taifu'' is an honorary title, the title was in heavy use for the holders of Fifth Rank, which ultimately led to ''Taifu'' colloquially becoming synonymous with Fifth Rank.<ref name=":0" />
Despite Fifth Rank being the lowest rank for [[peerage]], for regional [[samurai]] with no illustrious lineage, conferring a peerage and being awarded the title of ''Taifu'' was glorious.<ref name=":0" />
In the [[Edo period]], the top-ranking samurai officials and advisors, ''[[karō]]'', were called ''taifu''.<ref name=":1" /> A similar title seen in ancient [[Zhou dynasty|Zhou China]] as an official rank called 大夫 (''dàfū''), which ranked below 卿 (''qīng'') and above 士 (''shì''), was taken from the Zhou Chinese system, and of which the Japanized form ''taifu'' was used for the ''[[hatamoto]]''.<ref name=":0" />
At the [[Ise Grand Shrine]], the deputy senior priest, ''gonnegi'', holding the court rank of Fifth Rank is called ''Taifu''.<ref name=":1" />
== Pronunciation == Although the [[kana]] form of the title is "''taifu''", in modern Japanese the pronunciation would be "''tayū''".<ref name=":0" />
The government office of the same name (but pronounced ''daibu'') referred to the director of a government agency (''shiki'') or a government office (''bō''), such as High Steward of the [[Ukyō-ku, Kyoto|Right Capital District]] (''Ukyō no Daibu'') and Director of the Crown Prince's Quarters (''Tōgū no Daibu'').<ref name=":3" /><ref name=":1" /> This pronunciation was to differentiate from the Vice-Minister of the eight ministries with the same pronunciation but different ''[[kanji]]'' writing form (大輔).<ref name=":0" />
== Other usages == As Fifth Rank was seen as the entrance to peerage for the [[commoner]]s and low-ranking samurai alike, it became a popular word for a distinguished person. As a result, many respectable figures in entertainment such as leading actors, entertainers, priests and prostitutes began to be dubbed with this title.<ref name=":0" />
For example, in [[kabuki]] theater, the leading female role actor (''tateoyama'') is referred to as ''tayū'', and in ''[[sarugaku]]'' theater, the leader of a troupe is called ''tayū''. In [[Shinto]], a priest who practices rituals through [[performing arts]] is called ''tayū''. In [[Jōruri (music)|''jōruri'']] narrative music, the singer is called ''tayū''. Among [[Yūkaku|government permitted prostitutes]], the highest rank, ''matsu'', was also called ''tayū''.<ref name=":1">{{Cite book|last=Matsumura|first=Akira|title=Daijisen|publisher=Shōgakkan|others="Daijisen" Henshūbu|year=1995|isbn=4-09-501211-0|at=大夫|language=Japanese|oclc=34170147}}</ref>
In the modern times, similar usage of ''tayū'' exists in Japanese [[street performance]], ''kadozuke'' (performance at the gate of a house), and ''[[manzai]]''.<ref name=":1" />
== See also ==
* ''[[Kugyō]]'' * ''[[Omi (title)|Omi]]'' * [[Scholar-official]] (China)
== References == {{Reflist}}

At the [[Ise Grand Shrine]], the deputy senior priest, ''gonnegi'', holding the court rank of Fifth Rank is called ''Taifu''.<ref name=":1" />
== Pronunciation == Although the [[kana]] form of the title is "''taifu''", in modern Japanese the pronunciation would be "''tayū''".<ref name=":0" />
The government office of the same name (but pronounced ''daibu'') referred to the director of a government agency (''shiki'') or a government office (''bō''), such as High Steward of the [[Ukyō-ku, Kyoto|Right Capital District]] (''Ukyō no Daibu'') and Director of the Crown Prince's Quarters (''Tōgū no Daibu'').<ref name=":3" /><ref name=":1" /> This pronunciation was to differentiate from the Vice-Minister of the eight ministries with the same pronunciation but different ''[[kanji]]'' writing form (大輔).<ref name=":0" />
== Other usages == As Fifth Rank was seen as the entrance to peerage for the [[commoner]]s and low-ranking samurai alike, it became a popular word for a distinguished person. As a result, many respectable figures in entertainment such as leading actors, entertainers, priests and prostitutes began to be dubbed with this title.<ref name=":0" />
For example, in [[kabuki]] theater, the leading female role actor (''tateoyama'') is referred to as ''tayū'', and in ''[[sarugaku]]'' theater, the leader of a troupe is called ''tayū''. In [[Shinto]], a priest who practices rituals through [[performing arts]] is called ''tayū''. In [[Jōruri (music)|''jōruri'']] narrative music, the singer is called ''tayū''. Among [[Yūkaku|government permitted prostitutes]], the highest rank, ''matsu'', was also called ''tayū''.<ref name=":1">{{Cite book|last=Matsumura|first=Akira|title=Daijisen|publisher=Shōgakkan|others="Daijisen" Henshūbu|year=1995|isbn=4-09-501211-0|at=大夫|language=Japanese|oclc=34170147}}</ref>
In the modern times, similar usage of ''tayū'' exists in Japanese [[street performance]], ''kadozuke'' (performance at the gate of a house), and ''[[manzai]]''.<ref name=":1" />
== See also ==
* ''[[Kugyō]]'' * ''[[Omi (title)|Omi]]'' * [[Scholar-official]] (China)
== References == {{Reflist}}

{{Short description|Japanese noble title}} {{Disputed|date=December 2023}} '''''Taifu''''' (大夫) was a [[Nobility|noble]] title in Japan, denoting a [[List of Japanese court ranks, positions and hereditary titles|court rank]] between [[Senior First Rank|First Rank]] and [[Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade|Fifth Rank]] under the ''[[Ritsuryō]]'' system. It was also commonly used to refer to a holder of Fifth Rank, but also for holders of Fourth and Fifth Rank, to differentiate from holders of First, Second and Third Rank, collectively known as ''[[kugyō]]''.
== History == In the ancient [[Yamato period]] Japan, the title was used to refer to a close attendant of the [[Emperor of Japan|Emperor]] or [[Okimi]]. Prior to the [[Taika Reform]] in 645, a government official below [[Omi (title)|''Ōomi'']] and [[Muraji|''Ōmuraji'']] of the same name was called ''Maetsugimi'',<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|title=Nihon kokugo daijiten|publisher=Shōgakkan|others=Shōgakkan. Kokugo Jiten Henshūbu, 小学館. 国語辞典編集部.|year=2006|isbn=4-09-521021-4|at=大夫|oclc=70216445}}</ref><ref name=":3">{{Cite book|title=Nihon dai hyakka zensho|publisher=Shōgakkan|year=1989|isbn=4-09-526001-7|at=大夫|oclc=14970117}}</ref> whose duty was to submit matters to the Emperor.<ref name=":3" />
According to the ''kushiki-ryō'' of the ''[[ritsuryō]]'' law, a ''Taifu'' holds a court rank of Third Rank or higher in the [[Daijō-kan|Great Council of State]], a court rank of Fourth Rank in a government institution above a bureau, and a court rank of Fifth Rank or higher as a ''shi'' (official in a government department beneath a bureau) or in a ''chūgoku'' (a [[Provinces of Japan|province]] of the second highest rank) or lower.<ref name=":3" /><ref>{{Cite book|last=Kamochi|first=Masazumi|title=萬葉集古義 第 1 巻|publisher=Seibunkan (精文館)|year=1932|pages=61}}</ref>
Although ''Taifu'' is the collective honorary title denoting a court rank between First Rank and Fifth Rank, holders of high-ranking court ranks above Third Rank were referred to as ''[[kugyō]]'', and thus ''Taifu'' became commonly used for holders of Fourth and Fifth Rank.<ref name=":0" /><ref>{{Cite book|last=Kishimoto|first=Yuzuru|title=万葉集攷証 第3巻(上)|publisher=Kokonshoin (古今書院)|year=1924|at=大夫}}</ref> As ''Taifu'' is an honorary title, the title was in heavy use for the holders of Fifth Rank, which ultimately led to ''Taifu'' colloquially becoming synonymous with Fifth Rank.<ref name=":0" />
Despite Fifth Rank being the lowest rank for [[peerage]], for regional [[samurai]] with no illustrious lineage, conferring a peerage and being awarded the title of ''Taifu'' was glorious.<ref name=":0" />
In the [[Edo period]], the top-ranking samurai officials and advisors, ''[[karō]]'', were called ''taifu''.<ref name=":1" /> A similar title seen in ancient [[Zhou dynasty|Zhou China]] as an official rank called 大夫 (''dàfū''), which ranked below 卿 (''qīng'') and above 士 (''shì''), was taken from the Zhou Chinese system, and of which the Japanized form ''taifu'' was used for the ''[[hatamoto]]''.<ref name=":0" />
At the [[Ise Grand Shrine]], the deputy senior priest, ''gonnegi'', holding the court rank of Fifth Rank is called ''Taifu''.<ref name=":1" />
== Pronunciation == Although the [[kana]] form of the title is "''taifu''", in modern Japanese the pronunciation would be "''tayū''".<ref name=":0" />
The government office of the same name (but pronounced ''daibu'') referred to the director of a government agency (''shiki'') or a government office (''bō''), such as High Steward of the [[Ukyō-ku, Kyoto|Right Capital District]] (''Ukyō no Daibu'') and Director of the Crown Prince's Quarters (''Tōgū no Daibu'').<ref name=":3" /><ref name=":1" /> This pronunciation was to differentiate from the Vice-Minister of the eight ministries with the same pronunciation but different ''[[kanji]]'' writing form (大輔).<ref name=":0" />
== Other usages == As Fifth Rank was seen as the entrance to peerage for the [[commoner]]s and low-ranking samurai alike, it became a popular word for a distinguished person. As a result, many respectable figures in entertainment such as leading actors, entertainers, priests and prostitutes began to be dubbed with this title.<ref name=":0" />
For example, in [[kabuki]] theater, the leading female role actor (''tateoyama'') is referred to as ''tayū'', and in ''[[sarugaku]]'' theater, the leader of a troupe is called ''tayū''. In [[Shinto]], a priest who practices rituals through [[performing arts]] is called ''tayū''. In [[Jōruri (music)|''jōruri'']] narrative music, the singer is called ''tayū''. Among [[Yūkaku|government permitted prostitutes]], the highest rank, ''matsu'', was also called ''tayū''.<ref name=":1">{{Cite book|last=Matsumura|first=Akira|title=Daijisen|publisher=Shōgakkan|others="Daijisen" Henshūbu|year=1995|isbn=4-09-501211-0|at=大夫|language=Japanese|oclc=34170147}}</ref>
In the modern times, similar usage of ''tayū'' exists in Japanese [[street performance]], ''kadozuke'' (performance at the gate of a house), and ''[[manzai]]''.<ref name=":1" />
== See also ==
* ''[[Kugyō]]'' * ''[[Omi (title)|Omi]]'' * [[Scholar-official]] (China)
== References == {{Reflist}}

{{Short description|Japanese noble title}} {{Disputed|date=December 2023}} '''''Taifu''''' (大夫) was a [[Nobility|noble]] title in Japan, denoting a [[List of Japanese court ranks, positions and hereditary titles|court rank]] between [[Senior First Rank|First Rank]] and [[Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade|Fifth Rank]] under the ''[[Ritsuryō]]'' system. It was also commonly used to refer to a holder of Fifth Rank, but also for holders of Fourth and Fifth Rank, to differentiate from holders of First, Second and Third Rank, collectively known as ''[[kugyō]]''.
== History == In the ancient [[Yamato period]] Japan, the title was used to refer to a close attendant of the [[Emperor of Japan|Emperor]] or [[Okimi]]. Prior to the [[Taika Reform]] in 645, a government official below [[Omi (title)|''Ōomi'']] and [[Muraji|''Ōmuraji'']] of the same name was called ''Maetsugimi'',<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|title=Nihon kokugo daijiten|publisher=Shōgakkan|others=Shōgakkan. Kokugo Jiten Henshūbu, 小学館. 国語辞典編集部.|year=2006|isbn=4-09-521021-4|at=大夫|oclc=70216445}}</ref><ref name=":3">{{Cite book|title=Nihon dai hyakka zensho|publisher=Shōgakkan|year=1989|isbn=4-09-526001-7|at=大夫|oclc=14970117}}</ref> whose duty was to submit matters to the Emperor.<ref name=":3" />
According to the ''kushiki-ryō'' of the ''[[ritsuryō]]'' law, a ''Taifu'' holds a court rank of Third Rank or higher in the [[Daijō-kan|Great Council of State]], a court rank of Fourth Rank in a government institution above a bureau, and a court rank of Fifth Rank or higher as a ''shi'' (official in a government department beneath a bureau) or in a ''chūgoku'' (a [[Provinces of Japan|province]] of the second highest rank) or lower.<ref name=":3" /><ref>{{Cite book|last=Kamochi|first=Masazumi|title=萬葉集古義 第 1 巻|publisher=Seibunkan (精文館)|year=1932|pages=61}}</ref>
Although ''Taifu'' is the collective honorary title denoting a court rank between First Rank and Fifth Rank, holders of high-ranking court ranks above Third Rank were referred to as ''[[kugyō]]'', and thus ''Taifu'' became commonly used for holders of Fourth and Fifth Rank.<ref name=":0" /><ref>{{Cite book|last=Kishimoto|first=Yuzuru|title=万葉集攷証 第3巻(上)|publisher=Kokonshoin (古今書院)|year=1924|at=大夫}}</ref> As ''Taifu'' is an honorary title, the title was in heavy use for the holders of Fifth Rank, which ultimately led to ''Taifu'' colloquially becoming synonymous with Fifth Rank.<ref name=":0" />
Despite Fifth Rank being the lowest rank for [[peerage]], for regional [[samurai]] with no illustrious lineage, conferring a peerage and being awarded the title of ''Taifu'' was glorious.<ref name=":0" />
In the [[Edo period]], the top-ranking samurai officials and advisors, ''[[karō]]'', were called ''taifu''.<ref name=":1" /> A similar title seen in ancient [[Zhou dynasty|Zhou China]] as an official rank called 大夫 (''dàfū''), which ranked below 卿 (''qīng'') and above 士 (''shì''), was taken from the Zhou Chinese system, and of which the Japanized form ''taifu'' was used for the ''[[hatamoto]]''.<ref name=":0" />
At the [[Ise Grand Shrine]], the deputy senior priest, ''gonnegi'', holding the court rank of Fifth Rank is called ''Taifu''.<ref name=":1" />
== Pronunciation == Although the [[kana]] form of the title is "''taifu''", in modern Japanese the pronunciation would be "''tayū''".<ref name=":0" />
The government office of the same name (but pronounced ''daibu'') referred to the director of a government agency (''shiki'') or a government office (''bō''), such as High Steward of the [[Ukyō-ku, Kyoto|Right Capital District]] (''Ukyō no Daibu'') and Director of the Crown Prince's Quarters (''Tōgū no Daibu'').<ref name=":3" /><ref name=":1" /> This pronunciation was to differentiate from the Vice-Minister of the eight ministries with the same pronunciation but different ''[[kanji]]'' writing form (大輔).<ref name=":0" />
== Other usages == As Fifth Rank was seen as the entrance to peerage for the [[commoner]]s and low-ranking samurai alike, it became a popular word for a distinguished person. As a result, many respectable figures in entertainment such as leading actors, entertainers, priests and prostitutes began to be dubbed with this title.<ref name=":0" />
For example, in [[kabuki]] theater, the leading female role actor (''tateoyama'') is referred to as ''tayū'', and in ''[[sarugaku]]'' theater, the leader of a troupe is called ''tayū''. In [[Shinto]], a priest who practices rituals through [[performing arts]] is called ''tayū''. In [[Jōruri (music)|''jōruri'']] narrative music, the singer is called ''tayū''. Among [[Yūkaku|government permitted prostitutes]], the highest rank, ''matsu'', was also called ''tayū''.<ref name=":1">{{Cite book|last=Matsumura|first=Akira|title=Daijisen|publisher=Shōgakkan|others="Daijisen" Henshūbu|year=1995|isbn=4-09-501211-0|at=大夫|language=Japanese|oclc=34170147}}</ref>
In the modern times, similar usage of ''tayū'' exists in Japanese [[street performance]], ''kadozuke'' (performance at the gate of a house), and ''[[manzai]]''.<ref name=":1" />
== See also ==
* ''[[Kugyō]]'' * ''[[Omi (title)|Omi]]'' * [[Scholar-official]] (China)
== References == {{Reflist}}

== Pronunciation == Although the [[kana]] form of the title is "''taifu''", in modern Japanese the pronunciation would be "''tayū''".<ref name=":0" />
The government office of the same name (but pronounced ''daibu'') referred to the director of a government agency (''shiki'') or a government office (''bō''), such as High Steward of the [[Ukyō-ku, Kyoto|Right Capital District]] (''Ukyō no Daibu'') and Director of the Crown Prince's Quarters (''Tōgū no Daibu'').<ref name=":3" /><ref name=":1" /> This pronunciation was to differentiate from the Vice-Minister of the eight ministries with the same pronunciation but different ''[[kanji]]'' writing form (大輔).<ref name=":0" />
== Other usages == As Fifth Rank was seen as the entrance to peerage for the [[commoner]]s and low-ranking samurai alike, it became a popular word for a distinguished person. As a result, many respectable figures in entertainment such as leading actors, entertainers, priests and prostitutes began to be dubbed with this title.<ref name=":0" />
For example, in [[kabuki]] theater, the leading female role actor (''tateoyama'') is referred to as ''tayū'', and in ''[[sarugaku]]'' theater, the leader of a troupe is called ''tayū''. In [[Shinto]], a priest who practices rituals through [[performing arts]] is called ''tayū''. In [[Jōruri (music)|''jōruri'']] narrative music, the singer is called ''tayū''. Among [[Yūkaku|government permitted prostitutes]], the highest rank, ''matsu'', was also called ''tayū''.<ref name=":1">{{Cite book|last=Matsumura|first=Akira|title=Daijisen|publisher=Shōgakkan|others="Daijisen" Henshūbu|year=1995|isbn=4-09-501211-0|at=大夫|language=Japanese|oclc=34170147}}</ref>
In the modern times, similar usage of ''tayū'' exists in Japanese [[street performance]], ''kadozuke'' (performance at the gate of a house), and ''[[manzai]]''.<ref name=":1" />
== See also ==
* ''[[Kugyō]]'' * ''[[Omi (title)|Omi]]'' * [[Scholar-official]] (China)

{{Short description|Japanese noble title}} {{Disputed|date=December 2023}} '''''Taifu''''' (大夫) was a [[Nobility|noble]] title in Japan, denoting a [[List of Japanese court ranks, positions and hereditary titles|court rank]] between [[Senior First Rank|First Rank]] and [[Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade|Fifth Rank]] under the ''[[Ritsuryō]]'' system. It was also commonly used to refer to a holder of Fifth Rank, but also for holders of Fourth and Fifth Rank, to differentiate from holders of First, Second and Third Rank, collectively known as ''[[kugyō]]''.
== History == In the ancient [[Yamato period]] Japan, the title was used to refer to a close attendant of the [[Emperor of Japan|Emperor]] or [[Okimi]]. Prior to the [[Taika Reform]] in 645, a government official below [[Omi (title)|''Ōomi'']] and [[Muraji|''Ōmuraji'']] of the same name was called ''Maetsugimi'',<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|title=Nihon kokugo daijiten|publisher=Shōgakkan|others=Shōgakkan. Kokugo Jiten Henshūbu, 小学館. 国語辞典編集部.|year=2006|isbn=4-09-521021-4|at=大夫|oclc=70216445}}</ref><ref name=":3">{{Cite book|title=Nihon dai hyakka zensho|publisher=Shōgakkan|year=1989|isbn=4-09-526001-7|at=大夫|oclc=14970117}}</ref> whose duty was to submit matters to the Emperor.<ref name=":3" />
According to the ''kushiki-ryō'' of the ''[[ritsuryō]]'' law, a ''Taifu'' holds a court rank of Third Rank or higher in the [[Daijō-kan|Great Council of State]], a court rank of Fourth Rank in a government institution above a bureau, and a court rank of Fifth Rank or higher as a ''shi'' (official in a government department beneath a bureau) or in a ''chūgoku'' (a [[Provinces of Japan|province]] of the second highest rank) or lower.<ref name=":3" /><ref>{{Cite book|last=Kamochi|first=Masazumi|title=萬葉集古義 第 1 巻|publisher=Seibunkan (精文館)|year=1932|pages=61}}</ref>
Although ''Taifu'' is the collective honorary title denoting a court rank between First Rank and Fifth Rank, holders of high-ranking court ranks above Third Rank were referred to as ''[[kugyō]]'', and thus ''Taifu'' became commonly used for holders of Fourth and Fifth Rank.<ref name=":0" /><ref>{{Cite book|last=Kishimoto|first=Yuzuru|title=万葉集攷証 第3巻(上)|publisher=Kokonshoin (古今書院)|year=1924|at=大夫}}</ref> As ''Taifu'' is an honorary title, the title was in heavy use for the holders of Fifth Rank, which ultimately led to ''Taifu'' colloquially becoming synonymous with Fifth Rank.<ref name=":0" />
Despite Fifth Rank being the lowest rank for [[peerage]], for regional [[samurai]] with no illustrious lineage, conferring a peerage and being awarded the title of ''Taifu'' was glorious.<ref name=":0" />
In the [[Edo period]], the top-ranking samurai officials and advisors, ''[[karō]]'', were called ''taifu''.<ref name=":1" /> A similar title seen in ancient [[Zhou dynasty|Zhou China]] as an official rank called 大夫 (''dàfū''), which ranked below 卿 (''qīng'') and above 士 (''shì''), was taken from the Zhou Chinese system, and of which the Japanized form ''taifu'' was used for the ''[[hatamoto]]''.<ref name=":0" />
At the [[Ise Grand Shrine]], the deputy senior priest, ''gonnegi'', holding the court rank of Fifth Rank is called ''Taifu''.<ref name=":1" />
== Pronunciation == Although the [[kana]] form of the title is "''taifu''", in modern Japanese the pronunciation would be "''tayū''".<ref name=":0" />
The government office of the same name (but pronounced ''daibu'') referred to the director of a government agency (''shiki'') or a government office (''bō''), such as High Steward of the [[Ukyō-ku, Kyoto|Right Capital District]] (''Ukyō no Daibu'') and Director of the Crown Prince's Quarters (''Tōgū no Daibu'').<ref name=":3" /><ref name=":1" /> This pronunciation was to differentiate from the Vice-Minister of the eight ministries with the same pronunciation but different ''[[kanji]]'' writing form (大輔).<ref name=":0" />
== Other usages == As Fifth Rank was seen as the entrance to peerage for the [[commoner]]s and low-ranking samurai alike, it became a popular word for a distinguished person. As a result, many respectable figures in entertainment such as leading actors, entertainers, priests and prostitutes began to be dubbed with this title.<ref name=":0" />
For example, in [[kabuki]] theater, the leading female role actor (''tateoyama'') is referred to as ''tayū'', and in ''[[sarugaku]]'' theater, the leader of a troupe is called ''tayū''. In [[Shinto]], a priest who practices rituals through [[performing arts]] is called ''tayū''. In [[Jōruri (music)|''jōruri'']] narrative music, the singer is called ''tayū''. Among [[Yūkaku|government permitted prostitutes]], the highest rank, ''matsu'', was also called ''tayū''.<ref name=":1">{{Cite book|last=Matsumura|first=Akira|title=Daijisen|publisher=Shōgakkan|others="Daijisen" Henshūbu|year=1995|isbn=4-09-501211-0|at=大夫|language=Japanese|oclc=34170147}}</ref>
In the modern times, similar usage of ''tayū'' exists in Japanese [[street performance]], ''kadozuke'' (performance at the gate of a house), and ''[[manzai]]''.<ref name=":1" />
== See also ==
* ''[[Kugyō]]'' * ''[[Omi (title)|Omi]]'' * [[Scholar-official]] (China)
== References == {{Reflist}}

{{Short description|Japanese noble title}} {{Disputed|date=December 2023}} '''''Taifu''''' (大夫) was a [[Nobility|noble]] title in Japan, denoting a [[List of Japanese court Ranks, positions and hereditary titles|court Rank]] between [[Senior First Rank|First Rank]] and [[Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade|Fifth Rank]] under the ''[[Ritsuryō]]'' system. It was also commonly used to refer to a holder of Fifth Rank, but also for holders of Fourth and Fifth Rank, to differentiate from holders of First, Second and Third Rank, collectively known as ''[[kugyō]]''.
== History == In the ancient [[Yamato period]] Japan, the title was used to refer to a close attendant of the [[Emperor of Japan|Emperor]] or [[Okimi]]. Prior to the [[Taika Reform]] in 645, a government official below [[Omi (title)|''Ōomi'']] and [[Muraji|''Ōmuraji'']] of the same name was called ''Maetsugimi'',<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|title=Nihon kokugo daijiten|publisher=Shōgakkan|others=Shōgakkan. Kokugo Jiten Henshūbu, 小学館. 国語辞典編集部.|year=2006|isbn=4-09-521021-4|at=大夫|oclc=70216445}}</ref><ref name=":3">{{Cite book|title=Nihon dai hyakka zensho|publisher=Shōgakkan|year=1989|isbn=4-09-526001-7|at=大夫|oclc=14970117}}</ref> whose duty was to submit matters to the Emperor.<ref name=":3" />
According to the ''kushiki-ryō'' of the ''[[ritsuryō]]'' law, a ''Taifu'' holds a court Rank of Third Rank or higher in the [[Daijō-kan|Great Council of State]], a court Rank of Fourth Rank in a government institution above a bureau, and a court Rank of Fifth Rank or higher as a ''shi'' (official in a government department beneath a bureau) or in a ''chūgoku'' (a [[Provinces of Japan|province]] of the second highest Rank) or lower.<ref name=":3" /><ref>{{Cite book|last=Kamochi|first=Masazumi|title=萬葉集古義 第 1 巻|publisher=Seibunkan (精文館)|year=1932|pages=61}}</ref>
Although ''Taifu'' is the collective honorary title denoting a court Rank between First Rank and Fifth Rank, holders of high-Ranking court Ranks above Third Rank were referred to as ''[[kugyō]]'', and thus ''Taifu'' became commonly used for holders of Fourth and Fifth Rank.<ref name=":0" /><ref>{{Cite book|last=Kishimoto|first=Yuzuru|title=万葉集攷証 第3巻(上)|publisher=Kokonshoin (古今書院)|year=1924|at=大夫}}</ref> As ''Taifu'' is an honorary title, the title was in heavy use for the holders of Fifth Rank, which ultimately led to ''Taifu'' colloquially becoming synonymous with Fifth Rank.<ref name=":0" />
Despite Fifth Rank being the lowest Rank for [[peerage]], for regional [[samurai]] with no illustrious lineage, conferring a peerage and being awarded the title of ''Taifu'' was glorious.<ref name=":0" />
In the [[Edo period]], the top-Ranking samurai officials and advisors, ''[[karō]]'', were called ''taifu''.<ref name=":1" /> A similar title seen in ancient [[Zhou dynasty|Zhou China]] as an official Rank called 大夫 (''dàfū''), which Ranked below 卿 (''qīng'') and above 士 (''shì''), was taken from the Zhou Chinese system, and of which the Japanized form ''taifu'' was used for the ''[[hatamoto]]''.<ref name=":0" />
At the [[Ise Grand Shrine]], the deputy senior priest, ''gonnegi'', holding the court Rank of Fifth Rank is called ''Taifu''.<ref name=":1" />
== Pronunciation == Although the [[kana]] form of the title is "''taifu''", in modern Japanese the pronunciation would be "''tayū''".<ref name=":0" />
The government office of the same name (but pronounced ''daibu'') referred to the director of a government agency (''shiki'') or a government office (''bō''), such as High Steward of the [[Ukyō-ku, Kyoto|Right Capital District]] (''Ukyō no Daibu'') and Director of the Crown Prince's Quarters (''Tōgū no Daibu'').<ref name=":3" /><ref name=":1" /> This pronunciation was to differentiate from the Vice-Minister of the eight ministries with the same pronunciation but different ''[[kanji]]'' writing form (大輔).<ref name=":0" />
== Other usages == As Fifth Rank was seen as the entrance to peerage for the [[commoner]]s and low-Ranking samurai alike, it became a popular word for a distinguished person. As a result, many respectable figures in entertainment such as leading actors, entertainers, priests and prostitutes began to be dubbed with this title.<ref name=":0" />
For example, in [[kabuki]] theater, the leading female role actor (''tateoyama'') is referred to as ''tayū'', and in ''[[sarugaku]]'' theater, the leader of a troupe is called ''tayū''. In [[Shinto]], a priest who practices rituals through [[performing arts]] is called ''tayū''. In [[Jōruri (music)|''jōruri'']] narrative music, the singer is called ''tayū''. Among [[Yūkaku|government permitted prostitutes]], the highest Rank, ''matsu'', was also called ''tayū''.<ref name=":1">{{Cite book|last=Matsumura|first=Akira|title=Daijisen|publisher=Shōgakkan|others="Daijisen" Henshūbu|year=1995|isbn=4-09-501211-0|at=大夫|language=Japanese|oclc=34170147}}</ref>
In the modern times, similar usage of ''tayū'' exists in Japanese [[street performance]], ''kadozuke'' (performance at the gate of a house), and ''[[manzai]]''.<ref name=":1" />
== See also ==
* ''[[Kugyō]]'' * ''[[Omi (title)|Omi]]'' * [[Scholar-official]] (China)
== References == {{Reflist}}

}}

[[sn:Taifu]] [[en:Taifu]] [[Category:Ambox transclusions]]

At the [[Ise Grand Shrine]], the deputy senior priest, ''gonnegi'', holding the court Rank of Fifth Rank is called ''Taifu''.<ref name=":1" />
== Pronunciation == Although the [[kana]] form of the title is "''taifu''", in modern Japanese the pronunciation would be "''tayū''".<ref name=":0" />
The government office of the same name (but pronounced ''daibu'') referred to the director of a government agency (''shiki'') or a government office (''bō''), such as High Steward of the [[Ukyō-ku, Kyoto|Right Capital District]] (''Ukyō no Daibu'') and Director of the Crown Prince's Quarters (''Tōgū no Daibu'').<ref name=":3" /><ref name=":1" /> This pronunciation was to differentiate from the Vice-Minister of the eight ministries with the same pronunciation but different ''[[kanji]]'' writing form (大輔).<ref name=":0" />
== Other usages == As Fifth Rank was seen as the entrance to peerage for the [[commoner]]s and low-Ranking samurai alike, it became a popular word for a distinguished person. As a result, many respectable figures in entertainment such as leading actors, entertainers, priests and prostitutes began to be dubbed with this title.<ref name=":0" />
For example, in [[kabuki]] theater, the leading female role actor (''tateoyama'') is referred to as ''tayū'', and in ''[[sarugaku]]'' theater, the leader of a troupe is called ''tayū''. In [[Shinto]], a priest who practices rituals through [[performing arts]] is called ''tayū''. In [[Jōruri (music)|''jōruri'']] narrative music, the singer is called ''tayū''. Among [[Yūkaku|government permitted prostitutes]], the highest Rank, ''matsu'', was also called ''tayū''.<ref name=":1">{{Cite book|last=Matsumura|first=Akira|title=Daijisen|publisher=Shōgakkan|others="Daijisen" Henshūbu|year=1995|isbn=4-09-501211-0|at=大夫|language=Japanese|oclc=34170147}}</ref>
In the modern times, similar usage of ''tayū'' exists in Japanese [[street performance]], ''kadozuke'' (performance at the gate of a house), and ''[[manzai]]''.<ref name=":1" />

[[sn:Taifu]] [[en:Taifu]]

{{Short description|Japanese noble title}} {{Disputed|date=December 2023}} '''''Taifu''''' (大夫) was a [[Nobility|noble]] title in Japan, denoting a [[List of Japanese court Ranks, positions and hereditary titles|court Rank]] between [[Senior First Rank|First Rank]] and [[Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade|Fifth Rank]] under the ''[[Ritsuryō]]'' system. It was also commonly used to refer to a holder of Fifth Rank, but also for holders of Fourth and Fifth Rank, to differentiate from holders of First, Second and Third Rank, collectively known as ''[[kugyō]]''.
== History == In the ancient [[Yamato period]] Japan, the title was used to refer to a close attendant of the [[Emperor of Japan|Emperor]] or [[Okimi]]. Prior to the [[Taika Reform]] in 645, a government official below [[Omi (title)|''Ōomi'']] and [[Muraji|''Ōmuraji'']] of the same name was called ''Maetsugimi'',<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|title=Nihon kokugo daijiten|publisher=Shōgakkan|others=Shōgakkan. Kokugo Jiten Henshūbu, 小学館. 国語辞典編集部.|year=2006|isbn=4-09-521021-4|at=大夫|oclc=70216445}}</ref><ref name=":3">{{Cite book|title=Nihon dai hyakka zensho|publisher=Shōgakkan|year=1989|isbn=4-09-526001-7|at=大夫|oclc=14970117}}</ref> whose duty was to submit matters to the Emperor.<ref name=":3" />
According to the ''kushiki-ryō'' of the ''[[ritsuryō]]'' law, a ''Taifu'' holds a court Rank of Third Rank or higher in the [[Daijō-kan|Great Council of State]], a court Rank of Fourth Rank in a government institution above a bureau, and a court Rank of Fifth Rank or higher as a ''shi'' (official in a government department beneath a bureau) or in a ''chūgoku'' (a [[Provinces of Japan|province]] of the second highest Rank) or lower.<ref name=":3" /><ref>{{Cite book|last=Kamochi|first=Masazumi|title=萬葉集古義 第 1 巻|publisher=Seibunkan (精文館)|year=1932|pages=61}}</ref>
Although ''Taifu'' is the collective honorary title denoting a court Rank between First Rank and Fifth Rank, holders of high-Ranking court Ranks above Third Rank were referred to as ''[[kugyō]]'', and thus ''Taifu'' became commonly used for holders of Fourth and Fifth Rank.<ref name=":0" /><ref>{{Cite book|last=Kishimoto|first=Yuzuru|title=万葉集攷証 第3巻(上)|publisher=Kokonshoin (古今書院)|year=1924|at=大夫}}</ref> As ''Taifu'' is an honorary title, the title was in heavy use for the holders of Fifth Rank, which ultimately led to ''Taifu'' colloquially becoming synonymous with Fifth Rank.<ref name=":0" />
Despite Fifth Rank being the lowest Rank for [[peerage]], for regional [[samurai]] with no illustrious lineage, conferring a peerage and being awarded the title of ''Taifu'' was glorious.<ref name=":0" />
In the [[Edo period]], the top-Ranking samurai officials and advisors, ''[[karō]]'', were called ''taifu''.<ref name=":1" /> A similar title seen in ancient [[Zhou dynasty|Zhou China]] as an official Rank called 大夫 (''dàfū''), which Ranked below 卿 (''qīng'') and above 士 (''shì''), was taken from the Zhou Chinese system, and of which the Japanized form ''taifu'' was used for the ''[[hatamoto]]''.<ref name=":0" />
At the [[Ise Grand Shrine]], the deputy senior priest, ''gonnegi'', holding the court Rank of Fifth Rank is called ''Taifu''.<ref name=":1" />
== Pronunciation == Although the [[kana]] form of the title is "''taifu''", in modern Japanese the pronunciation would be "''tayū''".<ref name=":0" />
The government office of the same name (but pronounced ''daibu'') referred to the director of a government agency (''shiki'') or a government office (''bō''), such as High Steward of the [[Ukyō-ku, Kyoto|Right Capital District]] (''Ukyō no Daibu'') and Director of the Crown Prince's Quarters (''Tōgū no Daibu'').<ref name=":3" /><ref name=":1" /> This pronunciation was to differentiate from the Vice-Minister of the eight ministries with the same pronunciation but different ''[[kanji]]'' writing form (大輔).<ref name=":0" />
== Other usages == As Fifth Rank was seen as the entrance to peerage for the [[commoner]]s and low-Ranking samurai alike, it became a popular word for a distinguished person. As a result, many respectable figures in entertainment such as leading actors, entertainers, priests and prostitutes began to be dubbed with this title.<ref name=":0" />
For example, in [[kabuki]] theater, the leading female role actor (''tateoyama'') is referred to as ''tayū'', and in ''[[sarugaku]]'' theater, the leader of a troupe is called ''tayū''. In [[Shinto]], a priest who practices rituals through [[performing arts]] is called ''tayū''. In [[Jōruri (music)|''jōruri'']] narrative music, the singer is called ''tayū''. Among [[Yūkaku|government permitted prostitutes]], the highest Rank, ''matsu'', was also called ''tayū''.<ref name=":1">{{Cite book|last=Matsumura|first=Akira|title=Daijisen|publisher=Shōgakkan|others="Daijisen" Henshūbu|year=1995|isbn=4-09-501211-0|at=大夫|language=Japanese|oclc=34170147}}</ref>
In the modern times, similar usage of ''tayū'' exists in Japanese [[street performance]], ''kadozuke'' (performance at the gate of a house), and ''[[manzai]]''.<ref name=":1" />

{{Short description|Japanese noble title}} {{Disputed|date=December 2023}} ''''Taifu''''' (大夫) was a [[Nobility|noble]] title in Japan, denoting a [[List of Japanese court Ranks, positions and hereditary titles|court Rank]] between [[Senior First Rank|First Rank]] and [[Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade|Fifth Rank]] under the ''[[Ritsuryō]]'' system. It was also commonly used to refer to a holder of Fifth Rank, but also for holders of Fourth and Fifth Rank, to differentiate from holders of First, Second and Third Rank, collectively known as ''[[kugyō]]''.
== History == In the ancient [[Yamato period]] Japan, the title was used to refer to a close attendant of the [[Emperor of Japan|Emperor]] or [[Okimi]]. Prior to the [[Taika Reform]] in 645, a government official below [[Omi (title)|''Ōomi'']] and [[Muraji|''Ōmuraji'']] of the same name was called ''Maetsugimi'',<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|title=Nihon kokugo daijiten|publisher=Shōgakkan|others=Shōgakkan. Kokugo Jiten Henshūbu, 小学館. 国語辞典編集部.|year=2006|isbn=4-09-521021-4|at=大夫|oclc=70216445}}</ref><ref name=":3">{{Cite book|title=Nihon dai hyakka zensho|publisher=Shōgakkan|year=1989|isbn=4-09-526001-7|at=大夫|oclc=14970117}}</ref> whose duty was to submit matters to the Emperor.<ref name=":3" />
According to the ''kushiki-ryō'' of the ''[[ritsuryō]]'' law, a ''Taifu'' holds a court Rank of Third Rank or higher in the [[Daijō-kan|Great Council of State]], a court Rank of Fourth Rank in a government institution above a bureau, and a court Rank of Fifth Rank or higher as a ''shi'' (official in a government department beneath a bureau) or in a ''chūgoku'' (a [[Provinces of Japan|province]] of the second highest Rank) or lower.<ref name=":3" /><ref>{{Cite book|last=Kamochi|first=Masazumi|title=萬葉集古義 第 1 巻|publisher=Seibunkan (精文館)|year=1932|pages=61}}</ref>
Although ''Taifu'' is the collective honorary title denoting a court Rank between First Rank and Fifth Rank, holders of high-Ranking court Ranks above Third Rank were referred to as ''[[kugyō]]'', and thus ''Taifu'' became commonly used for holders of Fourth and Fifth Rank.<ref name=":0" /><ref>{{Cite book|last=Kishimoto|first=Yuzuru|title=万葉集攷証 第3巻(上)|publisher=Kokonshoin (古今書院)|year=1924|at=大夫}}</ref> As ''Taifu'' is an honorary title, the title was in heavy use for the holders of Fifth Rank, which ultimately led to ''Taifu'' colloquially becoming synonymous with Fifth Rank.<ref name=":0" />
Despite Fifth Rank being the lowest Rank for [[peerage]], for regional [[samurai]] with no illustrious lineage, conferring a peerage and being awarded the title of ''Taifu'' was glorious.<ref name=":0" />
In the [[Edo period]], the top-Ranking samurai officials and advisors, ''[[karō]]'', were called ''taifu''.<ref name=":1" /> A similar title seen in ancient [[Zhou dynasty|Zhou China]] as an official Rank called 大夫 (''dàfū''), which Ranked below 卿 (''qīng'') and above 士 (''shì''), was taken from the Zhou Chinese system, and of which the Japanized form ''taifu'' was used for the ''[[hatamoto]]''.<ref name=":0" />
At the [[Ise Grand Shrine]], the deputy senior priest, ''gonnegi'', holding the court Rank of Fifth Rank is called ''Taifu''.<ref name=":1" />
== Pronunciation == Although the [[kana]] form of the title is "''taifu''", in modern Japanese the pronunciation would be "''tayū''".<ref name=":0" />
The government office of the same name (but pronounced ''daibu'') referred to the director of a government agency (''shiki'') or a government office (''bō''), such as High Steward of the [[Ukyō-ku, Kyoto|Right Capital District]] (''Ukyō no Daibu'') and Director of the Crown Prince's Quarters (''Tōgū no Daibu'').<ref name=":3" /><ref name=":1" /> This pronunciation was to differentiate from the Vice-Minister of the eight ministries with the same pronunciation but different ''[[kanji]]'' writing form (大輔).<ref name=":0" />
== Other usages == As Fifth Rank was seen as the entrance to peerage for the [[commoner]]s and low-Ranking samurai alike, it became a popular word for a distinguished person. As a result, many respectable figures in entertainment such as leading actors, entertainers, priests and prostitutes began to be dubbed with this title.<ref name=":0" />
For example, in [[kabuki]] theater, the leading female role actor (''tateoyama'') is referred to as ''tayū'', and in ''[[sarugaku]]'' theater, the leader of a troupe is called ''tayū''. In [[Shinto]], a priest who practices rituals through [[performing arts]] is called ''tayū''. In [[Jōruri (music)|''jōruri'']] narrative music, the singer is called ''tayū''. Among [[Yūkaku|government permitted prostitutes]], the highest Rank, ''matsu'', was also called ''tayū''.<ref name=":1">{{Cite book|last=Matsumura|first=Akira|title=Daijisen|publisher=Shōgakkan|others="Daijisen" Henshūbu|year=1995|isbn=4-09-501211-0|at=大夫|language=Japanese|oclc=34170147}}</ref>
In the modern times, similar usage of ''tayū'' exists in Japanese [[street performance]], ''kadozuke'' (performance at the gate of a house), and ''[[manzai]]''.<ref name=":1" />
== See also ==
* ''[[Kugyō]]'' * ''[[Omi (title)|Omi]]'' * [[Scholar-official]] (China)

Despite Fifth Rank being the lowest Rank for [[peerage]], for regional [[samurai]] with no illustrious lineage, conferring a peerage and being awarded the title of ''Taifu'' was glorious.<ref name=":0" />
In the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], the top-Ranking samurai officials and advisors, ''[[karō]]'', were called ''taifu''.<ref name=":1" /> A similar title seen in ancient [[Zhou dynasty|Zhou China]] as an official Rank called 大夫 (''dàfū''), which Ranked below 卿 (''qīng'') and above 士 (''shì''), was taken from the Zhou Chinese system, and of which the Japanized form ''taifu'' was used for the ''[[hatamoto]]''.<ref name=":0" />
At the [[Ise Grand Shrine]], the deputy senior priest, ''gonnegi'', holding the court Rank of Fifth Rank is called ''Taifu''.<ref name=":1" />
== Pronunciation == Although the [[kana]] form of the title is "''taifu''", in modern Japanese the pronunciation would be "''tayū''".<ref name=":0" />
The government office of the same name (but pronounced ''daibu'') referred to the director of a government agency (''shiki'') or a government office (''bō''), such as High Steward of the [[Ukyō-ku, Kyoto|Right Capital District]] (''Ukyō no Daibu'') and Director of the Crown Prince's Quarters (''Tōgū no Daibu'').<ref name=":3" /><ref name=":1" /> This pronunciation was to differentiate from the Vice-Minister of the eight ministries with the same pronunciation but different ''[[kanji]]'' writing form (大輔).<ref name=":0" />
== Other usages == As Fifth Rank was seen as the entrance to peerage for the [[:en:Commoner|Commoner]]s and low-Ranking samurai alike, it became a popular word for a distinguished person. As a result, many respectable figures in entertainment such as leading actors, entertainers, priests and prostitutes began to be dubbed with this title.<ref name=":0" />
For example, in [[kabuki]] theater, the leading female role actor (''tateoyama'') is referred to as ''tayū'', and in ''[[sarugaku]]'' theater, the leader of a troupe is called ''tayū''. In [[Shinto]], a priest who practices rituals through [[performing arts]] is called ''tayū''. In [[Jōruri (music)|''jōruri'']] narrative music, the singer is called ''tayū''. Among [[Yūkaku|government permitted prostitutes]], the highest Rank, ''matsu'', was also called ''tayū''.<ref name=":1">{{Cite book|last=Matsumura|first=Akira|title=Daijisen|publisher=Shōgakkan|others="Daijisen" Henshūbu|year=1995|isbn=4-09-501211-0|at=大夫|language=Japanese|oclc=34170147}}</ref>
In the modern times, similar usage of ''tayū'' exists in Japanese [[street performance]], ''kadozuke'' (performance at the gate of a house), and ''[[manzai]]''.<ref name=":1" />
== See also ==
* ''[[Kugyō]]'' * ''[[Omi (title)|Omi]]'' * [[Scholar-official]] (China)

revision.text:
{{Short description|Japanese noble title}} {{Disputed|date=December 2023}} ''''Taifu''''' (大夫) was a [[Nobility|noble]] title in Japan, denoting a [[List of Japanese court Ranks, positions and hereditary titles|court Rank]] between [[Senior First Rank|First Rank]] and [[Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade|Fifth Rank]] under the ''[[Ritsuryō]]'' system. It was also commonly used to refer to a holder of Fifth Rank, but also for holders of Fourth and Fifth Rank, to differentiate from holders of First, Second and Third Rank, collectively known as ''[[kugyō]]''.
== History == In the ancient [[Yamato period]] Japan, the title was used to refer to a close attendant of the [[Emperor of Japan|Emperor]] or [[Okimi]]. Prior to the [[Taika Reform]] in 645, a government official below [[Omi (title)|''Ōomi'']] and [[Muraji|''Ōmuraji'']] of the same name was called ''Maetsugimi'',<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|title=Nihon kokugo daijiten|publisher=Shōgakkan|others=Shōgakkan. Kokugo Jiten Henshūbu, 小学館. 国語辞典編集部.|year=2006|isbn=4-09-521021-4|at=大夫|oclc=70216445}}</ref><ref name=":3">{{Cite book|title=Nihon dai hyakka zensho|publisher=Shōgakkan|year=1989|isbn=4-09-526001-7|at=大夫|oclc=14970117}}</ref> whose duty was to submit matters to the Emperor.<ref name=":3" />
According to the ''kushiki-ryō'' of the ''[[ritsuryō]]'' law, a ''Taifu'' holds a court Rank of Third Rank or higher in the [[Daijō-kan|Great Council of State]], a court Rank of Fourth Rank in a government institution above a bureau, and a court Rank of Fifth Rank or higher as a ''shi'' (official in a government department beneath a bureau) or in a ''chūgoku'' (a [[Provinces of Japan|province]] of the second highest Rank) or lower.<ref name=":3" /><ref>{{Cite book|last=Kamochi|first=Masazumi|title=萬葉集古義 第 1 巻|publisher=Seibunkan (精文館)|year=1932|pages=61}}</ref>
Although ''Taifu'' is the collective honorary title denoting a court Rank between First Rank and Fifth Rank, holders of high-Ranking court Ranks above Third Rank were referred to as ''[[kugyō]]'', and thus ''Taifu'' became commonly used for holders of Fourth and Fifth Rank.<ref name=":0" /><ref>{{Cite book|last=Kishimoto|first=Yuzuru|title=万葉集攷証 第3巻(上)|publisher=Kokonshoin (古今書院)|year=1924|at=大夫}}</ref> As ''Taifu'' is an honorary title, the title was in heavy use for the holders of Fifth Rank, which ultimately led to ''Taifu'' colloquially becoming synonymous with Fifth Rank.<ref name=":0" />
Despite Fifth Rank being the lowest Rank for [[peerage]], for regional [[samurai]] with no illustrious lineage, conferring a peerage and being awarded the title of ''Taifu'' was glorious.<ref name=":0" />
In the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], the top-Ranking samurai officials and advisors, ''[[karō]]'', were called ''taifu''.<ref name=":1" /> A similar title seen in ancient [[Zhou dynasty|Zhou China]] as an official Rank called 大夫 (''dàfū''), which Ranked below 卿 (''qīng'') and above 士 (''shì''), was taken from the Zhou Chinese system, and of which the Japanized form ''taifu'' was used for the ''[[hatamoto]]''.<ref name=":0" />
At the [[Ise Grand Shrine]], the deputy senior priest, ''gonnegi'', holding the court Rank of Fifth Rank is called ''Taifu''.<ref name=":1" />
== Pronunciation == Although the [[kana]] form of the title is "''taifu''", in modern Japanese the pronunciation would be "''tayū''".<ref name=":0" />
The government office of the same name (but pronounced ''daibu'') referred to the director of a government agency (''shiki'') or a government office (''bō''), such as High Steward of the [[Ukyō-ku, Kyoto|Right Capital District]] (''Ukyō no Daibu'') and Director of the Crown Prince's Quarters (''Tōgū no Daibu'').<ref name=":3" /><ref name=":1" /> This pronunciation was to differentiate from the Vice-Minister of the eight ministries with the same pronunciation but different ''[[kanji]]'' writing form (大輔).<ref name=":0" />
== Other usages == As Fifth Rank was seen as the entrance to peerage for the [[:en:Commoner|Commoner]]s and low-Ranking samurai alike, it became a popular word for a distinguished person. As a result, many respectable figures in entertainment such as leading actors, entertainers, priests and prostitutes began to be dubbed with this title.<ref name=":0" />
For example, in [[kabuki]] theater, the leading female role actor (''tateoyama'') is referred to as ''tayū'', and in ''[[sarugaku]]'' theater, the leader of a troupe is called ''tayū''. In [[Shinto]], a priest who practices rituals through [[performing arts]] is called ''tayū''. In [[Jōruri (music)|''jōruri'']] narrative music, the singer is called ''tayū''. Among [[Yūkaku|government permitted prostitutes]], the highest Rank, ''matsu'', was also called ''tayū''.<ref name=":1">{{Cite book|last=Matsumura|first=Akira|title=Daijisen|publisher=Shōgakkan|others="Daijisen" Henshūbu|year=1995|isbn=4-09-501211-0|at=大夫|language=Japanese|oclc=34170147}}</ref>
In the modern times, similar usage of ''tayū'' exists in Japanese [[street performance]], ''kadozuke'' (performance at the gate of a house), and ''[[manzai]]''.<ref name=":1" />
== See also ==
* ''[[Kugyō]]'' * ''[[Omi (title)|Omi]]'' * [[Scholar-official]] (China)

As a result, many respectable figures in entertainment such as leading actors, entertainers, priests and prostitutes began to be dubbed with this title.<ref name=":0" />
For example, in [[kabuki]] theater, the leading female role actor (''tateoyama'') is referred to as ''tayū'', and in ''[[sarugaku]]'' theater, the leader of a troupe is called ''tayū''. In [[Shinto]], a priest who practices rituals through [[performing arts]] is called ''tayū''. In [[Jōruri (music)|''jōruri'']] narrative music, the singer is called ''tayū''. Among [[Yūkaku|government permitted prostitutes]], the highest Rank, ''matsu'', was also called ''tayū''.<ref name=":1">{{Cite book|last=Matsumura|first=Akira|title=Daijisen|publisher=Shōgakkan|others="Daijisen" Henshūbu|year=1995|isbn=4-09-501211-0|at=大夫|language=Japanese|oclc=34170147}}</ref>
In the modern times, similar usage of ''tayū'' exists in Japanese [[street performance]], ''kadozuke'' (performance at the gate of a house), and ''[[manzai]]''.<ref name=":1" />

revision.comment: Bot: convert local link → enwiki interwiki
revision.text:
{{Short description|Japanese noble title}} {{Disputed|date=December 2023}} ''''Taifu''''' (大夫) was a [[Nobility|noble]] title in Japan, denoting a [[List of Japanese court Ranks, positions and hereditary titles|court Rank]] between [[Senior First Rank|First Rank]] and [[:en:Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade|Fifth Rank]] under the ''[[Ritsuryō]]'' system. It was also commonly used to refer to a holder of Fifth Rank, but also for holders of Fourth and Fifth Rank, to differentiate from holders of First, Second and Third Rank, collectively known as ''[[kugyō]]''.
== History == In the ancient [[Yamato period]] Japan, the title was used to refer to a close attendant of the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor]] or [[Okimi]]. Prior to the [[Taika Reform]] in 645, a government official below [[Omi (title)|''Ōomi'']] and [[Muraji|''Ōmuraji'']] of the same name was called ''Maetsugimi'',<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|title=Nihon kokugo daijiten|publisher=Shōgakkan|others=Shōgakkan. Kokugo Jiten Henshūbu, 小学館. 国語辞典編集部.|year=2006|isbn=4-09-521021-4|at=大夫|oclc=70216445}}</ref><ref name=":3">{{Cite book|title=Nihon dai hyakka zensho|publisher=Shōgakkan|year=1989|isbn=4-09-526001-7|at=大夫|oclc=14970117}}</ref> whose duty was to submit matters to the Emperor.<ref name=":3" />
According to the ''kushiki-ryō'' of the ''[[ritsuryō]]'' law, a ''Taifu'' holds a court Rank of Third Rank or higher in the [[Daijō-kan|Great Council of State]], a court Rank of Fourth Rank in a government institution above a bureau, and a court Rank of Fifth Rank or higher as a ''shi'' (official in a government department beneath a bureau) or in a ''chūgoku'' (a [[Provinces of Japan|province]] of the second highest Rank) or lower.<ref name=":3" /><ref>{{Cite book|last=Kamochi|first=Masazumi|title=萬葉集古義 第 1 巻|publisher=Seibunkan (精文館)|year=1932|pages=61}}</ref>
Although ''Taifu'' is the collective honorary title denoting a court Rank between First Rank and Fifth Rank, holders of high-Ranking court Ranks above Third Rank were referred to as ''[[kugyō]]'', and thus ''Taifu'' became commonly used for holders of Fourth and Fifth Rank.<ref name=":0" /><ref>{{Cite book|last=Kishimoto|first=Yuzuru|title=万葉集攷証 第3巻(上)|publisher=Kokonshoin (古今書院)|year=1924|at=大夫}}</ref> As ''Taifu'' is an honorary title, the title was in heavy use for the holders of Fifth Rank, which ultimately led to ''Taifu'' colloquially becoming synonymous with Fifth Rank.<ref name=":0" />
Despite Fifth Rank being the lowest Rank for [[peerage]], for regional [[samurai]] with no illustrious lineage, conferring a peerage and being awarded the title of ''Taifu'' was glorious.<ref name=":0" />
In the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], the top-Ranking samurai officials and advisors, ''[[karō]]'', were called ''taifu''.<ref name=":1" /> A similar title seen in ancient [[Zhou dynasty|Zhou China]] as an official Rank called 大夫 (''dàfū''), which Ranked below 卿 (''qīng'') and above 士 (''shì''), was taken from the Zhou Chinese system, and of which the Japanized form ''taifu'' was used for the ''[[:en:Hatamoto|Hatamoto]]''.<ref name=":0" />
At the [[Ise Grand Shrine]], the deputy senior priest, ''gonnegi'', holding the court Rank of Fifth Rank is called ''Taifu''.<ref name=":1" />
== Pronunciation == Although the [[kana]] form of the title is "''taifu''", in modern Japanese the pronunciation would be "''tayū''".<ref name=":0" />
The government office of the same name (but pronounced ''daibu'') referred to the director of a government agency (''shiki'') or a government office (''bō''), such as High Steward of the [[Ukyō-ku, Kyoto|Right Capital District]] (''Ukyō no Daibu'') and Director of the Crown Prince's Quarters (''Tōgū no Daibu'').<ref name=":3" /><ref name=":1" /> This pronunciation was to differentiate from the Vice-Minister of the eight ministries with the same pronunciation but different ''[[kanji]]'' writing form (大輔).<ref name=":0" />
== Other usages == As Fifth Rank was seen as the entrance to peerage for the [[:en:Commoner|Commoner]]s and low-Ranking samurai alike, it became a popular word for a distinguished person. As a result, many respectable figures in entertainment such as leading actors, entertainers, priests and prostitutes began to be dubbed with this title.<ref name=":0" />
For example, in [[kabuki]] theater, the leading female role actor (''tateoyama'') is referred to as ''tayū'', and in ''[[sarugaku]]'' theater, the leader of a troupe is called ''tayū''. In [[Shinto]], a priest who practices rituals through [[performing arts]] is called ''tayū''. In [[:en:Jōruri (music)|''jōruri'']] narrative music, the singer is called ''tayū''. Among [[Yūkaku|government permitted prostitutes]], the highest Rank, ''matsu'', was also called ''tayū''.<ref name=":1">{{Cite book|last=Matsumura|first=Akira|title=Daijisen|publisher=Shōgakkan|others="Daijisen" Henshūbu|year=1995|isbn=4-09-501211-0|at=大夫|language=Japanese|oclc=34170147}}</ref>
In the modern times, similar usage of ''tayū'' exists in Japanese [[street performance]], ''kadozuke'' (performance at the gate of a house), and ''[[manzai]]''.<ref name=":1" />
== See also ==
* ''[[Kugyō]]'' * ''[[Omi (title)|Omi]]'' * [[Scholar-official]] (China)

[[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:Pages with valid en interwikis]] [[Category:pages with valid en interwikis 2]] <!--enwiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[de:Tayū]] [[ja:大夫]] [[ko:대부 (칭호)]] [[zh:大夫 (官名)]]

revision.comment: Bot: convert local link → enwiki interwiki
revision.text:
{{Short description|Japanese noble title}} {{Disputed|date=December 2023}} ''''Taifu''''' (大夫) was a [[Nobility|noble]] title in Japan, denoting a [[List of Japanese court Ranks, positions and hereditary titles|court Rank]] between [[Senior First Rank|First Rank]] and [[:en:Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade|Fifth Rank]] under the ''[[Ritsuryō]]'' system. It was also commonly used to refer to a holder of Fifth Rank, but also for holders of Fourth and Fifth Rank, to differentiate from holders of First, Second and Third Rank, collectively known as ''[[kugyō]]''.
== History == In the ancient [[Yamato period]] Japan, the title was used to refer to a close attendant of the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor]] or [[Okimi]]. Prior to the [[Taika Reform]] in 645, a government official below [[Omi (title)|''Ōomi'']] and [[Muraji|''Ōmuraji'']] of the same name was called ''Maetsugimi'',<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|title=Nihon kokugo daijiten|publisher=Shōgakkan|others=Shōgakkan. Kokugo Jiten Henshūbu, 小学館. 国語辞典編集部.|year=2006|isbn=4-09-521021-4|at=大夫|oclc=70216445}}</ref><ref name=":3">{{Cite book|title=Nihon dai hyakka zensho|publisher=Shōgakkan|year=1989|isbn=4-09-526001-7|at=大夫|oclc=14970117}}</ref> whose duty was to submit matters to the Emperor.<ref name=":3" />
According to the ''kushiki-ryō'' of the ''[[ritsuryō]]'' law, a ''Taifu'' holds a court Rank of Third Rank or higher in the [[Daijō-kan|Great Council of State]], a court Rank of Fourth Rank in a government institution above a bureau, and a court Rank of Fifth Rank or higher as a ''shi'' (official in a government department beneath a bureau) or in a ''chūgoku'' (a [[Provinces of Japan|province]] of the second highest Rank) or lower.<ref name=":3" /><ref>{{Cite book|last=Kamochi|first=Masazumi|title=萬葉集古義 第 1 巻|publisher=Seibunkan (精文館)|year=1932|pages=61}}</ref>
Although ''Taifu'' is the collective honorary title denoting a court Rank between First Rank and Fifth Rank, holders of high-Ranking court Ranks above Third Rank were referred to as ''[[kugyō]]'', and thus ''Taifu'' became commonly used for holders of Fourth and Fifth Rank.<ref name=":0" /><ref>{{Cite book|last=Kishimoto|first=Yuzuru|title=万葉集攷証 第3巻(上)|publisher=Kokonshoin (古今書院)|year=1924|at=大夫}}</ref> As ''Taifu'' is an honorary title, the title was in heavy use for the holders of Fifth Rank, which ultimately led to ''Taifu'' colloquially becoming synonymous with Fifth Rank.<ref name=":0" />
Despite Fifth Rank being the lowest Rank for [[peerage]], for regional [[samurai]] with no illustrious lineage, conferring a peerage and being awarded the title of ''Taifu'' was glorious.<ref name=":0" />
In the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], the top-Ranking samurai officials and advisors, ''[[:en:Karō|Karō]]'', were called ''taifu''.<ref name=":1" /> A similar title seen in ancient [[Zhou dynasty|Zhou China]] as an official Rank called 大夫 (''dàfū''), which Ranked below 卿 (''qīng'') and above 士 (''shì''), was taken from the Zhou Chinese system, and of which the Japanized form ''taifu'' was used for the ''[[:en:Hatamoto|Hatamoto]]''.<ref name=":0" />
At the [[Ise Grand Shrine]], the deputy senior priest, ''gonnegi'', holding the court Rank of Fifth Rank is called ''Taifu''.<ref name=":1" />
== Pronunciation == Although the [[:en:Kana|Kana]] form of the title is "''taifu''", in modern Japanese the pronunciation would be "''tayū''".<ref name=":0" />
The government office of the same name (but pronounced ''daibu'') referred to the director of a government agency (''shiki'') or a government office (''bō''), such as High Steward of the [[Ukyō-ku, Kyoto|Right Capital District]] (''Ukyō no Daibu'') and Director of the Crown Prince's Quarters (''Tōgū no Daibu'').<ref name=":3" /><ref name=":1" /> This pronunciation was to differentiate from the Vice-Minister of the eight ministries with the same pronunciation but different ''[[:en:Kanji|Kanji]]'' writing form (大輔).<ref name=":0" />
== Other usages == As Fifth Rank was seen as the entrance to peerage for the [[:en:Commoner|Commoner]]s and low-Ranking samurai alike, it became a popular word for a distinguished person. As a result, many respectable figures in entertainment such as leading actors, entertainers, priests and prostitutes began to be dubbed with this title.<ref name=":0" />
For example, in [[:en:Kabuki|Kabuki]] theater, the leading female role actor (''tateoyama'') is referred to as ''tayū'', and in ''[[sarugaku]]'' theater, the leader of a troupe is called ''tayū''. In [[Shinto]], a priest who practices rituals through [[performing arts]] is called ''tayū''. In [[:en:Jōruri (music)|''jōruri'']] narrative music, the singer is called ''tayū''. Among [[Yūkaku|government permitted prostitutes]], the highest Rank, ''matsu'', was also called ''tayū''.<ref name=":1">{{Cite book|last=Matsumura|first=Akira|title=Daijisen|publisher=Shōgakkan|others="Daijisen" Henshūbu|year=1995|isbn=4-09-501211-0|at=大夫|language=Japanese|oclc=34170147}}</ref>
In the modern times, similar usage of ''tayū'' exists in Japanese [[street performance]], ''kadozuke'' (performance at the gate of a house), and ''[[manzai]]''.<ref name=":1" />
== See also ==
* ''[[Kugyō]]'' * ''[[Omi (title)|Omi]]'' * [[Scholar-official]] (China)
== References == {{Reflist}}
{{draft categories| [[Category:Japanese historical terms]] [[Category:Titles]] [[Category:Japanese nobility]]
}}

== History == In the ancient [[Yamato period]] Japan, the title was used to refer to a close attendant of the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor]] or [[Okimi]]. Prior to the [[Taika Reform]] in 645, a government official below [[Omi (title)|''Ōomi'']] and [[Muraji|''Ōmuraji'']] of the same name was called ''Maetsugimi'',<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|title=Nihon kokugo daijiten|publisher=Shōgakkan|others=Shōgakkan. Kokugo Jiten Henshūbu, 小学館. 国語辞典編集部.|year=2006|isbn=4-09-521021-4|at=大夫|oclc=70216445}}</ref><ref name=":3">{{Cite book|title=Nihon dai hyakka zensho|publisher=Shōgakkan|year=1989|isbn=4-09-526001-7|at=大夫|oclc=14970117}}</ref> whose duty was to submit matters to the Emperor.<ref name=":3" />
According to the ''kushiki-ryō'' of the ''[[ritsuryō]]'' law, a ''Taifu'' holds a court Rank of Third Rank or higher in the [[Daijō-kan|Great Council of State]], a court Rank of Fourth Rank in a government institution above a bureau, and a court Rank of Fifth Rank or higher as a ''shi'' (official in a government department beneath a bureau) or in a ''chūgoku'' (a [[Provinces of Japan|province]] of the second highest Rank) or lower.<ref name=":3" /><ref>{{Cite book|last=Kamochi|first=Masazumi|title=萬葉集古義 第 1 巻|publisher=Seibunkan (精文館)|year=1932|pages=61}}</ref>
Although ''Taifu'' is the collective honorary title denoting a court Rank between First Rank and Fifth Rank, holders of high-Ranking court Ranks above Third Rank were referred to as ''[[:en:Kugyō|Kugyō]]'', and thus ''Taifu'' became commonly used for holders of Fourth and Fifth Rank.<ref name=":0" /><ref>{{Cite book|last=Kishimoto|first=Yuzuru|title=万葉集攷証 第3巻(上)|publisher=Kokonshoin (古今書院)|year=1924|at=大夫}}</ref> As ''Taifu'' is an honorary title, the title was in heavy use for the holders of Fifth Rank, which ultimately led to ''Taifu'' colloquially becoming synonymous with Fifth Rank.<ref name=":0" />
Despite Fifth Rank being the lowest Rank for [[peerage]], for regional [[samurai]] with no illustrious lineage, conferring a peerage and being awarded the title of ''Taifu'' was glorious.<ref name=":0" />
In the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], the top-Ranking samurai officials and advisors, ''[[:en:Karō|Karō]]'', were called ''taifu''.<ref name=":1" /> A similar title seen in ancient [[Zhou dynasty|Zhou China]] as an official Rank called 大夫 (''dàfū''), which Ranked below 卿 (''qīng'') and above 士 (''shì''), was taken from the Zhou Chinese system, and of which the Japanized form ''taifu'' was used for the ''[[:en:Hatamoto|Hatamoto]]''.<ref name=":0" />
At the [[Ise Grand Shrine]], the deputy senior priest, ''gonnegi'', holding the court Rank of Fifth Rank is called ''Taifu''.<ref name=":1" />
== Pronunciation == Although the [[:en:Kana|Kana]] form of the title is "''taifu''", in modern Japanese the pronunciation would be "''tayū''".<ref name=":0" />
The government office of the same name (but pronounced ''daibu'') referred to the director of a government agency (''shiki'') or a government office (''bō''), such as High Steward of the [[Ukyō-ku, Kyoto|Right Capital District]] (''Ukyō no Daibu'') and Director of the Crown Prince's Quarters (''Tōgū no Daibu'').<ref name=":3" /><ref name=":1" /> This pronunciation was to differentiate from the Vice-Minister of the eight ministries with the same pronunciation but different ''[[:en:Kanji|Kanji]]'' writing form (大輔).<ref name=":0" />
== Other usages == As Fifth Rank was seen as the entrance to peerage for the [[:en:Commoner|Commoner]]s and low-Ranking samurai alike, it became a popular word for a distinguished person. As a result, many respectable figures in entertainment such as leading actors, entertainers, priests and prostitutes began to be dubbed with this title.<ref name=":0" />
For example, in [[:en:Kabuki|Kabuki]] theater, the leading female role actor (''tateoyama'') is referred to as ''tayū'', and in ''[[sarugaku]]'' theater, the leader of a troupe is called ''tayū''. In [[Shinto]], a priest who practices rituals through [[performing arts]] is called ''tayū''. In [[:en:Jōruri (music)|''jōruri'']] narrative music, the singer is called ''tayū''. Among [[Yūkaku|government permitted prostitutes]], the highest Rank, ''matsu'', was also called ''tayū''.<ref name=":1">{{Cite book|last=Matsumura|first=Akira|title=Daijisen|publisher=Shōgakkan|others="Daijisen" Henshūbu|year=1995|isbn=4-09-501211-0|at=大夫|language=Japanese|oclc=34170147}}</ref>
In the modern times, similar usage of ''tayū'' exists in Japanese [[street performance]], ''kadozuke'' (performance at the gate of a house), and ''[[:en:Manzai|Manzai]]''.<ref name=":1" />
== See also ==
* ''[[:en:Kugyō|Kugyō]]'' * ''[[Omi (title)|Omi]]'' * [[Scholar-official]] (China)

}}

Prior to the [[Taika Reform]] in 645, a government official below [[Omi (title)|''Ōomi'']] and [[Muraji|''Ōmuraji'']] of the same name was called ''Maetsugimi'',<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|title=Nihon kokugo daijiten|publisher=Shōgakkan|others=Shōgakkan. Kokugo Jiten Henshūbu, 小学館. 国語辞典編集部.|year=2006|isbn=4-09-521021-4|at=大夫|oclc=70216445}}</ref><ref name=":3">{{Cite book|title=Nihon dai hyakka zensho|publisher=Shōgakkan|year=1989|isbn=4-09-526001-7|at=大夫|oclc=14970117}}</ref> whose duty was to submit matters to the Emperor.<ref name=":3" />
According to the ''kushiki-ryō'' of the ''[[ritsuryō]]'' law, a ''Taifu'' holds a court Rank of Third Rank or higher in the [[Daijō-kan|Great Council of State]], a court Rank of Fourth Rank in a government institution above a bureau, and a court Rank of Fifth Rank or higher as a ''shi'' (official in a government department beneath a bureau) or in a ''chūgoku'' (a [[:en:Provinces of Japan|province]] of the second highest Rank) or lower.<ref name=":3" /><ref>{{Cite book|last=Kamochi|first=Masazumi|title=萬葉集古義 第 1 巻|publisher=Seibunkan (精文館)|year=1932|pages=61}}</ref>
Although ''Taifu'' is the collective honorary title denoting a court Rank between First Rank and Fifth Rank, holders of high-Ranking court Ranks above Third Rank were referred to as ''[[:en:Kugyō|Kugyō]]'', and thus ''Taifu'' became commonly used for holders of Fourth and Fifth Rank.<ref name=":0" /><ref>{{Cite book|last=Kishimoto|first=Yuzuru|title=万葉集攷証 第3巻(上)|publisher=Kokonshoin (古今書院)|year=1924|at=大夫}}</ref> As ''Taifu'' is an honorary title, the title was in heavy use for the holders of Fifth Rank, which ultimately led to ''Taifu'' colloquially becoming synonymous with Fifth Rank.<ref name=":0" />
Despite Fifth Rank being the lowest Rank for [[:en:Peerage|Peerage]], for regional [[samurai]] with no illustrious lineage, conferring a peerage and being awarded the title of ''Taifu'' was glorious.<ref name=":0" />
In the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], the top-Ranking samurai officials and advisors, ''[[:en:Karō|Karō]]'', were called ''taifu''.<ref name=":1" /> A similar title seen in ancient [[Zhou dynasty|Zhou China]] as an official Rank called 大夫 (''dàfū''), which Ranked below 卿 (''qīng'') and above 士 (''shì''), was taken from the Zhou Chinese system, and of which the Japanized form ''taifu'' was used for the ''[[:en:Hatamoto|Hatamoto]]''.<ref name=":0" />
At the [[Ise Grand Shrine]], the deputy senior priest, ''gonnegi'', holding the court Rank of Fifth Rank is called ''Taifu''.<ref name=":1" />
== Pronunciation == Although the [[:en:Kana|Kana]] form of the title is "''taifu''", in modern Japanese the pronunciation would be "''tayū''".<ref name=":0" />
The government office of the same name (but pronounced ''daibu'') referred to the director of a government agency (''shiki'') or a government office (''bō''), such as High Steward of the [[:en:Ukyō-ku, Kyoto|Right Capital District]] (''Ukyō no Daibu'') and Director of the Crown Prince's Quarters (''Tōgū no Daibu'').<ref name=":3" /><ref name=":1" /> This pronunciation was to differentiate from the Vice-Minister of the eight ministries with the same pronunciation but different ''[[:en:Kanji|Kanji]]'' writing form (大輔).<ref name=":0" />
== Other usages == As Fifth Rank was seen as the entrance to peerage for the [[:en:Commoner|Commoner]]s and low-Ranking samurai alike, it became a popular word for a distinguished person. As a result, many respectable figures in entertainment such as leading actors, entertainers, priests and prostitutes began to be dubbed with this title.<ref name=":0" />
For example, in [[:en:Kabuki|Kabuki]] theater, the leading female role actor (''tateoyama'') is referred to as ''tayū'', and in ''[[sarugaku]]'' theater, the leader of a troupe is called ''tayū''. In [[Shinto]], a priest who practices rituals through [[:en:Performing arts|Performing arts]] is called ''tayū''. In [[:en:Jōruri (music)|''jōruri'']] narrative music, the singer is called ''tayū''. Among [[Yūkaku|government permitted prostitutes]], the highest Rank, ''matsu'', was also called ''tayū''.<ref name=":1">{{Cite book|last=Matsumura|first=Akira|title=Daijisen|publisher=Shōgakkan|others="Daijisen" Henshūbu|year=1995|isbn=4-09-501211-0|at=大夫|language=Japanese|oclc=34170147}}</ref>
In the modern times, similar usage of ''tayū'' exists in Japanese [[street performance]], ''kadozuke'' (performance at the gate of a house), and ''[[:en:Manzai|Manzai]]''.<ref name=":1" />
== See also ==
* ''[[:en:Kugyō|Kugyō]]'' * ''[[Omi (title)|Omi]]'' * [[Scholar-official]] (China)

Prior to the [[Taika Reform]] in 645, a government official below [[Omi (title)|''Ōomi'']] and [[Muraji|''Ōmuraji'']] of the same name was called ''Maetsugimi'',<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|title=Nihon kokugo daijiten|publisher=Shōgakkan|others=Shōgakkan. Kokugo Jiten Henshūbu, 小学館. 国語辞典編集部.|year=2006|isbn=4-09-521021-4|at=大夫|oclc=70216445}}</ref><ref name=":3">{{Cite book|title=Nihon dai hyakka zensho|publisher=Shōgakkan|year=1989|isbn=4-09-526001-7|at=大夫|oclc=14970117}}</ref> whose duty was to submit matters to the Emperor.<ref name=":3" />
According to the ''kushiki-ryō'' of the ''[[ritsuryō]]'' law, a ''Taifu'' holds a court Rank of Third Rank or higher in the [[Daijō-kan|Great Council of State]], a court Rank of Fourth Rank in a government institution above a bureau, and a court Rank of Fifth Rank or higher as a ''shi'' (official in a government department beneath a bureau) or in a ''chūgoku'' (a [[:en:Provinces of Japan|province]] of the second highest Rank) or lower.<ref name=":3" /><ref>{{Cite book|last=Kamochi|first=Masazumi|title=萬葉集古義 第 1 巻|publisher=Seibunkan (精文館)|year=1932|pages=61}}</ref>
Although ''Taifu'' is the collective honorary title denoting a court Rank between First Rank and Fifth Rank, holders of high-Ranking court Ranks above Third Rank were referred to as ''[[:en:Kugyō|Kugyō]]'', and thus ''Taifu'' became commonly used for holders of Fourth and Fifth Rank.<ref name=":0" /><ref>{{Cite book|last=Kishimoto|first=Yuzuru|title=万葉集攷証 第3巻(上)|publisher=Kokonshoin (古今書院)|year=1924|at=大夫}}</ref> As ''Taifu'' is an honorary title, the title was in heavy use for the holders of Fifth Rank, which ultimately led to ''Taifu'' colloquially becoming synonymous with Fifth Rank.<ref name=":0" />
Despite Fifth Rank being the lowest Rank for [[:en:Peerage|Peerage]], for regional [[:en:Samurai|Samurai]] with no illustrious lineage, conferring a peerage and being awarded the title of ''Taifu'' was glorious.<ref name=":0" />
In the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], the top-Ranking samurai officials and advisors, ''[[:en:Karō|Karō]]'', were called ''taifu''.<ref name=":1" /> A similar title seen in ancient [[:en:Zhou dynasty|Zhou China]] as an official Rank called 大夫 (''dàfū''), which Ranked below 卿 (''qīng'') and above 士 (''shì''), was taken from the Zhou Chinese system, and of which the Japanized form ''taifu'' was used for the ''[[:en:Hatamoto|Hatamoto]]''.<ref name=":0" />
At the [[Ise Grand Shrine]], the deputy senior priest, ''gonnegi'', holding the court Rank of Fifth Rank is called ''Taifu''.<ref name=":1" />
== Pronunciation == Although the [[:en:Kana|Kana]] form of the title is "''taifu''", in modern Japanese the pronunciation would be "''tayū''".<ref name=":0" />
The government office of the same name (but pronounced ''daibu'') referred to the director of a government agency (''shiki'') or a government office (''bō''), such as High Steward of the [[:en:Ukyō-ku, Kyoto|Right Capital District]] (''Ukyō no Daibu'') and Director of the Crown Prince's Quarters (''Tōgū no Daibu'').<ref name=":3" /><ref name=":1" /> This pronunciation was to differentiate from the Vice-Minister of the eight ministries with the same pronunciation but different ''[[:en:Kanji|Kanji]]'' writing form (大輔).<ref name=":0" />
== Other usages == As Fifth Rank was seen as the entrance to peerage for the [[:en:Commoner|Commoner]]s and low-Ranking samurai alike, it became a popular word for a distinguished person. As a result, many respectable figures in entertainment such as leading actors, entertainers, priests and prostitutes began to be dubbed with this title.<ref name=":0" />
For example, in [[:en:Kabuki|Kabuki]] theater, the leading female role actor (''tateoyama'') is referred to as ''tayū'', and in ''[[:en:Sarugaku|Sarugaku]]'' theater, the leader of a troupe is called ''tayū''. In [[Shinto]], a priest who practices rituals through [[:en:Performing arts|Performing arts]] is called ''tayū''. In [[:en:Jōruri (music)|''jōruri'']] narrative music, the singer is called ''tayū''. Among [[Yūkaku|government permitted prostitutes]], the highest Rank, ''matsu'', was also called ''tayū''.<ref name=":1">{{Cite book|last=Matsumura|first=Akira|title=Daijisen|publisher=Shōgakkan|others="Daijisen" Henshūbu|year=1995|isbn=4-09-501211-0|at=大夫|language=Japanese|oclc=34170147}}</ref>
In the modern times, similar usage of ''tayū'' exists in Japanese [[street performance]], ''kadozuke'' (performance at the gate of a house), and ''[[:en:Manzai|Manzai]]''.<ref name=":1" />
== See also ==
* ''[[:en:Kugyō|Kugyō]]'' * ''[[Omi (title)|Omi]]'' * [[Scholar-official]] (China)

As a result, many respectable figures in entertainment such as leading actors, entertainers, priests and prostitutes began to be dubbed with this title.<ref name=":0" />
For example, in [[:en:Kabuki|Kabuki]] theater, the leading female role actor (''tateoyama'') is referred to as ''tayū'', and in ''[[:en:Sarugaku|Sarugaku]]'' theater, the leader of a troupe is called ''tayū''. Prior to the [[Taika Reform]] in 645, a government official below [[Omi (title)|''Ōomi'']] and [[Muraji|''Ōmuraji'']] of the same name was called ''Maetsugimi'',<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|title=Nihon kokugo daijiten|publisher=Shōgakkan|others=Shōgakkan.
