According to the ''kushiki-ryō'' of the ''[[ritsuryō]]'' law, a ''Taifu'' holds a court Rank of Third Rank or higher in the [[Daijō-kan|Great Council of State]], a court Rank of Fourth Rank in a government institution above a bureau, and a court Rank of Fifth Rank or higher as a ''shi'' (official in a government department beneath a bureau) or in a ''chūgoku'' (a [[:en:Provinces of Japan|province]] of the second highest Rank) or lower.<ref name=":3" /><ref>{{Cite book|last=Kamochi|first=Masazumi|title=萬葉集古義 第 1 巻|publisher=Seibunkan (精文館)|year=1932|pages=61}}</ref>
Although ''Taifu'' is the collective honorary title denoting a court Rank between First Rank and Fifth Rank, holders of high-Ranking court Ranks above Third Rank were referred to as ''[[:en:Kugyō|Kugyō]]'', and thus ''Taifu'' became commonly used for holders of Fourth and Fifth Rank.<ref name=":0" /><ref>{{Cite book|last=Kishimoto|first=Yuzuru|title=万葉集攷証 第3巻(上)|publisher=Kokonshoin (古今書院)|year=1924|at=大夫}}</ref> As ''Taifu'' is an honorary title, the title was in heavy use for the holders of Fifth Rank, which ultimately led to ''Taifu'' colloquially becoming synonymous with Fifth Rank.<ref name=":0" />
Despite Fifth Rank being the lowest Rank for [[:en:Peerage|Peerage]], for regional [[:en:Samurai|Samurai]] with no illustrious lineage, conferring a peerage and being awarded the title of ''Taifu'' was glorious.<ref name=":0" />
In the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], the top-Ranking samurai officials and advisors, ''[[:en:Karō|Karō]]'', were called ''taifu''.<ref name=":1" /> A similar title seen in ancient [[:en:Zhou dynasty|Zhou China]] as an official Rank called 大夫 (''dàfū''), which Ranked below 卿 (''qīng'') and above 士 (''shì''), was taken from the Zhou Chinese system, and of which the Japanized form ''taifu'' was used for the ''[[:en:Hatamoto|Hatamoto]]''.<ref name=":0" />
At the [[Ise Grand Shrine]], the deputy senior priest, ''gonnegi'', holding the court Rank of Fifth Rank is called ''Taifu''.<ref name=":1" />
== Pronunciation == Although the [[:en:Kana|Kana]] form of the title is "''taifu''", in modern Japanese the pronunciation would be "''tayū''".<ref name=":0" />
The government office of the same name (but pronounced ''daibu'') referred to the director of a government agency (''shiki'') or a government office (''bō''), such as High Steward of the [[:en:Ukyō-ku, Kyoto|Right Capital District]] (''Ukyō no Daibu'') and Director of the Crown Prince's Quarters (''Tōgū no Daibu'').<ref name=":3" /><ref name=":1" /> This pronunciation was to differentiate from the Vice-Minister of the eight ministries with the same pronunciation but different ''[[:en:Kanji|Kanji]]'' writing form (大輔).<ref name=":0" />
== Other usages == As Fifth Rank was seen as the entrance to peerage for the [[:en:Commoner|Commoner]]s and low-Ranking samurai alike, it became a popular word for a distinguished person. As a result, many respectable figures in entertainment such as leading actors, entertainers, priests and prostitutes began to be dubbed with this title.<ref name=":0" />
For example, in [[:en:Kabuki|Kabuki]] theater, the leading female role actor (''tateoyama'') is referred to as ''tayū'', and in ''[[:en:Sarugaku|Sarugaku]]'' theater, the leader of a troupe is called ''tayū''. In [[Shinto]], a priest who practices rituals through [[:en:Performing arts|Performing arts]] is called ''tayū''. In [[:en:Jōruri (music)|''jōruri'']] narrative music, the singer is called ''tayū''. Among [[Yūkaku|government permitted prostitutes]], the highest Rank, ''matsu'', was also called ''tayū''.<ref name=":1">{{Cite book|last=Matsumura|first=Akira|title=Daijisen|publisher=Shōgakkan|others="Daijisen" Henshūbu|year=1995|isbn=4-09-501211-0|at=大夫|language=Japanese|oclc=34170147}}</ref>
In the modern times, similar usage of ''tayū'' exists in Japanese [[:en:street performance|street performance]], ''kadozuke'' (performance at the gate of a house), and ''[[:en:Manzai|Manzai]]''.<ref name=":1" />
== See also ==
* ''[[:en:Kugyō|Kugyō]]'' * ''[[Omi (title)|Omi]]'' * [[:en:Scholar-official|Scholar-official]] (China)

}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[de:Tayū]][[en:Taifu]][[ja:大夫]][[ko:대부 (칭호)]][[zh:大夫 (官名)]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q14126428]] Wikidata name: Taifu [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:41, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Japanese noble title}} {{Disputed|date=December 2023}} ''''Taifu''''' (大夫) was a [[:en:Japanese nobility|noble]] title in Japan, denoting a [[List of Japanese court Ranks, positions and hereditary titles|court Rank]] between [[Senior First Rank|First Rank]] and [[:en:Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade|Fifth Rank]] under the ''[[Ritsuryō]]'' system. It was also commonly used to refer to a holder of Fifth Rank, but also for holders of Fourth and Fifth Rank, to differentiate from holders of First, Second and Third Rank, collectively known as ''[[:en:Kugyō|Kugyō]]''.
== History == In the ancient [[:en:Yamato period|Yamato period]] Japan, the title was used to refer to a close attendant of the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor]] or [[Okimi]]. Prior to the [[Taika Reform]] in 645, a government official below [[Omi (title)|''Ōomi'']] and [[Muraji|''Ōmuraji'']] of the same name was called ''Maetsugimi'',<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|title=Nihon kokugo daijiten|publisher=Shōgakkan|others=Shōgakkan. Kokugo Jiten Henshūbu, 小学館. 国語辞典編集部.|year=2006|isbn=4-09-521021-4|at=大夫|oclc=70216445}}</ref><ref name=":3">{{Cite book|title=Nihon dai hyakka zensho|publisher=Shōgakkan|year=1989|isbn=4-09-526001-7|at=大夫|oclc=14970117}}</ref> whose duty was to submit matters to the Emperor.<ref name=":3" />
According to the ''kushiki-ryō'' of the ''[[ritsuryō]]'' law, a ''Taifu'' holds a court Rank of Third Rank or higher in the [[Daijō-kan|Great Council of State]], a court Rank of Fourth Rank in a government institution above a bureau, and a court Rank of Fifth Rank or higher as a ''shi'' (official in a government department beneath a bureau) or in a ''chūgoku'' (a [[:en:Provinces of Japan|province]] of the second highest Rank) or lower.<ref name=":3" /><ref>{{Cite book|last=Kamochi|first=Masazumi|title=萬葉集古義 第 1 巻|publisher=Seibunkan (精文館)|year=1932|pages=61}}</ref>
Although ''Taifu'' is the collective honorary title denoting a court Rank between First Rank and Fifth Rank, holders of high-Ranking court Ranks above Third Rank were referred to as ''[[:en:Kugyō|Kugyō]]'', and thus ''Taifu'' became commonly used for holders of Fourth and Fifth Rank.<ref name=":0" /><ref>{{Cite book|last=Kishimoto|first=Yuzuru|title=万葉集攷証 第3巻(上)|publisher=Kokonshoin (古今書院)|year=1924|at=大夫}}</ref> As ''Taifu'' is an honorary title, the title was in heavy use for the holders of Fifth Rank, which ultimately led to ''Taifu'' colloquially becoming synonymous with Fifth Rank.<ref name=":0" />
Despite Fifth Rank being the lowest Rank for [[:en:Peerage|Peerage]], for regional [[:en:Samurai|Samurai]] with no illustrious lineage, conferring a peerage and being awarded the title of ''Taifu'' was glorious.<ref name=":0" />
In the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], the top-Ranking samurai officials and advisors, ''[[:en:Karō|Karō]]'', were called ''taifu''.<ref name=":1" /> A similar title seen in ancient [[:en:Zhou dynasty|Zhou China]] as an official Rank called 大夫 (''dàfū''), which Ranked below 卿 (''qīng'') and above 士 (''shì''), was taken from the Zhou Chinese system, and of which the Japanized form ''taifu'' was used for the ''[[:en:Hatamoto|Hatamoto]]''.<ref name=":0" />
At the [[Ise Grand Shrine]], the deputy senior priest, ''gonnegi'', holding the court Rank of Fifth Rank is called ''Taifu''.<ref name=":1" />
== Pronunciation == Although the [[:en:Kana|Kana]] form of the title is "''taifu''", in modern Japanese the pronunciation would be "''tayū''".<ref name=":0" />
The government office of the same name (but pronounced ''daibu'') referred to the director of a government agency (''shiki'') or a government office (''bō''), such as High Steward of the [[:en:Ukyō-ku, Kyoto|Right Capital District]] (''Ukyō no Daibu'') and Director of the Crown Prince's Quarters (''Tōgū no Daibu'').<ref name=":3" /><ref name=":1" /> This pronunciation was to differentiate from the Vice-Minister of the eight ministries with the same pronunciation but different ''[[:en:Kanji|Kanji]]'' writing form (大輔).<ref name=":0" />
== Other usages == As Fifth Rank was seen as the entrance to peerage for the [[:en:Commoner|Commoner]]s and low-Ranking samurai alike, it became a popular word for a distinguished person. As a result, many respectable figures in entertainment such as leading actors, entertainers, priests and prostitutes began to be dubbed with this title.<ref name=":0" />
For example, in [[:en:Kabuki|Kabuki]] theater, the leading female role actor (''tateoyama'') is referred to as ''tayū'', and in ''[[:en:Sarugaku|Sarugaku]]'' theater, the leader of a troupe is called ''tayū''. In [[Shinto]], a priest who practices rituals through [[:en:Performing arts|Performing arts]] is called ''tayū''. In [[:en:Jōruri (music)|''jōruri'']] narrative music, the singer is called ''tayū''. Among [[Yūkaku|government permitted prostitutes]], the highest Rank, ''matsu'', was also called ''tayū''.<ref name=":1">{{Cite book|last=Matsumura|first=Akira|title=Daijisen|publisher=Shōgakkan|others="Daijisen" Henshūbu|year=1995|isbn=4-09-501211-0|at=大夫|language=Japanese|oclc=34170147}}</ref>
In the modern times, similar usage of ''tayū'' exists in Japanese [[:en:street performance|street performance]], ''kadozuke'' (performance at the gate of a house), and ''[[:en:Manzai|Manzai]]''.<ref name=":1" />
== See also ==
* ''[[:en:Kugyō|Kugyō]]'' * ''[[Omi (title)|Omi]]'' * [[:en:Scholar-official|Scholar-official]] (China)

}}

In [[Shinto]], a priest who practices rituals through [[:en:Performing arts|Performing arts]] is called ''tayū''. In [[:en:Jōruri (music)|''jōruri'']] narrative music, the singer is called ''tayū''. Among [[Yūkaku|government permitted prostitutes]], the highest Rank, ''matsu'', was also called ''tayū''.<ref name=":1">{{Cite book|last=Matsumura|first=Akira|title=Daijisen|publisher=Shōgakkan|others="Daijisen" Henshūbu|year=1995|isbn=4-09-501211-0|at=大夫|language=Japanese|oclc=34170147}}</ref>
In the modern times, similar usage of ''tayū'' exists in Japanese [[:en:street performance|street performance]], ''kadozuke'' (performance at the gate of a house), and ''[[:en:Manzai|Manzai]]''.<ref name=":1" />
== See also ==
* ''[[:en:Kugyō|Kugyō]]'' * ''[[Omi (title)|Omi]]'' * [[:en:Scholar-official|Scholar-official]] (China)

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[de:Tayū]][[en:Taifu]][[ja:大夫]][[ko:대부 (칭호)]][[zh:大夫 (官名)]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q14126428]] Wikidata name: Taifu [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:41, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Japanese noble title}} {{Disputed|date=December 2023}} ''''Taifu''''' (大夫) was a [[:en:Japanese nobility|noble]] title in Japan, denoting a [[List of Japanese court Ranks, positions and hereditary titles|court Rank]] between [[Senior First Rank|First Rank]] and [[:en:Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade|Fifth Rank]] under the ''[[Ritsuryō]]'' system. It was also commonly used to refer to a holder of Fifth Rank, but also for holders of Fourth and Fifth Rank, to differentiate from holders of First, Second and Third Rank, collectively known as ''[[:en:Kugyō|Kugyō]]''.
== History == In the ancient [[:en:Yamato period|Yamato period]] Japan, the title was used to refer to a close attendant of the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor]] or [[Okimi]]. Prior to the [[Taika Reform]] in 645, a government official below [[Omi (title)|''Ōomi'']] and [[Muraji|''Ōmuraji'']] of the same name was called ''Maetsugimi'',<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|title=Nihon kokugo daijiten|publisher=Shōgakkan|others=Shōgakkan. Kokugo Jiten Henshūbu, 小学館. 国語辞典編集部.|year=2006|isbn=4-09-521021-4|at=大夫|oclc=70216445}}</ref><ref name=":3">{{Cite book|title=Nihon dai hyakka zensho|publisher=Shōgakkan|year=1989|isbn=4-09-526001-7|at=大夫|oclc=14970117}}</ref> whose duty was to submit matters to the Emperor.<ref name=":3" />
According to the ''kushiki-ryō'' of the ''[[ritsuryō]]'' law, a ''Taifu'' holds a court Rank of Third Rank or higher in the [[Daijō-kan|Great Council of State]], a court Rank of Fourth Rank in a government institution above a bureau, and a court Rank of Fifth Rank or higher as a ''shi'' (official in a government department beneath a bureau) or in a ''chūgoku'' (a [[:en:Provinces of Japan|province]] of the second highest Rank) or lower.<ref name=":3" /><ref>{{Cite book|last=Kamochi|first=Masazumi|title=萬葉集古義 第 1 巻|publisher=Seibunkan (精文館)|year=1932|pages=61}}</ref>
Although ''Taifu'' is the collective honorary title denoting a court Rank between First Rank and Fifth Rank, holders of high-Ranking court Ranks above Third Rank were referred to as ''[[:en:Kugyō|Kugyō]]'', and thus ''Taifu'' became commonly used for holders of Fourth and Fifth Rank.<ref name=":0" /><ref>{{Cite book|last=Kishimoto|first=Yuzuru|title=万葉集攷証 第3巻(上)|publisher=Kokonshoin (古今書院)|year=1924|at=大夫}}</ref> As ''Taifu'' is an honorary title, the title was in heavy use for the holders of Fifth Rank, which ultimately led to ''Taifu'' colloquially becoming synonymous with Fifth Rank.<ref name=":0" />
Despite Fifth Rank being the lowest Rank for [[:en:Peerage|Peerage]], for regional [[:en:Samurai|Samurai]] with no illustrious lineage, conferring a peerage and being awarded the title of ''Taifu'' was glorious.<ref name=":0" />
In the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], the top-Ranking samurai officials and advisors, ''[[:en:Karō|Karō]]'', were called ''taifu''.<ref name=":1" /> A similar title seen in ancient [[:en:Zhou dynasty|Zhou China]] as an official Rank called 大夫 (''dàfū''), which Ranked below 卿 (''qīng'') and above 士 (''shì''), was taken from the Zhou Chinese system, and of which the Japanized form ''taifu'' was used for the ''[[:en:Hatamoto|Hatamoto]]''.<ref name=":0" />
At the [[Ise Grand Shrine]], the deputy senior priest, ''gonnegi'', holding the court Rank of Fifth Rank is called ''Taifu''.<ref name=":1" />
== Pronunciation == Although the [[:en:Kana|Kana]] form of the title is "''taifu''", in modern Japanese the pronunciation would be "''tayū''".<ref name=":0" />
The government office of the same name (but pronounced ''daibu'') referred to the director of a government agency (''shiki'') or a government office (''bō''), such as High Steward of the [[:en:Ukyō-ku, Kyoto|Right Capital District]] (''Ukyō no Daibu'') and Director of the Crown Prince's Quarters (''Tōgū no Daibu'').<ref name=":3" /><ref name=":1" /> This pronunciation was to differentiate from the Vice-Minister of the eight ministries with the same pronunciation but different ''[[:en:Kanji|Kanji]]'' writing form (大輔).<ref name=":0" />
== Other usages == As Fifth Rank was seen as the entrance to peerage for the [[:en:Commoner|Commoner]]s and low-Ranking samurai alike, it became a popular word for a distinguished person. As a result, many respectable figures in entertainment such as leading actors, entertainers, priests and prostitutes began to be dubbed with this title.<ref name=":0" />
For example, in [[:en:Kabuki|Kabuki]] theater, the leading female role actor (''tateoyama'') is referred to as ''tayū'', and in ''[[:en:Sarugaku|Sarugaku]]'' theater, the leader of a troupe is called ''tayū''. In [[Shinto]], a priest who practices rituals through [[:en:Performing arts|Performing arts]] is called ''tayū''. In [[:en:Jōruri (music)|''jōruri'']] narrative music, the singer is called ''tayū''. Among [[Yūkaku|government permitted prostitutes]], the highest Rank, ''matsu'', was also called ''tayū''.<ref name=":1">{{Cite book|last=Matsumura|first=Akira|title=Daijisen|publisher=Shōgakkan|others="Daijisen" Henshūbu|year=1995|isbn=4-09-501211-0|at=大夫|language=Japanese|oclc=34170147}}</ref>
In the modern times, similar usage of ''tayū'' exists in Japanese [[:en:street performance|street performance]], ''kadozuke'' (performance at the gate of a house), and ''[[:en:Manzai|Manzai]]''.<ref name=":1" />
== See also ==
* ''[[:en:Kugyō|Kugyō]]'' * ''[[Omi (title)|Omi]]'' * [[:en:Scholar-official|Scholar-official]] (China)

Although ''Taifu'' is the collective honorary title denoting a court Rank between First Rank and Fifth Rank, holders of high-Ranking court Ranks above Third Rank were referred to as ''[[:en:Kugyō|Kugyō]]'', and thus ''Taifu'' became commonly used for holders of Fourth and Fifth Rank.<ref name=":0" /><ref>{{Cite book|last=Kishimoto|first=Yuzuru|title=万葉集攷証 第3巻(上)|publisher=Kokonshoin (古今書院)|year=1924|at=大夫}}</ref> As ''Taifu'' is an honorary title, the title was in heavy use for the holders of Fifth Rank, which ultimately led to ''Taifu'' colloquially becoming synonymous with Fifth Rank.<ref name=":0" />
Despite Fifth Rank being the lowest Rank for [[:en:Peerage|Peerage]], for regional [[:en:Samurai|Samurai]] with no illustrious lineage, conferring a peerage and being awarded the title of ''Taifu'' was glorious.<ref name=":0" />
In the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], the top-Ranking samurai officials and advisors, ''[[:en:Karō|Karō]]'', were called ''taifu''.<ref name=":1" /> A similar title seen in ancient [[:en:Zhou dynasty|Zhou China]] as an official Rank called 大夫 (''dàfū''), which Ranked below 卿 (''qīng'') and above 士 (''shì''), was taken from the Zhou Chinese system, and of which the Japanized form ''taifu'' was used for the ''[[:en:Hatamoto|Hatamoto]]''.<ref name=":0" />
At the [[Ise Grand Shrine]], the deputy senior priest, ''gonnegi'', holding the court Rank of Fifth Rank is called ''Taifu''.<ref name=":1" />
== Pronunciation == Although the [[:en:Kana|Kana]] form of the title is "''taifu''", in modern Japanese the pronunciation would be "''tayū''".<ref name=":0" />
The government office of the same name (but pronounced ''daibu'') referred to the director of a government agency (''shiki'') or a government office (''bō''), such as High Steward of the [[:en:Ukyō-ku, Kyoto|Right Capital District]] (''Ukyō no Daibu'') and Director of the Crown Prince's Quarters (''Tōgū no Daibu'').<ref name=":3" /><ref name=":1" /> This pronunciation was to differentiate from the Vice-Minister of the eight ministries with the same pronunciation but different ''[[:en:Kanji|Kanji]]'' writing form (大輔).<ref name=":0" />
== Other usages == As Fifth Rank was seen as the entrance to peerage for the [[:en:Commoner|Commoner]]s and low-Ranking samurai alike, it became a popular word for a distinguished person. As a result, many respectable figures in entertainment such as leading actors, entertainers, priests and prostitutes began to be dubbed with this title.<ref name=":0" />
For example, in [[:en:Kabuki|Kabuki]] theater, the leading female role actor (''tateoyama'') is referred to as ''tayū'', and in ''[[:en:Sarugaku|Sarugaku]]'' theater, the leader of a troupe is called ''tayū''. In [[Shinto]], a priest who practices rituals through [[:en:Performing arts|Performing arts]] is called ''tayū''. In [[:en:Jōruri (music)|''jōruri'']] narrative music, the singer is called ''tayū''. Among [[Yūkaku|government permitted prostitutes]], the highest Rank, ''matsu'', was also called ''tayū''.<ref name=":1">{{Cite book|last=Matsumura|first=Akira|title=Daijisen|publisher=Shōgakkan|others="Daijisen" Henshūbu|year=1995|isbn=4-09-501211-0|at=大夫|language=Japanese|oclc=34170147}}</ref>
In the modern times, similar usage of ''tayū'' exists in Japanese [[:en:street performance|street performance]], ''kadozuke'' (performance at the gate of a house), and ''[[:en:Manzai|Manzai]]''.<ref name=":1" />
== See also ==
* ''[[:en:Kugyō|Kugyō]]'' * ''[[Omi (title)|Omi]]'' * [[:en:Scholar-official|Scholar-official]] (China)

<!--enwiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[de:Tayū]] [[ja:大夫]] [[ko:대부 (칭호)]] [[zh:大夫 (官名)]] [[Category:Special:LonelyPages 22 May 2025]] [[Category:Special:LonelyPages 26 May 2025]] {{wikidata link|Q14126428}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[de:Tayū]][[en:Taifu]][[ja:大夫]][[ko:대부 (칭호)]][[zh:大夫 (官名)]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q14126428]] Wikidata name: Taifu [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:41, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Japanese noble title}} {{Disputed|date=December 2023}} ''''Taifu''''' (大夫) was a [[:en:Japanese nobility|noble]] title in Japan, denoting a [[List of Japanese court Ranks, positions and hereditary titles|court Rank]] between [[Senior First Rank|First Rank]] and [[:en:Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade|Fifth Rank]] under the ''[[Ritsuryō]]'' system. It was also commonly used to refer to a holder of Fifth Rank, but also for holders of Fourth and Fifth Rank, to differentiate from holders of First, Second and Third Rank, collectively known as ''[[:en:Kugyō|Kugyō]]''.
== History == In the ancient [[:en:Yamato period|Yamato period]] Japan, the title was used to refer to a close attendant of the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor]] or [[Okimi]]. Prior to the [[Taika Reform]] in 645, a government official below [[Omi (title)|''Ōomi'']] and [[Muraji|''Ōmuraji'']] of the same name was called ''Maetsugimi'',<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|title=Nihon kokugo daijiten|publisher=Shōgakkan|others=Shōgakkan. Kokugo Jiten Henshūbu, 小学館. 国語辞典編集部.|year=2006|isbn=4-09-521021-4|at=大夫|oclc=70216445}}</ref><ref name=":3">{{Cite book|title=Nihon dai hyakka zensho|publisher=Shōgakkan|year=1989|isbn=4-09-526001-7|at=大夫|oclc=14970117}}</ref> whose duty was to submit matters to the Emperor.<ref name=":3" />
According to the ''kushiki-ryō'' of the ''[[ritsuryō]]'' law, a ''Taifu'' holds a court Rank of Third Rank or higher in the [[Daijō-kan|Great Council of State]], a court Rank of Fourth Rank in a government institution above a bureau, and a court Rank of Fifth Rank or higher as a ''shi'' (official in a government department beneath a bureau) or in a ''chūgoku'' (a [[:en:Provinces of Japan|province]] of the second highest Rank) or lower.<ref name=":3" /><ref>{{Cite book|last=Kamochi|first=Masazumi|title=萬葉集古義 第 1 巻|publisher=Seibunkan (精文館)|year=1932|pages=61}}</ref>
Although ''Taifu'' is the collective honorary title denoting a court Rank between First Rank and Fifth Rank, holders of high-Ranking court Ranks above Third Rank were referred to as ''[[:en:Kugyō|Kugyō]]'', and thus ''Taifu'' became commonly used for holders of Fourth and Fifth Rank.<ref name=":0" /><ref>{{Cite book|last=Kishimoto|first=Yuzuru|title=万葉集攷証 第3巻(上)|publisher=Kokonshoin (古今書院)|year=1924|at=大夫}}</ref> As ''Taifu'' is an honorary title, the title was in heavy use for the holders of Fifth Rank, which ultimately led to ''Taifu'' colloquially becoming synonymous with Fifth Rank.<ref name=":0" />
Despite Fifth Rank being the lowest Rank for [[:en:Peerage|Peerage]], for regional [[:en:Samurai|Samurai]] with no illustrious lineage, conferring a peerage and being awarded the title of ''Taifu'' was glorious.<ref name=":0" />
In the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], the top-Ranking samurai officials and advisors, ''[[:en:Karō|Karō]]'', were called ''taifu''.<ref name=":1" /> A similar title seen in ancient [[:en:Zhou dynasty|Zhou China]] as an official Rank called 大夫 (''dàfū''), which Ranked below 卿 (''qīng'') and above 士 (''shì''), was taken from the Zhou Chinese system, and of which the Japanized form ''taifu'' was used for the ''[[:en:Hatamoto|Hatamoto]]''.<ref name=":0" />
At the [[Ise Grand Shrine]], the deputy senior priest, ''gonnegi'', holding the court Rank of Fifth Rank is called ''Taifu''.<ref name=":1" />
== Pronunciation == Although the [[:en:Kana|Kana]] form of the title is "''taifu''", in modern Japanese the pronunciation would be "''tayū''".<ref name=":0" />
The government office of the same name (but pronounced ''daibu'') referred to the director of a government agency (''shiki'') or a government office (''bō''), such as High Steward of the [[:en:Ukyō-ku, Kyoto|Right Capital District]] (''Ukyō no Daibu'') and Director of the Crown Prince's Quarters (''Tōgū no Daibu'').<ref name=":3" /><ref name=":1" /> This pronunciation was to differentiate from the Vice-Minister of the eight ministries with the same pronunciation but different ''[[:en:Kanji|Kanji]]'' writing form (大輔).<ref name=":0" />
== Other usages == As Fifth Rank was seen as the entrance to peerage for the [[:en:Commoner|Commoner]]s and low-Ranking samurai alike, it became a popular word for a distinguished person. As a result, many respectable figures in entertainment such as leading actors, entertainers, priests and prostitutes began to be dubbed with this title.<ref name=":0" />
For example, in [[:en:Kabuki|Kabuki]] theater, the leading female role actor (''tateoyama'') is referred to as ''tayū'', and in ''[[:en:Sarugaku|Sarugaku]]'' theater, the leader of a troupe is called ''tayū''. In [[Shinto]], a priest who practices rituals through [[:en:Performing arts|Performing arts]] is called ''tayū''. In [[:en:Jōruri (music)|''jōruri'']] narrative music, the singer is called ''tayū''. Among [[Yūkaku|government permitted prostitutes]], the highest Rank, ''matsu'', was also called ''tayū''.<ref name=":1">{{Cite book|last=Matsumura|first=Akira|title=Daijisen|publisher=Shōgakkan|others="Daijisen" Henshūbu|year=1995|isbn=4-09-501211-0|at=大夫|language=Japanese|oclc=34170147}}</ref>
In the modern times, similar usage of ''tayū'' exists in Japanese [[:en:street performance|street performance]], ''kadozuke'' (performance at the gate of a house), and ''[[:en:Manzai|Manzai]]''.<ref name=":1" />

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[de:Tayū]][[en:Taifu]][[ja:大夫]][[ko:대부 (칭호)]][[zh:大夫 (官名)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[de:Tayū]][[en:Taifu]][[ja:大夫]][[ko:대부 (칭호)]][[zh:大夫 (官名)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[de:Tayū]][[en:Taifu]][[ja:大夫]][[ko:대부 (칭호)]][[zh:大夫 (官名)]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q14126428]] Wikidata name: Taifu [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:41, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Japanese noble title}} {{Disputed|date=December 2023}} ''''Taifu''''' (大夫) was a [[:en:Japanese nobility|noble]] title in Japan, denoting a [[List of Japanese court Ranks, positions and hereditary titles|court Rank]] between [[Senior First Rank|First Rank]] and [[:en:Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade|Fifth Rank]] under the ''[[Ritsuryō]]'' system. It was also commonly used to refer to a holder of Fifth Rank, but also for holders of Fourth and Fifth Rank, to differentiate from holders of First, Second and Third Rank, collectively known as ''[[:en:Kugyō|Kugyō]]''.
== History == In the ancient [[:en:Yamato period|Yamato period]] Japan, the title was used to refer to a close attendant of the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor]] or [[Okimi]]. Prior to the [[Taika Reform]] in 645, a government official below [[Omi (title)|''Ōomi'']] and [[Muraji|''Ōmuraji'']] of the same name was called ''Maetsugimi'',<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|title=Nihon kokugo daijiten|publisher=Shōgakkan|others=Shōgakkan. Kokugo Jiten Henshūbu, 小学館. 国語辞典編集部.|year=2006|isbn=4-09-521021-4|at=大夫|oclc=70216445}}</ref><ref name=":3">{{Cite book|title=Nihon dai hyakka zensho|publisher=Shōgakkan|year=1989|isbn=4-09-526001-7|at=大夫|oclc=14970117}}</ref> whose duty was to submit matters to the Emperor.<ref name=":3" />
According to the ''kushiki-ryō'' of the ''[[ritsuryō]]'' law, a ''Taifu'' holds a court Rank of Third Rank or higher in the [[Daijō-kan|Great Council of State]], a court Rank of Fourth Rank in a government institution above a bureau, and a court Rank of Fifth Rank or higher as a ''shi'' (official in a government department beneath a bureau) or in a ''chūgoku'' (a [[:en:Provinces of Japan|province]] of the second highest Rank) or lower.<ref name=":3" /><ref>{{Cite book|last=Kamochi|first=Masazumi|title=萬葉集古義 第 1 巻|publisher=Seibunkan (精文館)|year=1932|pages=61}}</ref>
Although ''Taifu'' is the collective honorary title denoting a court Rank between First Rank and Fifth Rank, holders of high-Ranking court Ranks above Third Rank were referred to as ''[[:en:Kugyō|Kugyō]]'', and thus ''Taifu'' became commonly used for holders of Fourth and Fifth Rank.<ref name=":0" /><ref>{{Cite book|last=Kishimoto|first=Yuzuru|title=万葉集攷証 第3巻(上)|publisher=Kokonshoin (古今書院)|year=1924|at=大夫}}</ref> As ''Taifu'' is an honorary title, the title was in heavy use for the holders of Fifth Rank, which ultimately led to ''Taifu'' colloquially becoming synonymous with Fifth Rank.<ref name=":0" />
Despite Fifth Rank being the lowest Rank for [[:en:Peerage|Peerage]], for regional [[:en:Samurai|Samurai]] with no illustrious lineage, conferring a peerage and being awarded the title of ''Taifu'' was glorious.<ref name=":0" />
In the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], the top-Ranking samurai officials and advisors, ''[[:en:Karō|Karō]]'', were called ''taifu''.<ref name=":1" /> A similar title seen in ancient [[:en:Zhou dynasty|Zhou China]] as an official Rank called 大夫 (''dàfū''), which Ranked below 卿 (''qīng'') and above 士 (''shì''), was taken from the Zhou Chinese system, and of which the Japanized form ''taifu'' was used for the ''[[:en:Hatamoto|Hatamoto]]''.<ref name=":0" />
At the [[Ise Grand Shrine]], the deputy senior priest, ''gonnegi'', holding the court Rank of Fifth Rank is called ''Taifu''.<ref name=":1" />
== Pronunciation == Although the [[:en:Kana|Kana]] form of the title is "''taifu''", in modern Japanese the pronunciation would be "''tayū''".<ref name=":0" />
The government office of the same name (but pronounced ''daibu'') referred to the director of a government agency (''shiki'') or a government office (''bō''), such as High Steward of the [[:en:Ukyō-ku, Kyoto|Right Capital District]] (''Ukyō no Daibu'') and Director of the Crown Prince's Quarters (''Tōgū no Daibu'').<ref name=":3" /><ref name=":1" /> This pronunciation was to differentiate from the Vice-Minister of the eight ministries with the same pronunciation but different ''[[:en:Kanji|Kanji]]'' writing form (大輔).<ref name=":0" />
== Other usages == As Fifth Rank was seen as the entrance to peerage for the [[:en:Commoner|Commoner]]s and low-Ranking samurai alike, it became a popular word for a distinguished person. As a result, many respectable figures in entertainment such as leading actors, entertainers, priests and prostitutes began to be dubbed with this title.<ref name=":0" />
For example, in [[:en:Kabuki|Kabuki]] theater, the leading female role actor (''tateoyama'') is referred to as ''tayū'', and in ''[[:en:Sarugaku|Sarugaku]]'' theater, the leader of a troupe is called ''tayū''. In [[Shinto]], a priest who practices rituals through [[:en:Performing arts|Performing arts]] is called ''tayū''. In [[:en:Jōruri (music)|''jōruri'']] narrative music, the singer is called ''tayū''. Among [[Yūkaku|government permitted prostitutes]], the highest Rank, ''matsu'', was also called ''tayū''.<ref name=":1">{{Cite book|last=Matsumura|first=Akira|title=Daijisen|publisher=Shōgakkan|others="Daijisen" Henshūbu|year=1995|isbn=4-09-501211-0|at=大夫|language=Japanese|oclc=34170147}}</ref>
In the modern times, similar usage of ''tayū'' exists in Japanese [[:en:street performance|street performance]], ''kadozuke'' (performance at the gate of a house), and ''[[:en:Manzai|Manzai]]''.<ref name=":1" />
== See also ==
* ''[[:en:Kugyō|Kugyō]]'' * ''[[Omi (title)|Omi]]'' * [[:en:Scholar-official|Scholar-official]] (China)

}}

Despite Fifth Rank being the lowest Rank for [[:en:Peerage|Peerage]], for regional [[:en:Samurai|Samurai]] with no illustrious lineage, conferring a peerage and being awarded the title of ''Taifu'' was glorious.<ref name=":0" />
In the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], the top-Ranking samurai officials and advisors, ''[[:en:Karō|Karō]]'', were called ''taifu''.<ref name=":1" /> A similar title seen in ancient [[:en:Zhou dynasty|Zhou China]] as an official Rank called 大夫 (''dàfū''), which Ranked below 卿 (''qīng'') and above 士 (''shì''), was taken from the Zhou Chinese system, and of which the Japanized form ''taifu'' was used for the ''[[:en:Hatamoto|Hatamoto]]''.<ref name=":0" />
At the [[Ise Grand Shrine]], the deputy senior priest, ''gonnegi'', holding the court Rank of Fifth Rank is called ''Taifu''.<ref name=":1" />
== Pronunciation == Although the [[:en:Kana|Kana]] form of the title is "''taifu''", in modern Japanese the pronunciation would be "''tayū''".<ref name=":0" />
The government office of the same name (but pronounced ''daibu'') referred to the director of a government agency (''shiki'') or a government office (''bō''), such as High Steward of the [[:en:Ukyō-ku, Kyoto|Right Capital District]] (''Ukyō no Daibu'') and Director of the Crown Prince's Quarters (''Tōgū no Daibu'').<ref name=":3" /><ref name=":1" /> This pronunciation was to differentiate from the Vice-Minister of the eight ministries with the same pronunciation but different ''[[:en:Kanji|Kanji]]'' writing form (大輔).<ref name=":0" />
== Other usages == As Fifth Rank was seen as the entrance to peerage for the [[:en:Commoner|Commoner]]s and low-Ranking samurai alike, it became a popular word for a distinguished person. As a result, many respectable figures in entertainment such as leading actors, entertainers, priests and prostitutes began to be dubbed with this title.<ref name=":0" />
For example, in [[:en:Kabuki|Kabuki]] theater, the leading female role actor (''tateoyama'') is referred to as ''tayū'', and in ''[[:en:Sarugaku|Sarugaku]]'' theater, the leader of a troupe is called ''tayū''. In [[Shinto]], a priest who practices rituals through [[:en:Performing arts|Performing arts]] is called ''tayū''. In [[:en:Jōruri (music)|''jōruri'']] narrative music, the singer is called ''tayū''. Among [[Yūkaku|government permitted prostitutes]], the highest Rank, ''matsu'', was also called ''tayū''.<ref name=":1">{{Cite book|last=Matsumura|first=Akira|title=Daijisen|publisher=Shōgakkan|others="Daijisen" Henshūbu|year=1995|isbn=4-09-501211-0|at=大夫|language=Japanese|oclc=34170147}}</ref>
In the modern times, similar usage of ''tayū'' exists in Japanese [[:en:street performance|street performance]], ''kadozuke'' (performance at the gate of a house), and ''[[:en:Manzai|Manzai]]''.<ref name=":1" />
== See also ==
* ''[[:en:Kugyō|Kugyō]]'' * ''[[Omi (title)|Omi]]'' * [[:en:Scholar-official|Scholar-official]] (China)

{{wikidata link|Q14126428}} [[Category:Japanese historical terms]] [[Category:Japanese nobility]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:Special:LonelyPages 22 May 2025]] [[Category:Special:LonelyPages 26 May 2025]] [[Category:Titles]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[de:Tayū]][[en:Taifu]][[ja:大夫]][[ko:대부 (칭호)]][[zh:大夫 (官名)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[de:Tayū]][[en:Taifu]][[ja:大夫]][[ko:대부 (칭호)]][[zh:大夫 (官名)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[de:Tayū]][[en:Taifu]][[ja:大夫]][[ko:대부 (칭호)]][[zh:大夫 (官名)]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q14126428]] Wikidata name: Taifu [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:41, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Japanese noble title}} {{Disputed|date=December 2023}} ''''Taifu''''' (大夫) was a [[:en:Japanese nobility|noble]] title in Japan, denoting a [[List of Japanese court Ranks, positions and hereditary titles|court Rank]] between [[Senior First Rank|First Rank]] and [[:en:Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade|Fifth Rank]] under the ''[[Ritsuryō]]'' system. It was also commonly used to refer to a holder of Fifth Rank, but also for holders of Fourth and Fifth Rank, to differentiate from holders of First, Second and Third Rank, collectively known as ''[[:en:Kugyō|Kugyō]]''.
== History == In the ancient [[:en:Yamato period|Yamato period]] Japan, the title was used to refer to a close attendant of the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor]] or [[Okimi]]. Prior to the [[Taika Reform]] in 645, a government official below [[Omi (title)|''Ōomi'']] and [[Muraji|''Ōmuraji'']] of the same name was called ''Maetsugimi'',<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|title=Nihon kokugo daijiten|publisher=Shōgakkan|others=Shōgakkan. Kokugo Jiten Henshūbu, 小学館. 国語辞典編集部.|year=2006|isbn=4-09-521021-4|at=大夫|oclc=70216445}}</ref><ref name=":3">{{Cite book|title=Nihon dai hyakka zensho|publisher=Shōgakkan|year=1989|isbn=4-09-526001-7|at=大夫|oclc=14970117}}</ref> whose duty was to submit matters to the Emperor.<ref name=":3" />
According to the ''kushiki-ryō'' of the ''[[ritsuryō]]'' law, a ''Taifu'' holds a court Rank of Third Rank or higher in the [[Daijō-kan|Great Council of State]], a court Rank of Fourth Rank in a government institution above a bureau, and a court Rank of Fifth Rank or higher as a ''shi'' (official in a government department beneath a bureau) or in a ''chūgoku'' (a [[:en:Provinces of Japan|province]] of the second highest Rank) or lower.<ref name=":3" /><ref>{{Cite book|last=Kamochi|first=Masazumi|title=萬葉集古義 第 1 巻|publisher=Seibunkan (精文館)|year=1932|pages=61}}</ref>
Although ''Taifu'' is the collective honorary title denoting a court Rank between First Rank and Fifth Rank, holders of high-Ranking court Ranks above Third Rank were referred to as ''[[:en:Kugyō|Kugyō]]'', and thus ''Taifu'' became commonly used for holders of Fourth and Fifth Rank.<ref name=":0" /><ref>{{Cite book|last=Kishimoto|first=Yuzuru|title=万葉集攷証 第3巻(上)|publisher=Kokonshoin (古今書院)|year=1924|at=大夫}}</ref> As ''Taifu'' is an honorary title, the title was in heavy use for the holders of Fifth Rank, which ultimately led to ''Taifu'' colloquially becoming synonymous with Fifth Rank.<ref name=":0" />
Despite Fifth Rank being the lowest Rank for [[:en:Peerage|Peerage]], for regional [[:en:Samurai|Samurai]] with no illustrious lineage, conferring a peerage and being awarded the title of ''Taifu'' was glorious.<ref name=":0" />
In the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], the top-Ranking samurai officials and advisors, ''[[:en:Karō|Karō]]'', were called ''taifu''.<ref name=":1" /> A similar title seen in ancient [[:en:Zhou dynasty|Zhou China]] as an official Rank called 大夫 (''dàfū''), which Ranked below 卿 (''qīng'') and above 士 (''shì''), was taken from the Zhou Chinese system, and of which the Japanized form ''taifu'' was used for the ''[[:en:Hatamoto|Hatamoto]]''.<ref name=":0" />
At the [[Ise Grand Shrine]], the deputy senior priest, ''gonnegi'', holding the court Rank of Fifth Rank is called ''Taifu''.<ref name=":1" />
== Pronunciation == Although the [[:en:Kana|Kana]] form of the title is "''taifu''", in modern Japanese the pronunciation would be "''tayū''".<ref name=":0" />
The government office of the same name (but pronounced ''daibu'') referred to the director of a government agency (''shiki'') or a government office (''bō''), such as High Steward of the [[:en:Ukyō-ku, Kyoto|Right Capital District]] (''Ukyō no Daibu'') and Director of the Crown Prince's Quarters (''Tōgū no Daibu'').<ref name=":3" /><ref name=":1" /> This pronunciation was to differentiate from the Vice-Minister of the eight ministries with the same pronunciation but different ''[[:en:Kanji|Kanji]]'' writing form (大輔).<ref name=":0" />
== Other usages == As Fifth Rank was seen as the entrance to peerage for the [[:en:Commoner|Commoner]]s and low-Ranking samurai alike, it became a popular word for a distinguished person. As a result, many respectable figures in entertainment such as leading actors, entertainers, priests and prostitutes began to be dubbed with this title.<ref name=":0" />
For example, in [[:en:Kabuki|Kabuki]] theater, the leading female role actor (''tateoyama'') is referred to as ''tayū'', and in ''[[:en:Sarugaku|Sarugaku]]'' theater, the leader of a troupe is called ''tayū''. In [[Shinto]], a priest who practices rituals through [[:en:Performing arts|Performing arts]] is called ''tayū''. In [[:en:Jōruri (music)|''jōruri'']] narrative music, the singer is called ''tayū''. Among [[Yūkaku|government permitted prostitutes]], the highest Rank, ''matsu'', was also called ''tayū''.<ref name=":1">{{Cite book|last=Matsumura|first=Akira|title=Daijisen|publisher=Shōgakkan|others="Daijisen" Henshūbu|year=1995|isbn=4-09-501211-0|at=大夫|language=Japanese|oclc=34170147}}</ref>
In the modern times, similar usage of ''tayū'' exists in Japanese [[:en:street performance|street performance]], ''kadozuke'' (performance at the gate of a house), and ''[[:en:Manzai|Manzai]]''.<ref name=":1" />

In the modern times, similar usage of ''tayū'' exists in Japanese {{ill|street performance|en|street performance|WD=Q823571|lt=street performance}}, ''kadozuke'' (performance at the gate of a house), and ''{{ill|Manzai|en|Manzai|WD=Q44784|lt=Manzai}}''.<ref name=":1" />

* ''{{ill|Kugyō|en|Kugyō|WD=Q3138036|lt=Kugyō}}'' * ''[[Omi (title)|Omi]]'' * {{ill|Scholar-official|en|Scholar-official|WD=Q708299|lt=Scholar-official}} (China)

[[en:Taifu]] <!--enwiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[de:Tayū]] [[ja:大夫]] [[ko:대부 (칭호)]] [[zh:大夫 (官名)]] {{wikidata link|Q14126428}} [[Category:Japanese historical terms]] [[Category:Japanese nobility]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:Special:LonelyPages 22 May 2025]] [[Category:Special:LonelyPages 26 May 2025]] [[Category:Titles]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

revision.comment: Text replacement - "[[Category:Pages without translation templates]]" to "[[Category:qq]]"
revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[de:Tayū]][[en:Taifu]][[ja:大夫]][[ko:대부 (칭호)]][[zh:大夫 (官名)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[de:Tayū]][[en:Taifu]][[ja:大夫]][[ko:대부 (칭호)]][[zh:大夫 (官名)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[de:Tayū]][[en:Taifu]][[ja:大夫]][[ko:대부 (칭호)]][[zh:大夫 (官名)]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q14126428]] Wikidata name: Taifu [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:41, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Japanese noble title}} {{Disputed|date=December 2023}} ''''Taifu''''' (大夫) was a [[:en:Japanese nobility|noble]] title in Japan, denoting a [[List of Japanese court Ranks, positions and hereditary titles|court Rank]] between [[Senior First Rank|First Rank]] and [[:en:Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade|Fifth Rank]] under the ''[[Ritsuryō]]'' system. It was also commonly used to refer to a holder of Fifth Rank, but also for holders of Fourth and Fifth Rank, to differentiate from holders of First, Second and Third Rank, collectively known as ''{{ill|Kugyō|en|Kugyō|WD=Q3138036|lt=Kugyō}}''.
== History == In the ancient {{ill|Yamato period|en|Yamato period|WD=Q633881|lt=Yamato period}} Japan, the title was used to refer to a close attendant of the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|Emperor of Japan|WD=Q208233|lt=Emperor}} or [[Okimi]]. Prior to the [[Taika Reform]] in 645, a government official below [[Omi (title)|''Ōomi'']] and [[Muraji|''Ōmuraji'']] of the same name was called ''Maetsugimi'',<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|title=Nihon kokugo daijiten|publisher=Shōgakkan|others=Shōgakkan. Kokugo Jiten Henshūbu, 小学館. 国語辞典編集部.|year=2006|isbn=4-09-521021-4|at=大夫|oclc=70216445}}</ref><ref name=":3">{{Cite book|title=Nihon dai hyakka zensho|publisher=Shōgakkan|year=1989|isbn=4-09-526001-7|at=大夫|oclc=14970117}}</ref> whose duty was to submit matters to the Emperor.<ref name=":3" />
According to the ''kushiki-ryō'' of the ''[[ritsuryō]]'' law, a ''Taifu'' holds a court Rank of Third Rank or higher in the [[Daijō-kan|Great Council of State]], a court Rank of Fourth Rank in a government institution above a bureau, and a court Rank of Fifth Rank or higher as a ''shi'' (official in a government department beneath a bureau) or in a ''chūgoku'' (a {{ill|Provinces of Japan|en|Provinces of Japan|WD=Q860290|lt=province}} of the second highest Rank) or lower.<ref name=":3" /><ref>{{Cite book|last=Kamochi|first=Masazumi|title=萬葉集古義 第 1 巻|publisher=Seibunkan (精文館)|year=1932|pages=61}}</ref>
Although ''Taifu'' is the collective honorary title denoting a court Rank between First Rank and Fifth Rank, holders of high-Ranking court Ranks above Third Rank were referred to as ''{{ill|Kugyō|en|Kugyō|WD=Q3138036|lt=Kugyō}}'', and thus ''Taifu'' became commonly used for holders of Fourth and Fifth Rank.<ref name=":0" /><ref>{{Cite book|last=Kishimoto|first=Yuzuru|title=万葉集攷証 第3巻(上)|publisher=Kokonshoin (古今書院)|year=1924|at=大夫}}</ref> As ''Taifu'' is an honorary title, the title was in heavy use for the holders of Fifth Rank, which ultimately led to ''Taifu'' colloquially becoming synonymous with Fifth Rank.<ref name=":0" />
Despite Fifth Rank being the lowest Rank for {{ill|Peerage|en|Peerage|WD=Q51280089|lt=Peerage}}, for regional {{ill|Samurai|en|Samurai|WD=Q38142|lt=Samurai}} with no illustrious lineage, conferring a peerage and being awarded the title of ''Taifu'' was glorious.<ref name=":0" />
In the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}, the top-Ranking samurai officials and advisors, ''{{ill|Karō|en|Karō|WD=Q1316445|lt=Karō}}'', were called ''taifu''.<ref name=":1" /> A similar title seen in ancient {{ill|Zhou dynasty|en|Zhou dynasty|WD=Q35216|lt=Zhou China}} as an official Rank called 大夫 (''dàfū''), which Ranked below 卿 (''qīng'') and above 士 (''shì''), was taken from the Zhou Chinese system, and of which the Japanized form ''taifu'' was used for the ''{{ill|Hatamoto|en|Hatamoto|WD=Q572662|lt=Hatamoto}}''.<ref name=":0" />
At the [[Ise Grand Shrine]], the deputy senior priest, ''gonnegi'', holding the court Rank of Fifth Rank is called ''Taifu''.<ref name=":1" />
== Pronunciation == Although the {{ill|Kana|en|Kana|WD=Q187659|lt=Kana}} form of the title is "''taifu''", in modern Japanese the pronunciation would be "''tayū''".<ref name=":0" />
The government office of the same name (but pronounced ''daibu'') referred to the director of a government agency (''shiki'') or a government office (''bō''), such as High Steward of the {{ill|Ukyō-ku, Kyoto|en|Ukyō-ku, Kyoto|WD=Q580186|lt=Right Capital District}} (''Ukyō no Daibu'') and Director of the Crown Prince's Quarters (''Tōgū no Daibu'').<ref name=":3" /><ref name=":1" /> This pronunciation was to differentiate from the Vice-Minister of the eight ministries with the same pronunciation but different ''{{ill|Kanji|en|Kanji|WD=Q82772|lt=Kanji}}'' writing form (大輔).<ref name=":0" />
== Other usages == As Fifth Rank was seen as the entrance to peerage for the {{ill|Commoner|en|Commoner|WD=Q3443060|lt=Commoner}}s and low-Ranking samurai alike, it became a popular word for a distinguished person. As a result, many respectable figures in entertainment such as leading actors, entertainers, priests and prostitutes began to be dubbed with this title.<ref name=":0" />
For example, in {{ill|Kabuki|en|Kabuki|WD=Q199701|lt=Kabuki}} theater, the leading female role actor (''tateoyama'') is referred to as ''tayū'', and in ''{{ill|Sarugaku|en|Sarugaku|WD=Q1204318|lt=Sarugaku}}'' theater, the leader of a troupe is called ''tayū''. In [[Shinto]], a priest who practices rituals through {{ill|Performing arts|en|Performing arts|WD=Q184485|lt=Performing arts}} is called ''tayū''. In {{ill|Jōruri (music)|en|Jōruri (music)|WD=Q1361891|lt=''jōruri''}} narrative music, the singer is called ''tayū''. Among [[Yūkaku|government permitted prostitutes]], the highest Rank, ''matsu'', was also called ''tayū''.<ref name=":1">{{Cite book|last=Matsumura|first=Akira|title=Daijisen|publisher=Shōgakkan|others="Daijisen" Henshūbu|year=1995|isbn=4-09-501211-0|at=大夫|language=Japanese|oclc=34170147}}</ref>
In the modern times, similar usage of ''tayū'' exists in Japanese {{ill|street performance|en|street performance|WD=Q823571|lt=street performance}}, ''kadozuke'' (performance at the gate of a house), and ''{{ill|Manzai|en|Manzai|WD=Q44784|lt=Manzai}}''.<ref name=":1" />
== See also ==
* ''{{ill|Kugyō|en|Kugyō|WD=Q3138036|lt=Kugyō}}'' * ''[[Omi (title)|Omi]]'' * {{ill|Scholar-official|en|Scholar-official|WD=Q708299|lt=Scholar-official}} (China)
== References == {{Reflist}}
[[en:Taifu]] <!--enwiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[de:Tayū]] [[ja:大夫]] [[ko:대부 (칭호)]] [[zh:大夫 (官名)]] {{wikidata link|Q14126428}} [[Category:Japanese historical terms]] [[Category:Japanese nobility]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Special:LonelyPages 22 May 2025]] [[Category:Special:LonelyPages 26 May 2025]] [[Category:Titles]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[de:Tayū]][[en:Taifu]][[ja:大夫]][[ko:대부 (칭호)]][[zh:大夫 (官名)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[de:Tayū]][[en:Taifu]][[ja:大夫]][[ko:대부 (칭호)]][[zh:大夫 (官名)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[de:Tayū]][[en:Taifu]][[ja:大夫]][[ko:대부 (칭호)]][[zh:大夫 (官名)]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q14126428]] Wikidata name: Taifu [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:41, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Japanese noble title}} {{Disputed|date=December 2023}} ''''Taifu''''' (大夫) was a [[:en:Japanese nobility|noble]] title in Japan, denoting a [[List of Japanese court Ranks, positions and hereditary titles|court Rank]] between [[Senior First Rank|First Rank]] and [[:en:Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade|Fifth Rank]] under the ''[[Ritsuryō]]'' system. It was also commonly used to refer to a holder of Fifth Rank, but also for holders of Fourth and Fifth Rank, to differentiate from holders of First, Second and Third Rank, collectively known as ''{{ill|Kugyō|en|Kugyō|WD=Q3138036|lt=Kugyō}}''.
== History == In the ancient {{ill|Yamato period|en|Yamato period|WD=Q633881|lt=Yamato period}} Japan, the title was used to refer to a close attendant of the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|Emperor of Japan|WD=Q208233|lt=Emperor}} or [[Okimi]]. Prior to the [[Taika Reform]] in 645, a government official below [[Omi (title)|''Ōomi'']] and [[Muraji|''Ōmuraji'']] of the same name was called ''Maetsugimi'',<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|title=Nihon kokugo daijiten|publisher=Shōgakkan|others=Shōgakkan. Kokugo Jiten Henshūbu, 小学館. 国語辞典編集部.|year=2006|isbn=4-09-521021-4|at=大夫|oclc=70216445}}</ref><ref name=":3">{{Cite book|title=Nihon dai hyakka zensho|publisher=Shōgakkan|year=1989|isbn=4-09-526001-7|at=大夫|oclc=14970117}}</ref> whose duty was to submit matters to the Emperor.<ref name=":3" />
According to the ''kushiki-ryō'' of the ''[[ritsuryō]]'' law, a ''Taifu'' holds a court Rank of Third Rank or higher in the [[Daijō-kan|Great Council of State]], a court Rank of Fourth Rank in a government institution above a bureau, and a court Rank of Fifth Rank or higher as a ''shi'' (official in a government department beneath a bureau) or in a ''chūgoku'' (a {{ill|Provinces of Japan|en|Provinces of Japan|WD=Q860290|lt=province}} of the second highest Rank) or lower.<ref name=":3" /><ref>{{Cite book|last=Kamochi|first=Masazumi|title=萬葉集古義 第 1 巻|publisher=Seibunkan (精文館)|year=1932|pages=61}}</ref>
Although ''Taifu'' is the collective honorary title denoting a court Rank between First Rank and Fifth Rank, holders of high-Ranking court Ranks above Third Rank were referred to as ''{{ill|Kugyō|en|Kugyō|WD=Q3138036|lt=Kugyō}}'', and thus ''Taifu'' became commonly used for holders of Fourth and Fifth Rank.<ref name=":0" /><ref>{{Cite book|last=Kishimoto|first=Yuzuru|title=万葉集攷証 第3巻(上)|publisher=Kokonshoin (古今書院)|year=1924|at=大夫}}</ref> As ''Taifu'' is an honorary title, the title was in heavy use for the holders of Fifth Rank, which ultimately led to ''Taifu'' colloquially becoming synonymous with Fifth Rank.<ref name=":0" />
Despite Fifth Rank being the lowest Rank for {{ill|Peerage|en|Peerage|WD=Q51280089|lt=Peerage}}, for regional {{ill|Samurai|en|Samurai|WD=Q38142|lt=Samurai}} with no illustrious lineage, conferring a peerage and being awarded the title of ''Taifu'' was glorious.<ref name=":0" />
In the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}, the top-Ranking samurai officials and advisors, ''{{ill|Karō|en|Karō|WD=Q1316445|lt=Karō}}'', were called ''taifu''.<ref name=":1" /> A similar title seen in ancient {{ill|Zhou dynasty|en|Zhou dynasty|WD=Q35216|lt=Zhou China}} as an official Rank called 大夫 (''dàfū''), which Ranked below 卿 (''qīng'') and above 士 (''shì''), was taken from the Zhou Chinese system, and of which the Japanized form ''taifu'' was used for the ''{{ill|Hatamoto|en|Hatamoto|WD=Q572662|lt=Hatamoto}}''.<ref name=":0" />
At the [[Ise Grand Shrine]], the deputy senior priest, ''gonnegi'', holding the court Rank of Fifth Rank is called ''Taifu''.<ref name=":1" />
== Pronunciation == Although the {{ill|Kana|en|Kana|WD=Q187659|lt=Kana}} form of the title is "''taifu''", in modern Japanese the pronunciation would be "''tayū''".<ref name=":0" />
The government office of the same name (but pronounced ''daibu'') referred to the director of a government agency (''shiki'') or a government office (''bō''), such as High Steward of the {{ill|Ukyō-ku, Kyoto|en|Ukyō-ku, Kyoto|WD=Q580186|lt=Right Capital District}} (''Ukyō no Daibu'') and Director of the Crown Prince's Quarters (''Tōgū no Daibu'').<ref name=":3" /><ref name=":1" /> This pronunciation was to differentiate from the Vice-Minister of the eight ministries with the same pronunciation but different ''{{ill|Kanji|en|Kanji|WD=Q82772|lt=Kanji}}'' writing form (大輔).<ref name=":0" />
== Other usages == As Fifth Rank was seen as the entrance to peerage for the {{ill|Commoner|en|Commoner|WD=Q3443060|lt=Commoner}}s and low-Ranking samurai alike, it became a popular word for a distinguished person. As a result, many respectable figures in entertainment such as leading actors, entertainers, priests and prostitutes began to be dubbed with this title.<ref name=":0" />
For example, in {{ill|Kabuki|en|Kabuki|WD=Q199701|lt=Kabuki}} theater, the leading female role actor (''tateoyama'') is referred to as ''tayū'', and in ''{{ill|Sarugaku|en|Sarugaku|WD=Q1204318|lt=Sarugaku}}'' theater, the leader of a troupe is called ''tayū''. In [[Shinto]], a priest who practices rituals through {{ill|Performing arts|en|Performing arts|WD=Q184485|lt=Performing arts}} is called ''tayū''. In {{ill|Jōruri (music)|en|Jōruri (music)|WD=Q1361891|lt=''jōruri''}} narrative music, the singer is called ''tayū''. Among [[Yūkaku|government permitted prostitutes]], the highest Rank, ''matsu'', was also called ''tayū''.<ref name=":1">{{Cite book|last=Matsumura|first=Akira|title=Daijisen|publisher=Shōgakkan|others="Daijisen" Henshūbu|year=1995|isbn=4-09-501211-0|at=大夫|language=Japanese|oclc=34170147}}</ref>
In the modern times, similar usage of ''tayū'' exists in Japanese {{ill|street performance|en|street performance|WD=Q823571|lt=street performance}}, ''kadozuke'' (performance at the gate of a house), and ''{{ill|Manzai|en|Manzai|WD=Q44784|lt=Manzai}}''.<ref name=":1" />
== See also ==
* ''{{ill|Kugyō|en|Kugyō|WD=Q3138036|lt=Kugyō}}'' * ''[[Omi (title)|Omi]]'' * {{ill|Scholar-official|en|Scholar-official|WD=Q708299|lt=Scholar-official}} (China)

{{Short description|Japanese noble title}} {{Disputed|date=December 2023}} ''''Taifu''''' (大夫) was a [[:en:Japanese nobility|noble]] title in Japan, denoting a [[List of Japanese court Ranks, positions and hereditary titles|court Rank]] between [[Senior First Rank|First Rank]] and [[:en:Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade|Fifth Rank]] under the ''[[Ritsuryō]]'' system. It was also commonly used to refer to a holder of Fifth Rank, but also for holders of Fourth and Fifth Rank, to differentiate from holders of First, Second and Third Rank, collectively known as ''{{ill|Kugyō|en|Kugyō|WD=Q3138036|lt=Kugyō}}''.
== History == In the ancient {{ill|Yamato period|en|Yamato period|WD=Q633881|lt=Yamato period}} Japan, the title was used to refer to a close attendant of the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|Emperor of Japan|WD=Q208233|lt=Emperor}} or [[Okimi]]. Prior to the [[Taika Reform]] in 645, a government official below [[Omi (title)|''Ōomi'']] and [[Muraji|''Ōmuraji'']] of the same name was called ''Maetsugimi'',<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|title=Nihon kokugo daijiten|publisher=Shōgakkan|others=Shōgakkan. Kokugo Jiten Henshūbu, 小学館. 国語辞典編集部.|year=2006|isbn=4-09-521021-4|at=大夫|oclc=70216445}}</ref><ref name=":3">{{Cite book|title=Nihon dai hyakka zensho|publisher=Shōgakkan|year=1989|isbn=4-09-526001-7|at=大夫|oclc=14970117}}</ref> whose duty was to submit matters to the Emperor.<ref name=":3" />
According to the ''kushiki-ryō'' of the ''[[ritsuryō]]'' law, a ''Taifu'' holds a court Rank of Third Rank or higher in the [[Daijō-kan|Great Council of State]], a court Rank of Fourth Rank in a government institution above a bureau, and a court Rank of Fifth Rank or higher as a ''shi'' (official in a government department beneath a bureau) or in a ''chūgoku'' (a {{ill|Provinces of Japan|en|Provinces of Japan|WD=Q860290|lt=province}} of the second highest Rank) or lower.<ref name=":3" /><ref>{{Cite book|last=Kamochi|first=Masazumi|title=萬葉集古義 第 1 巻|publisher=Seibunkan (精文館)|year=1932|pages=61}}</ref>
Although ''Taifu'' is the collective honorary title denoting a court Rank between First Rank and Fifth Rank, holders of high-Ranking court Ranks above Third Rank were referred to as ''{{ill|Kugyō|en|Kugyō|WD=Q3138036|lt=Kugyō}}'', and thus ''Taifu'' became commonly used for holders of Fourth and Fifth Rank.<ref name=":0" /><ref>{{Cite book|last=Kishimoto|first=Yuzuru|title=万葉集攷証 第3巻(上)|publisher=Kokonshoin (古今書院)|year=1924|at=大夫}}</ref> As ''Taifu'' is an honorary title, the title was in heavy use for the holders of Fifth Rank, which ultimately led to ''Taifu'' colloquially becoming synonymous with Fifth Rank.<ref name=":0" />
Despite Fifth Rank being the lowest Rank for {{ill|Peerage|en|Peerage|WD=Q51280089|lt=Peerage}}, for regional {{ill|Samurai|en|Samurai|WD=Q38142|lt=Samurai}} with no illustrious lineage, conferring a peerage and being awarded the title of ''Taifu'' was glorious.<ref name=":0" />
In the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}, the top-Ranking samurai officials and advisors, ''{{ill|Karō|en|Karō|WD=Q1316445|lt=Karō}}'', were called ''taifu''.<ref name=":1" /> A similar title seen in ancient {{ill|Zhou dynasty|en|Zhou dynasty|WD=Q35216|lt=Zhou China}} as an official Rank called 大夫 (''dàfū''), which Ranked below 卿 (''qīng'') and above 士 (''shì''), was taken from the Zhou Chinese system, and of which the Japanized form ''taifu'' was used for the ''{{ill|Hatamoto|en|Hatamoto|WD=Q572662|lt=Hatamoto}}''.<ref name=":0" />
At the [[Ise Grand Shrine]], the deputy senior priest, ''gonnegi'', holding the court Rank of Fifth Rank is called ''Taifu''.<ref name=":1" />
== Pronunciation == Although the {{ill|Kana|en|Kana|WD=Q187659|lt=Kana}} form of the title is "''taifu''", in modern Japanese the pronunciation would be "''tayū''".<ref name=":0" />
The government office of the same name (but pronounced ''daibu'') referred to the director of a government agency (''shiki'') or a government office (''bō''), such as High Steward of the {{ill|Ukyō-ku, Kyoto|en|Ukyō-ku, Kyoto|WD=Q580186|lt=Right Capital District}} (''Ukyō no Daibu'') and Director of the Crown Prince's Quarters (''Tōgū no Daibu'').<ref name=":3" /><ref name=":1" /> This pronunciation was to differentiate from the Vice-Minister of the eight ministries with the same pronunciation but different ''{{ill|Kanji|en|Kanji|WD=Q82772|lt=Kanji}}'' writing form (大輔).<ref name=":0" />
== Other usages == As Fifth Rank was seen as the entrance to peerage for the {{ill|Commoner|en|Commoner|WD=Q3443060|lt=Commoner}}s and low-Ranking samurai alike, it became a popular word for a distinguished person. As a result, many respectable figures in entertainment such as leading actors, entertainers, priests and prostitutes began to be dubbed with this title.<ref name=":0" />
For example, in {{ill|Kabuki|en|Kabuki|WD=Q199701|lt=Kabuki}} theater, the leading female role actor (''tateoyama'') is referred to as ''tayū'', and in ''{{ill|Sarugaku|en|Sarugaku|WD=Q1204318|lt=Sarugaku}}'' theater, the leader of a troupe is called ''tayū''. In [[Shinto]], a priest who practices rituals through {{ill|Performing arts|en|Performing arts|WD=Q184485|lt=Performing arts}} is called ''tayū''. In {{ill|Jōruri (music)|en|Jōruri (music)|WD=Q1361891|lt=''jōruri''}} narrative music, the singer is called ''tayū''. Among [[Yūkaku|government permitted prostitutes]], the highest Rank, ''matsu'', was also called ''tayū''.<ref name=":1">{{Cite book|last=Matsumura|first=Akira|title=Daijisen|publisher=Shōgakkan|others="Daijisen" Henshūbu|year=1995|isbn=4-09-501211-0|at=大夫|language=Japanese|oclc=34170147}}</ref>
In the modern times, similar usage of ''tayū'' exists in Japanese {{ill|street performance|en|street performance|WD=Q823571|lt=street performance}}, ''kadozuke'' (performance at the gate of a house), and ''{{ill|Manzai|en|Manzai|WD=Q44784|lt=Manzai}}''.<ref name=":1" />
== See also ==
* ''{{ill|Kugyō|en|Kugyō|WD=Q3138036|lt=Kugyō}}'' * ''[[Omi (title)|Omi]]'' * {{ill|Scholar-official|en|Scholar-official|WD=Q708299|lt=Scholar-official}} (China)
== References == {{Reflist}}

== History == In the ancient {{ill|Yamato period|en|Yamato period|WD=Q633881|lt=Yamato period|1=Yamato period}} Japan, the title was used to refer to a close attendant of the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|Emperor of Japan|WD=Q208233|lt=Emperor|1=Emperor of Japan}} or [[Okimi]]. Prior to the [[Taika Reform]] in 645, a government official below [[Omi (title)|''Ōomi'']] and [[Muraji|''Ōmuraji'']] of the same name was called ''Maetsugimi'',<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|title=Nihon kokugo daijiten|publisher=Shōgakkan|others=Shōgakkan. Kokugo Jiten Henshūbu, 小学館. 国語辞典編集部.|year=2006|isbn=4-09-521021-4|at=大夫|oclc=70216445}}</ref><ref name=":3">{{Cite book|title=Nihon dai hyakka zensho|publisher=Shōgakkan|year=1989|isbn=4-09-526001-7|at=大夫|oclc=14970117}}</ref> whose duty was to submit matters to the Emperor.<ref name=":3" />
According to the ''kushiki-ryō'' of the ''[[ritsuryō]]'' law, a ''Taifu'' holds a court Rank of Third Rank or higher in the [[Daijō-kan|Great Council of State]], a court Rank of Fourth Rank in a government institution above a bureau, and a court Rank of Fifth Rank or higher as a ''shi'' (official in a government department beneath a bureau) or in a ''chūgoku'' (a {{ill|Provinces of Japan|en|Provinces of Japan|WD=Q860290|lt=province|1=Provinces of Japan}} of the second highest Rank) or lower.<ref name=":3" /><ref>{{Cite book|last=Kamochi|first=Masazumi|title=萬葉集古義 第 1 巻|publisher=Seibunkan (精文館)|year=1932|pages=61}}</ref>
Although ''Taifu'' is the collective honorary title denoting a court Rank between First Rank and Fifth Rank, holders of high-Ranking court Ranks above Third Rank were referred to as ''{{ill|Kugyō|en|Kugyō|WD=Q3138036|lt=Kugyō|1=Kugyō}}'', and thus ''Taifu'' became commonly used for holders of Fourth and Fifth Rank.<ref name=":0" /><ref>{{Cite book|last=Kishimoto|first=Yuzuru|title=万葉集攷証 第3巻(上)|publisher=Kokonshoin (古今書院)|year=1924|at=大夫}}</ref> As ''Taifu'' is an honorary title, the title was in heavy use for the holders of Fifth Rank, which ultimately led to ''Taifu'' colloquially becoming synonymous with Fifth Rank.<ref name=":0" />
Despite Fifth Rank being the lowest Rank for {{ill|Peerage|en|Peerage|WD=Q51280089|lt=Peerage|1=Peerage}}, for regional {{ill|Samurai|en|Samurai|WD=Q38142|lt=Samurai|1=Samurai}} with no illustrious lineage, conferring a peerage and being awarded the title of ''Taifu'' was glorious.<ref name=":0" />
In the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}, the top-Ranking samurai officials and advisors, ''{{ill|Karō|en|Karō|WD=Q1316445|lt=Karō|1=Karō}}'', were called ''taifu''.<ref name=":1" /> A similar title seen in ancient {{ill|Zhou dynasty|en|Zhou dynasty|WD=Q35216|lt=Zhou China|1=Zhou dynasty}} as an official Rank called 大夫 (''dàfū''), which Ranked below 卿 (''qīng'') and above 士 (''shì''), was taken from the Zhou Chinese system, and of which the Japanized form ''taifu'' was used for the ''{{ill|Hatamoto|en|Hatamoto|WD=Q572662|lt=Hatamoto|1=Hatamoto}}''.<ref name=":0" />
At the [[Ise Grand Shrine]], the deputy senior priest, ''gonnegi'', holding the court Rank of Fifth Rank is called ''Taifu''.<ref name=":1" />
== Pronunciation == Although the {{ill|Kana|en|Kana|WD=Q187659|lt=Kana|1=Kana}} form of the title is "''taifu''", in modern Japanese the pronunciation would be "''tayū''".<ref name=":0" />
The government office of the same name (but pronounced ''daibu'') referred to the director of a government agency (''shiki'') or a government office (''bō''), such as High Steward of the {{ill|Ukyō-ku, Kyoto|en|Ukyō-ku, Kyoto|WD=Q580186|lt=Right Capital District|1=Ukyō-ku, Kyoto}} (''Ukyō no Daibu'') and Director of the Crown Prince's Quarters (''Tōgū no Daibu'').<ref name=":3" /><ref name=":1" /> This pronunciation was to differentiate from the Vice-Minister of the eight ministries with the same pronunciation but different ''{{ill|Kanji|en|Kanji|WD=Q82772|lt=Kanji|1=Kanji}}'' writing form (大輔).<ref name=":0" />
== Other usages == As Fifth Rank was seen as the entrance to peerage for the {{ill|Commoner|en|Commoner|WD=Q3443060|lt=Commoner|1=Heimin}}s and low-Ranking samurai alike, it became a popular word for a distinguished person. As a result, many respectable figures in entertainment such as leading actors, entertainers, priests and prostitutes began to be dubbed with this title.<ref name=":0" />
For example, in {{ill|Kabuki|en|Kabuki|WD=Q199701|lt=Kabuki|1=Kabuki}} theater, the leading female role actor (''tateoyama'') is referred to as ''tayū'', and in ''{{ill|Sarugaku|en|Sarugaku|WD=Q1204318|lt=Sarugaku|1=Sarugaku}}'' theater, the leader of a troupe is called ''tayū''. In [[Shinto]], a priest who practices rituals through {{ill|Performing arts|en|Performing arts|WD=Q184485|lt=Performing arts|1=Performing arts}} is called ''tayū''. In {{ill|Jōruri (music)|en|Jōruri (music)|WD=Q1361891|lt=''jōruri''|1=Jōruri (music)}} narrative music, the singer is called ''tayū''. Among [[Yūkaku|government permitted prostitutes]], the highest Rank, ''matsu'', was also called ''tayū''.<ref name=":1">{{Cite book|last=Matsumura|first=Akira|title=Daijisen|publisher=Shōgakkan|others="Daijisen" Henshūbu|year=1995|isbn=4-09-501211-0|at=大夫|language=Japanese|oclc=34170147}}</ref>
In the modern times, similar usage of ''tayū'' exists in Japanese {{ill|street performance|en|street performance|WD=Q823571|lt=street performance|1=Street performance}}, ''kadozuke'' (performance at the gate of a house), and ''{{ill|Manzai|en|Manzai|WD=Q44784|lt=Manzai|1=Manzai}}''.<ref name=":1" />
